claim under Section 8 is moot and (2) a company violates Section 8 by appointing a natural 
person to serve on the board of a competing company.  This statement also explains how 
interlocking board member arrangements can undermine fair competition and therefore violate 
the FTC Act and, by extension, California’s UCL.  Finally, this statement discusses the legal 
standard for when group boycotts can run afoul of Section 1.  
The Agencies take no position on any other issue in this case, including the facts alleged 
by Plaintiffs. 
FACTUAL BACKGROUND 
The First Amended Complaint, ECF No. 32, alleges that “Artificial General 
Intelligence,” i.e., “a machine having intelligence for a wide variety of tasks like a human,” 
poses certain risks. Am. Compl. ¶¶ 4, 74-75. To  address these risks, Plaintiff Musk helped 
establish OpenAI in 2015. Am. Compl. ¶ 88. That same year, OpenAI incorporated as a 
“nonprofit corporation” committed to the “research, development and distribution of technology 
related to artificial intelligence.”  Am. Compl. ¶ 89.   
The First Amended Complaint further alleges that (1) defendant Reid Hoffman 
simultaneously served on OpenAI’s and Microsoft’s boards of directors from March 2017 to 
March 2023, and (2) defendant Deannah Templeton simultaneously served as Vice President of 
Partnerships and Operations in the Technology & Research division at Microsoft and as a non-
voting member of OpenAI’s board of directors from November 2023 until July 2024.  Am. 
Compl. ¶¶ 163, 371; see also id. ¶¶ 168, 331(m), 374. 
Based on these allegations, and others, Plaintiffs bring 26 separate claims including 
breach of contract, fraud, false advertising, breach of fiduciary duty, and claims under the 
Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act.  Plaintiffs also allege Defendants violated 
the Sherman and Clayton Acts based in part on conduct affecting the market for “generative AI 
models and platforms.” Am. Compl. ¶ 204. 
On November 29, 2024, Plaintiffs filed a Motion for a Preliminary Injunction seeking to 
enjoin Defendants from, among other things, “directly or indirectly undertaking any action for 
the purpose of or tending to have the effect of, interlocking directorates or benefitting from

ARGUMENT 
The Agencies offer their views on the proper application of Section 8 of the Clayton Act, 
Section 1 of the Sherman Act, and the UCL to the specific issues discussed below.  The 
Agencies take no position on the ultimate disposition of the motion. 
I. Section 8’s Bar on Interlocking Directorates 
A. Statutory Background 
Section 8 of the Clayton Act provides that “[n]o person shall, at the same time, serve as a 
director or officer in any two corporations . . . that are . . . competitors such that the elimination 
of competition by agreement between them would constitute a violation of any of the antitrust 
laws.” 15 U.S.C. § 19. Section 8 makes interloc king directorates, i.e., overlapping directors or 
officers among competing companies, per se unlawful.  TRW, Inc. v. FTC, 647 F.2d 942, 948 
(9th Cir. 1981). At the same time, this broad prohibition is subject to certain limitations and 
exceptions. See, e.g., 15 U.S.C. § 19(a)(2), (b).  For example, the companies must be engaged in 
interstate commerce and have “capital, surplus, and undivided profits aggregating more than” 
$48,559,000. Id.  Section 8 also does not apply if competitive sales: (A) of either corporation are 
less than $4,855,900; (B) of either corporation are less than two percent of the corporation’s total 
sales; or (C) of each corporation are less than four percent of the corporation’s total sales.  Id. at 
§ 8(a)(2).  Finally, Section 19(b) grants a one-year grace period to individuals who were eligible 
to serve at the time of election but a change in circumstances later created an illegal interlock.  
The purpose of Section 8’s prohibition on interlocking directorates is “to nip in the bud 
incipient antitrust violations by removing the opportunity or temptation for such violations.”  
TRW, 647 F.2d at, 946–47.  Put another way, Section 8 “prevent[s] restraints on competition 
before they materialize[] by outlawing a particular practice thought to facilitate such restraints.”  
Id. at 948; see also Reading Int’l, Inc. v. Oaktree Cap. Mgmt. LLC, 317 F. Supp. 2d 301, 330 
(S.D.N.Y. 2003). For example, preventing interl ocking directorates helps “avoid the opportunity

for the coordination of business decisions by competitors and . . . the exchange of commercially 
sensitive information by competitors.” Square D Co. v. Schneider S.A., 760 F. Supp. 362, 366 
(S.D.N.Y. 1991). Such conduct can facilitate unr easonable restraints of trade in violation of 
Section 1 of the Sherman Act (15 U.S.C. § 1) or help a monopolist maintain and entrench its 
power in violation of Section 2 of the Sherman Act (15 U.S.C. § 2).  In this way, Section 8 
supports and reinforces the federal antitrust laws more generally.  See United States v. Sears, 
Roebuck & Co., 111 F. Supp. 614, 616 (S.D.N.Y. 1953) (“Section 8 was but one of a series of 
measures which finally emerged as the Clayton Act, all intended to strengthen the Sherman 
Act.”). 
Courts have consistently interpreted Section 8 in view of these “prophylactic and 
remedial purposes.” Square D Co. v. Schneider S.A., 760 F. Supp. 362, 366 (S.D.N.Y. 1991). 
For example, the Ninth Circuit has held that establishing a Section 8 violation does not require 
“proof that the interlock has an actual anticompetitive effect.” TRW, 647 F.2d at 946–47 (citing 
Protectoseal Co. v. Barancik, 484 F.2d 585, 589 (7th Cir. 1973) (Stevens, J.)). “On the contrary, 
the statute reflects a public interest in preventing directors from serving in positions which 
involve either a potential conflict of interest or a potential frustration of competition.”  
Protectoseal, 484 F.2d at 589. Likewise, courts have refuse d to read the statute so rigidly that a 
company can evade Section 8 liability through tactics that elevate form over substance, e.g., “by 
calling its agents on the competitors’ board something other than either officers or directors.”  
Square, 760 F. Supp. at 366; Reading, 317 F. Supp. 2d at 327–28 (rejecting reading of Section 8 
that would “elevate form over substance” and render the statue “a formalism”). 
B. Section 8 Claims Are Not Mooted by Simply Unwinding an Interlock  
Defendants argue “there is no live ‘case or controversy’” here because “neither Hoffman 
nor Templeton is currently affiliated with OpenAI’s board in any capacity.”  ECF No. 64 at 13. 
But ending an interlocking directorate, e.g., by having a person resign from a corporate board, is 
not sufficient, on its own, to moot a claim under Section 8 of the Clayton Act.  See United States 
v. W. T. Grant Co., 345 U.S. 629, 632 (1953); TRW, 647 F.2d at 953. In resolving this matter, 
the Court should avoid holding otherwise.

“[A]s a general rule, ‘voluntary cessation of allegedly illegal conduct does not deprive the 
tribunal of power to hear and determine the case, i.e., does not make the case moot.’”  Cty. of Los 
Angeles v. Davis, 440 U.S. 625, 631 (1979). Otherwise “[a] defendant [would be] free to return 
to his old ways,” and that, “together with a public interest in having the legality of the [disputed] 
practices settled, militates against a mootness conclusion.”  United States v. W. T. Grant Co., 345 
U.S. 629, 632 (1953). Thus, where a defendant volunt arily ceases illegal conduct, it still bears a 
“heavy burden” to demonstrate it is “absolutely clear that the allegedly wrongful behavior could 
not reasonably be expected to recur.” TRW, 647 F.2d at 953 (quoting United States v. 
Concentrated Phosphate Export Ass’n, 393 U.S. 199, 203 (1968)). Additionally, the relevant 
“concern is with repeated violations of the same law, and not merely with repetition of the same 
offensive conduct.” Id. 
The issue of mootness in the context of Section 8 was addressed squarely by the Ninth 
Circuit in TRW. 647 F.2d at 953. There, the interlocking director (i) resigned his position at the 
competing company, (ii) “decided not to stand for reelection,” (iii) and “provided sworn 
assurances that [he] would avoid offensive directorships in the future.”  Id. The Ninth Circuit 
still held the case was not moot.  Id. As the Ninth Circuit expl ained, mootness depends on the 
risk that a defendant may violate Section 8 in the future, not simply whether it might resume the 
specific interlock at issue.  Id. That risk may remain even af ter an interlock is unwound despite 
“good faith . . . assurances of future compliance.” Id. 
Moreover, “a potential conflict of interest or a potential frustration of competition,” 
Protectoseal, 484 F.2d at 589, may remain even after an interlock is resolved and even if one is 
unlikely to recur. For example, if compan ies use interlocking directors to exchange 
competitively sensitive information, see Square, 760 F. Supp. at 367, they may frustrate 
competition so long as the information remains sensitive and in their possession.  Thus, holding 
that a company moots a potential claim under Section 8 by simply ending an interlock would be 
contrary to the purpose of the statute and would significantly undermine enforcement of the 
Clayton Act. Cf. United States v. Paramount Pictures, 334 U.S. 131, 171–72 (1948) (“[T]he 
requirement that the defendants restore what they unlawfully obtained [through an unlawful

Unwound interlocks are subject to the same mootness analysis as other abandoned per se conduct 
such as market allocation or price fixing.  W. T. Grant Co., 345 U.S. at 632 (voluntary cessation 
of Section 8 interlock does not deprive the tribunal of power to hear and determine the case);

cessation of allegedly illegal conduct does not moot a case” involving price-fixing under Section

combination . . . or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce’ which § 1 of the Act strikes 
down, whether the concerted activity be wholly nascent or abortive on the one hand, or 
successful on the other.”) (citation omitted). 
Accordingly, if a plaintiff properly pleads a likelihood of recurrence or an ongoing harm 
through the wrongful retention of competitively sensitive information obtained through the 
alleged interlocks, Section 8 claims are not moot.  See TRW, 647 F.2d at 953. Even in the face of 
good faith assurances of future compliance with Section 8, a court may find that “the heavy 
burden of proof regarding mootness has not been satisfied.”  Id. 
C. Section 8 Bars Relationships that Create an Interlock Regardless of Form  
It is well-established that an individual or a company can violate Section 8 when it 
“serve[s] as a director or officer” directly or indirectly, e.g., through an agent, deputy, or 
representative. For example, in TRW, the Ninth Circuit held that “section 8 prohibits 
corporations from choosing, and natural persons from serving as, directors in violation of section 
8’s substantive requirements.” Id. at 949. Consistent with TRW, if a company chooses a natural 
person to serve on a competitor’s board as its agent, the company and the natural person violate 
Section 8 unless an exception or safe harbor applies.

California v. Fed. Power Comm’n, 369 U.S. 482, 485 (1962) (“Immunity from the antitrust laws 
is not lightly implied.”). Cons equently, an individual cannot evade liability by serving as an 
“observer” on a competitor’s board.  Similarly, companies cannot evade liability by relying on an 
agent to serve on a competitor’s board. In resolving this matter, the Court should avoid holding 
otherwise. 
A board “observer” may be a “director” under 15 U.S.C. § 19 in certain circumstances.  
Specifically, a company or individual cannot use an indirect means to a prohibited end, such as 
by asking another person to serve as a board observer to obtain entry to a meeting that is 
otherwise off limits due to Section 8’s ban on interlocks.  Such misdirection would undermine 
Section 8’s intent to impose a clear ban on direct involvement in the management of a 
competitor. Accordingly, the Court should determine whether Defendants violated Section 8 
based on case-specific allegations without “exalt[ing] form over substance,” Square, 760 F. 
Supp. at 366, or disturbing longstanding precedent holding that a company may violate Section 8 
when it appoints an agent, deputy, or representative to serve on the board of a competitor, see 
TRW, 647 F.2d at 949; see also Square, 760 F. Supp. at 367 (“[A] cause of action under § 8 is 
stated where a company attempts to place on the Board of a competitor individuals who are 
agents of, and have an employment or business relationship with, such company.”); Reading, 
317 F. Supp. 2d at 331-32 (similar).

Interlocking directorates are an unfair method of competition under federal antitrust law, 
including the FTC Act.  Courts have looked to Section 5 of the FTC Act and its prohibition 
Ohio 1974), aff’d, 513 F.2d 633 (6th Cir. 1975).  In each case, however, the key question is

director’ of [the competing company].” Reading, 317 F. Supp. 2d at 331–32; see also Blau v. 
Lehman, 368 U.S. 403, 410 (1962) (“Lehman Brothers would be a ‘director’ of Tide Water [for 
purposes of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934], if . . . Lehman actually functioned as a 
director through Thomas, who had been deputized by Lehman to perform a director’s duties not 
for himself but for Lehman.”).

III. Group Boycotts Under Section 1 
Plaintiffs seek to enjoin an alleged “fund-no-competitors” edict, which they unify under a 
group boycott theory of antitrust, in violation of Section 1 of the Sherman Act.  The Agencies 
take no position on the alleged facts but file this statement in order to clarify the proper legal 
analysis for group boycotts.
5  Section 1 of the Sherman Act forbids “[e]very contract, 
combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade.” 15 U.S.C. § 1.  
A claim under Section 1 therefore requires two key elements: (1) “a contract, combination, or 
conspiracy”—i.e., “concerted action”— (2) that “unreasonably restrains trade.”  Am. Needle, Inc. 
v. Nat’l Football League, 560 U.S. 183, 186 (2010). Concerted action can be unreasonable in 
one of two ways. Ohio v. Am. Express Co., 585 U.S. 529, 540 (2018). First, it may be 
unreasonable per se, without any further inquiry into its competitive effects or justifications, 
because of its inherently anticompetitive “nature and character.”  Standard Oil Co. of New Jersey 
v. United States, 221 U.S. 1, 64 (1911). Typically, only ho rizontal restraints, i.e., concerted 
action between actual or potential competitors, are per se unlawful.  Am. Express, 585 U.S. at 
541. Second, a restraint may be unreasonable under the “rule of reason,” a “fact-specific 
assessment” of challenged conduct’s “effect on competition.”  Id. (internal quotation marks 
omitted). Group boycotts involving competing firm s can properly be analyzed under the per se 
rule. See Honey Bum, LLC v. Fashion Nova, Inc., 63 F.4th 813, 820–21 (9th Cir. 2023). 
 The litigants disagree about whether the alleged fund-no-competitors edict is horizontal 
or vertical.6  This distinction does not preclude application of Section 1.  Without commenting

unregulated merger.” The charac terization of the relationship between the entities for antitrust 
purposes is a factual inquiry on which the Agencies take no position at this time. 
6 To the extent Defendants dispute the existence of concerted action, i.e., a group boycott against 
OpenAI’s rivals, the Court would err if it required Plaintiffs to establish the existence of a

on the facts as alleged, a group boycott can have a vertical aspect; and per se liability under 
Section 1 can attach so long as there is also a horizontal element to the alleged conspiracy.  See 
NYNEX Corp. v. Discon, Inc., 525 U.S. 128, 136 (1998) (surveying precedent and distinguishing 
purely vertical agreement between supplier and customer from cases also involving a horizontal 
agreement among competitors). Indeed, “[t]he horizontal agreement can exist either among the 
initiators of the boycott (as in Fashion Originators) or those pressured into joining (as in 
Klor’s).” Honey Bum, 63 F.4th at 821. Thus, a conspiracy  among competing firms can be per se 
unlawful even if it is organized by one of their customers, as is the case with some hub-and-
spoke conspiracies. E.g., Klor’s Inc. v. Broadway-Hale Stores, Inc., 359 U.S. 207, 211 (1959); 
Toys “R” Us, Inc. v. FTC, 221 F.3d 928, 936–38 (7th Cir. 2000); United States v. Apple, Inc., 
791 F.3d 290, 323 (2d Cir. 2015). For example, in Apple, a price fixing conspiracy was 
condemned as per se unlawful even though it was organized by a distributor (Apple) that did not 
compete with the other co-conspirators (book publishers).  791 F.3d at 323 (2d Cir. 2015) 
(anticompetitive “threat is just as significant when a vertical market participant organizes the 
conspiracy”). 
Moreover, group boycotts are “not to be tolerated merely because the victim is just one 
merchant whose business is so small that his destruction makes little difference to the economy.” 
Klor’s, 359 U.S. at 213 (“Monopoly can as surely thrive by the elimination of such small 
businessmen, one at a time, as it can by driving them out in large groups”).  And the fact that 
alternative resources may be secured by the boycotted firm beyond those supplied by the 
boycotting group cannot serve as a defense. See PLS.com, 32 F.4th at 835 (“[A] group of 
competitors coercing a competitor’s suppliers . . . constitutes a group boycott even if the 
competitors do not completely cut off the competitor’s access to inputs it needs.”); Klor’s, 359 
“formal agreement” between members of the alleged boycott.  Am. Tobacco v. United States, 328 
U.S. 781, 809 (1946). A “tacit” agreement can also qualify.  Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 
U.S. 544, 553 (2007); see White v. R.M. Packer Co., 635 F.3d 571, 576 (1st Cir. 2011) (A tacit 
agreement is “one in which only the conspirators’ actions . . . indicate [its] existence.”).  For 
example, concerted action may be inferred if one firm invites others to participate in a boycott 
and then those firms engage in conduct showing acceptance.  PLS.com v. Nat’l Ass’n of Realtors, 
32 F.4th 824, 843 (9th Cir. 2022) (citing Interstate Cir. v. United States, 306 U.S. 208, 227 
(1939)).

Musk v. Altman
is an ongoing lawsuit in the
United States District Court for the Northern District of California
in which
Elon Musk
accuses
OpenAI
and its executives, including CEO
Sam Altman
, of violating the company's founding agreement by prioritizing profits over AI safety.
Background
[
edit
]
This section
needs expansion
. You can help by
adding missing information
.
(
April 2026
)
Musk and Altman are co-founders of OpenAI, which was founded in 2015. Musk left in 2018 in the aftermath of a
power struggle
.
[
1
]
Timeline
[
edit
]
Leadup (2024–2026)
[
edit
]
The lawsuit was originally filed on February 29, 2024.
[
2
]
[
3
]
[
4
]
[
5
]
In November 2024, Musk filed a motion for
preliminary injunction
to block OpenAI from changing from a nonprofit to a for-profit.
He believes the conversion violates the terms of his contributions, which total $44 million, to OpenAI from 2016 to 2020.
[
6
]
In February 2025, United States District Judge
Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers
said that Musk's claim of irreparable harm was a stretch.
[
7
]
In April 2025, twelve former employees of OpenAI stated in an amicus brief that OpenAI had abandoned its nonprofit roots and that Altman "was a person of low integrity who had directly lied to employees about the extent of his knowledge and involvement in OpenAI’s practices of forcing departing employees to sign lifetime non-disparagement agreements." The motion was filed by Harvard law professor
Lawrence Lessig
.
[
8
]
In April 2025, OpenAI countersued Musk, claiming that his actions were deliberate tactics to slow OpenAI in order to benefit his own interests.
[
9
]
That month, the California attorney general declined to join Musk's lawsuit.
[
10
]
On May 1, 2025, Judge
Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers
trimmed the lawsuit, excluding claims of false advertising and breach of fiduciary duty, but allowed claims of fraud and unjust enrichment to proceed.
[
11
]
On May 5, 2025, OpenAI announced that it was no longer planning to restructure into a for-profit company separate from its nonprofit board. Later that day, Musk's lawyer
Marc Toberoff
, announced that the lawsuit against OpenAI would continue.
[
12
]
In October 2025, OpenAI formed a for-profit company –  OpenAI Group PBC – with the original non-profit receiving 26 percent ownership in the new entity and Microsoft receiving a 27 percent stake.
[
13
]
As part of the case files disclosed by Musk's lawyers in federal court in November 2025, Altman was shown to have reached out to
Shivon Zilis
on February 9, 2023, asking her advice about whether to publicly praise Musk on X, which he later did.
[
14
]
In April 2026, Musk amended the lawsuit asking that any monetary damages he might be awarded be given to OpenAI's charity and that Altman be removed from OpenAI's board.
[
15
]
In addition to Altman, various other OpenAI executives, including OpenAI president
Greg Brockman
, are being sued by Musk in the lawsuit as well.

----- SOURCE: openai_charter -----
Title: OpenAI Charter
URL: https://openai.com/charter/
Type: primary_source_charter
Description: OpenAI's published charter, central to the breach-of-mission claim.

OpenAI
Company
OpenAI Charter
Our Charter describes the principles we use to execute on OpenAI’s mission.
This document reflects the strategy we’ve refined over the past two years, including feedback from many people internal and external to OpenAI. The timeline to AGI remains uncertain, but our Charter will guide us in acting in the best interests of humanity throughout its development.
OpenAI’s mission is to ensure that artificial general intelligence (AGI)—by which we mean highly autonomous systems that outperform humans at most economically valuable work—benefits all of humanity. We will attempt to directly build safe and beneficial AGI, but will also consider our mission fulfilled if our work aids others to achieve this outcome. To that end, we commit to the following principles:
Broadly distributed benefits
We commit to use any influence we obtain over AGI’s deployment to ensure it is used for the benefit of all, and to avoid enabling uses of AI or AGI that harm humanity or unduly concentrate power.
Our primary fiduciary duty is to humanity. We anticipate needing to marshal substantial resources to fulfill our mission, but will always diligently act to minimize conflicts of interest among our employees and stakeholders that could compromise broad benefit.
Long-term safety
We are committed to doing the research required to make AGI safe, and to driving the broad adoption of such research across the AI community.
We are concerned about late-stage AGI development becoming a competitive race without time for adequate safety precautions. Therefore, if a value-aligned, safety-conscious project comes close to building AGI before we do, we commit to stop competing with and start assisting this project. We will work out specifics in case-by-case agreements, but a typical triggering condition might be “a better-than-even chance of success in the next two years.”
Technical leadership
To be effective at addressing AGI’s impact on society, OpenAI must be on the cutting edge of AI capabilities—policy and safety advocacy alone would be insufficient.
We believe that AI will have broad societal impact before AGI, and we’ll strive to lead in those areas that are directly aligned with our mission and expertise.
Cooperative orientation
We will actively cooperate with other research and policy institutions; we seek to create a global community working together to address AGI’s global challenges.
We are committed to providing public goods that help society navigate the path to AGI. Today this includes publishing most of our AI research, but we expect that safety and security concerns will reduce our traditional publishing in the future, while increasing the importance of sharing safety, policy, and standards research.
----- END SOURCE: openai_charter -----

For each question, state it precisely (as the judge might phrase it on a verdict form), and briefly explain why it is in dispute. Do not yet take a position on any question.
transcript.assistant:
# Legal Questions for the Jury in *Musk v. Altman et al.*

After reviewing all materials, I identify the following legal questions that a juror would likely need to answer on a verdict form. I organize them by claim category.

---

## I. CONTRACT-BASED CLAIMS

### Question 1: Existence of a Contract (Founding Agreement)

**"Have Plaintiffs proven by a preponderance of the evidence that a binding contract — referred to as the 'Founding Agreement' — existed between Elon Musk and one or more Defendants, under which OpenAI would (a) operate as a nonprofit developing AGI for the benefit of humanity rather than for private profit, and (b) make its technology open-source, subject only to safety considerations?"**

**Why disputed:** Musk contends that a binding agreement was formed through oral discussions, emails, and the Certificate of Incorporation. Defendants argue that no enforceable contract existed — that the Certificate of Incorporation is a corporate governance document, not a bilateral contract with Musk; that the emails reflect aspirational discussions, not contractual commitments with definite terms and mutual obligations; and that Musk's donations were charitable contributions, not contractual consideration. The question of whether there was offer, acceptance, consideration, and sufficiently definite terms is genuinely contested.

### Question 2: Terms of the Alleged Contract

**"If a binding contract existed, what were its material terms — specifically, did the contract require that (a) OpenAI would never create a for-profit subsidiary or partner with a for-profit entity, (b) all OpenAI technology would be made publicly available as open source, and (c) OpenAI would never license its technology exclusively to any commercial entity?"**

**Why disputed:** Even if some agreement existed, the parties dispute its scope. OpenAI points to Musk's own emails showing he supported transitioning to a for-profit structure in 2017, even creating a public benefit corporation for that purpose. OpenAI also cites the email exchange where Ilya Sutskever told Musk that "it's totally OK to not share the science" and Musk replied "Yup." Musk contends the core commitments — nonprofit mission, open-source development, benefit to humanity — were non-negotiable foundational terms.

### Question 3: Breach of Contract

**"If a binding contract existed, have Plaintiffs proven by a preponderance of the evidence that Defendants breached the contract by one or more of the following acts: (a) licensing GPT-4 or other technology exclusively to Microsoft; (b) failing to disclose GPT-4's architecture, training data, and methods to the public; (c) operating OpenAI's for-profit subsidiaries in a manner that prioritizes private profit over the public benefit; or (d) permitting Microsoft to exert undue influence over OpenAI, Inc.'s nonprofit board?"**

**Why disputed:** Defendants argue that the capped-profit structure was a permissible evolution consistent with the mission, that Musk was informed of and did not object to the for-profit subsidiary's creation in 2019, that the nonprofit retains control through the board, and that decisions about what to open-source are legitimately driven by safety (as the Charter contemplates reducing traditional publishing). Musk argues the entire trajectory — closed-source GPT-4, exclusive Microsoft licensing, reconstituted board — constitutes a fundamental betrayal of the founding commitments.

### Question 4: Damages for Breach of Contract

**"If Defendants breached the contract, what damages, if any, did Plaintiff suffer as a direct and proximate result of the breach?"**

**Why disputed:** Musk's monetary damages are unclear. He donated approximately $44 million. Whether those donations constitute recoverable damages or whether the proper remedy is equitable (specific performance, injunction) is contested. Defendants argue Musk suffered no cognizable monetary harm because his donations were tax-deductible charitable contributions, and he was offered but declined equity in the for-profit entity.

---

## II. PROMISSORY ESTOPPEL

### Question 5: Promissory Estoppel

**"Have Plaintiffs proven by a preponderance of the evidence that (a) Defendants made clear and unambiguous promises that OpenAI would remain a nonprofit developing open-source AGI for the benefit of humanity; (b) Defendants expected or should have reasonably expected that Plaintiff would rely on those promises; (c) Plaintiff actually and reasonably relied on those promises to his detriment by contributing tens of millions of dollars, time, and resources; and (d) injustice can only be avoided by enforcement of the promises?"**

**Why disputed:** This claim is an alternative to breach of contract — if no formal contract existed, Musk argues the promises still bound Defendants. Defendants counter that Musk's reliance was not reasonable given his active participation in discussions about converting to a for-profit structure, that the promises were not sufficiently definite, and that Musk's own conduct (leaving the organization, declining equity, starting a competitor) undermines his claim of ongoing detrimental reliance.

---

## III. FRAUD / UNJUST ENRICHMENT

### Question 6: Fraud

**"Have Plaintiffs proven by clear and convincing evidence that Defendants made material misrepresentations of fact (or omissions) regarding OpenAI's commitment to its nonprofit mission and open-source principles, that Defendants knew those representations were false or made them with reckless disregard for their truth, that Defendants intended Plaintiff to rely on the representations, that Plaintiff justifiably relied on them, and that Plaintiff suffered damages as a result?"**

**Why disputed:** This claim survived the May 2025 motion to dismiss. Musk argues that Altman and Brockman repeatedly affirmed the nonprofit mission to induce his continued donations while secretly planning or acquiescing to a for-profit transformation. Defendants argue their statements were genuinely held beliefs at the time, that the mission evolved transparently, that Musk was kept informed, and that the nonprofit structure does still technically control the for-profit entities.

### Question 7: Unjust Enrichment

**"Have Plaintiffs proven by a preponderance of the evidence that Defendants were unjustly enriched by receiving Plaintiff's donations and contributions, which were used to develop technology that was then converted to private commercial benefit in a manner inconsistent with the purpose for which the contributions were made?"**

**Why disputed:** This claim also survived dismissal. Musk argues his $44 million in donations were given for a specific charitable purpose and that using the resulting technology (or its derivatives) to generate private profit for Altman, Brockman, and Microsoft constitutes unjust enrichment. Defendants argue the nonprofit legitimately used the funds for AI research consistent with its stated purposes, that the capped-profit structure was disclosed, and that Musk received the tax benefits of charitable giving.

---

## IV. ANTITRUST CLAIMS

### Question 8: Section 8 of the Clayton Act (Interlocking Directorates)

**"Have Plaintiffs proven by a preponderance of the evidence that (a) Reid Hoffman simultaneously served on the boards of Microsoft and OpenAI while the two entities were competitors, or that Deannah Templeton served as a de facto director or agent of Microsoft on OpenAI's board, in violation of Section 8 of the Clayton Act; (b) this conduct has not been mooted by the subsequent resignations; and (c) Plaintiffs suffered injury as a result?"**

**Why disputed:** Defendants argue these interlocks have been unwound and the claims are therefore moot. The FTC/DOJ Statement of Interest specifically addresses this, arguing that mere cessation of an interlock does not automatically moot the claim, and that a board "observer" may constitute a "director" under the statute. The question of whether Microsoft and OpenAI are "competitors" for Section 8 purposes, and whether the statutory thresholds are met, is also contested.

### Question 9: Section 1 of the Sherman Act (Group Boycott / Fund-No-Competitors Edict)

**"Have Plaintiffs proven by a preponderance of the evidence that Defendants engaged in a contract, combination, or conspiracy in restraint of trade — specifically, a group boycott or 'fund-no-competitors' arrangement — whereby Microsoft and/or OpenAI coordinated to prevent investors from funding OpenAI's competitors, and that this unreasonably restrained trade in the market for generative AI models and platforms?"**

**Why disputed:** Plaintiffs allege that Microsoft and OpenAI coordinated to discourage investment in competing AI firms. Defendants deny any such arrangement. The FTC/DOJ Statement of Interest discusses the legal framework for group boycotts but takes no position on the facts. Whether there was concerted action (horizontal or vertical), whether it constituted an unreasonable restraint of trade, and whether Musk (who operates xAI, a competitor) has standing and suffered antitrust injury are all contested.

---

## V. UNFAIR COMPETITION (California Business & Professions Code § 17200)

### Question 10: Unfair Business Practices

**"Have Plaintiffs proven by a preponderance of the evidence that Defendants engaged in unfair, unlawful, or fraudulent business practices by (a) soliciting charitable donations under false pretenses regarding OpenAI's nonprofit mission, (b) maintaining interlocking directorates with a competitor, or (c) engaging in other conduct that threatens an incipient violation of antitrust law or violates the policy or spirit of antitrust law?"**

**Why disputed:** California's UCL is broad, encompassing conduct that is unlawful, unfair, or fraudulent. Musk argues the solicitation of donations for a nonprofit that was secretly being converted to serve private profit is inherently unfair and deceptive. Defendants argue their conduct was lawful, transparent, and consistent with the evolving needs of the organization's mission. The FTC's Statement of Interest suggests that interlocking directorates may constitute unfair methods of competition cognizable under the UCL, even if the specific interlocks no longer exist.

---

## VI. EQUITABLE AND STRUCTURAL REMEDIES

### Question 11: Specific Performance / Injunctive Relief

**"If Plaintiffs have proven one or more of their claims, should the Court order specific performance or injunctive relief requiring Defendants to (a) make OpenAI's AI research and technology available to the public; (b) prohibit Defendants from using OpenAI's assets for the financial benefit of private individuals or entities; and/or (c) remove Sam Altman from OpenAI's board?"**

**Why disputed:** Musk seeks sweeping structural remedies, including that any monetary damages be directed to OpenAI's charity and that Altman be removed from the board. Defendants argue such remedies are unwarranted, unworkable, and would harm the organization and its mission. Whether the court can or should order such relief — and whether a jury or the judge would determine this — is contested.

### Question 12: AGI Determination

**"Has OpenAI attained Artificial General Intelligence ('AGI') — defined as 'a highly autonomous system that outperforms humans at most economically valuable work' — such that GPT-4 or subsequent models fall outside the scope of Microsoft's license?"**

**Why disputed:** Musk's original complaint sought a judicial determination that GPT-4 constitutes AGI. Microsoft's own researchers have described GPT-4 as potentially "an early (yet still incomplete) version of an artificial general intelligence (AGI) system." Defendants argue GPT-4 is not AGI and that this determination is contractually reserved to OpenAI's board. The judge has indicated that "existential risk" testimony is outside the scope of trial, which may limit how this question is framed. Whether this is properly a question for the jury at all, or is a matter of contract interpretation for the court, is itself disputed.

---

## VII. ACCOUNTING

### Question 13: Right to an Accounting

**"Is Plaintiff entitled to an accounting of how his charitable contributions to OpenAI, Inc. were used, and of the intellectual property and derivative works funded by those contributions?"**

**Why disputed:** Musk argues he cannot determine the full extent of how his donations were used without an accounting and that fiduciary obligations to donors require transparency. Defendants argue Musk has no special right to an accounting beyond what is available to any member of the public through nonprofit disclosure requirements, and that his status as a donor does not create a fiduciary relationship entitling him to internal financial records.

---

## CROSS-CUTTING ISSUES

### Question 14: Comparative Fault / Unclean Hands

**"Have Defendants proven by a preponderance of the evidence that Plaintiff's own conduct — including his participation in for-profit restructuring discussions, his departure from OpenAI, his declining of offered equity, and his founding of a direct competitor (xAI) — bars or limits his recovery?"**

**Why disputed:** OpenAI's counterclaim alleges Musk's lawsuit is a bad-faith tactic to slow a competitor. Defendants point to extensive evidence that Musk himself pushed for a for-profit conversion, sought majority control and the CEO title, created a public benefit corporation for that purpose, and only turned against OpenAI after it succeeded without him and he started a competing venture. Musk argues his conduct was consistent with the mission — he wanted any for-profit structure to remain under proper governance controls and left when he couldn't ensure that.

### Question 15: Standing / Cognizable Injury

**"Has Plaintiff demonstrated that he suffered a cognizable injury-in-fact as a result of Defendants' alleged conduct, distinct from any generalized grievance shared by the public at large?"**

**Why disputed:** Defendants argue that Musk's claimed injuries are either too speculative (general harm to humanity) or are offset by tax benefits he received on his donations. They also argue that as the founder of a direct competitor, his real interest is commercial, not charitable. Musk argues his concrete injury is the misuse of his $44 million in donations and the breach of specific promises made to him personally.

---

These questions collectively frame the factual and legal determinations the jury would need to make. Some of these (particularly the equitable remedies and certain legal standards) may ultimately be decided by the judge rather than the jury, but they represent the full scope of contested issues arising from the materials.

transcript.user: Second task. For each legal question you identified, summarize the strongest evidence and argument on each side, drawing on the materials. Be specific about which document or fact supports which claim. Where the materials are silent or contested, say so. Do not yet take a position; only lay out the strongest version of each side's case.
transcript.assistant:
# Evidence and Arguments on Each Legal Question

---

## QUESTION 1: Existence of a Binding Contract (Founding Agreement)

### Strongest Case for Musk (Plaintiff)

**The Certificate of Incorporation is a written memorial of the agreement.** The December 8, 2015 Certificate of Incorporation (Exhibit 1, complaint_initial pp. 37-38) explicitly states: "The resulting technology will benefit the public and the corporation will seek to open source technology for the public benefit when applicable. The corporation is not organized for the private gain of any person." It further states that OpenAI's property is "irrevocably dedicated" to these purposes.

**Email exchanges establish mutual assent.** On June 24, 2015, Altman emailed Musk a detailed proposal stating: "The mission would be to create the first general AI and use it for individual empowerment... The technology would be owned by the foundation and used 'for the good of the world.'" Musk responded: "Agree on all." (Exhibit 2; complaint_initial ¶ 51). This exchange has the hallmarks of offer and acceptance with specific terms.

**Consideration was provided.** Musk contributed over $44 million from 2016 through September 2020 (complaint_initial ¶ 61), leased OpenAI's initial office space (complaint_initial ¶ 62), and was instrumental in recruiting key personnel including Ilya Sutskever (complaint_initial ¶ 59). These are concrete, quantifiable acts of reliance and consideration, not mere charitable gifts — they were made "in exchange and as consideration for the Founding Agreement" (complaint_initial ¶ 124).

**The agreement was reaffirmed over time.** In April 2018, Altman sent Musk a draft Charter describing OpenAI's mission as ensuring AGI "benefits all of humanity" and stating "[o]ur primary fiduciary duty is to humanity" (complaint_initial ¶ 66). The published OpenAI Charter (openai_charter) echoes these same commitments. In September 2017, when the for-profit idea was floated, Altman expressly told Musk: "I remain enthusiastic about the non-profit structure!" (openai_blog_for_profit, September 20, 2017 email). This reaffirmation induced Musk to continue funding.

**The March 2019 for-profit subsidiary announcement explicitly preserved the nonprofit mission.** The term sheet warned investors that the entity "exists to advance OpenAI Inc.'s mission" and that the mission "take[s] precedence over any obligation to generate a profit," advising investors to "view any investment in OpenAI LP in the spirit of a donation" (complaint_initial ¶ 67). This shows the parties understood the nonprofit mission was a binding constraint, not merely aspirational.

### Strongest Case for Defendants

**No formal bilateral contract was ever signed.** There is no document titled "Founding Agreement" and no signed contract between Musk and OpenAI or Musk and Altman/Brockman. The emails cited by Musk are informal discussions about organizational direction, not contractual documents with offer, acceptance, and definite terms. "Agree on all" in response to a conceptual email does not create an enforceable contract.

**The Certificate of Incorporation is a corporate governance document, not a contract with Musk.** Delaware corporate law governs the Certificate, which creates obligations for the corporation and its directors, not bilateral contractual obligations to donors. Musk was never a party to the Certificate — it was signed by incorporator Jonathan Levy (complaint_initial p. 38). Nonprofit donors generally do not acquire contractual rights to dictate corporate governance.

**Musk's contributions were charitable donations, not contractual consideration.** Musk received tax deductions for his contributions (complaint_initial ¶ 114 discusses the tax benefit). Charitable donations are, by their nature, gifts — not payments made in exchange for contractual rights. The distinction matters legally: a donor to the Red Cross cannot later sue to dictate how the Red Cross operates by claiming his donation was "consideration" for an agreement about organizational structure.

**The terms are too indefinite to be enforceable.** What does "open source" mean in practice? What does "benefit humanity" require? When does a safety concern override the open-source commitment? The Charter itself acknowledges: "we expect that safety and security concerns will reduce our traditional publishing in the future" (openai_charter). An agreement whose core terms are this vague fails for indefiniteness.

**Musk himself understood the agreement was evolving.** Musk actively participated in discussions about converting to a for-profit in 2017 (openai_blog_for_profit: "Time to make the next step for OpenAI. This is the triggering event" — August 11, 2017 email). He even created the public benefit corporation "Open Artificial Intelligence Technologies, Inc." on September 15, 2017 (openai_blog_for_profit, Delaware filing). His own conduct shows he did not regard the nonprofit structure as a permanent, immutable contractual term.

---

## QUESTION 2: Terms of the Alleged Contract

### Strongest Case for Musk

**The terms were specific and repeatedly articulated.** Musk argues two core terms: (a) nonprofit structure developing AGI for humanity's benefit, not private profit; and (b) open-source technology, subject only to safety exceptions. These terms appear in the Certificate of Incorporation (Exhibit 1), in Altman's June 2015 email ("The technology would be owned by the foundation and used 'for the good of the world'" — Exhibit 2), in the December 2015 public announcement ("Our goal is to advance digital intelligence in the way that is most likely to benefit humanity as a whole, unconstrained by a need to generate financial return" — Exhibit 3, complaint_initial ¶ 57), and in the Charter (openai_charter).

**The open-source commitment was a defining feature.** The very name "OpenAI" was chosen by Musk to reflect this commitment (complaint_initial ¶ 54). The organization followed through on this for years, openly publishing GPT, GPT-2, and GPT-3's architecture and training details (complaint_initial ¶¶ 79-83). This consistent practice establishes what the parties understood the term to require.

**The terms did not permit converting the nonprofit into a de facto subsidiary of a for-profit corporation.** Even when OpenAI, L.P. was formed, investors were explicitly warned that the mission "take[s] precedence over any obligation to generate a profit" (complaint_initial ¶ 67). The terms contemplated fundraising, not abandonment of the nonprofit mission.

### Strongest Case for Defendants

**Musk's own emails show he did not regard "open source" as absolute.** When Ilya Sutskever wrote: "As we get closer to building AI, it will make sense to start being less open. The Open in openAI means that everyone should benefit from the fruits of AI after its built, but it's totally OK to not share the science," Musk replied: "Yup" (openai_blog_response, footnote 4). This directly contradicts the claim that "open source everything" was a fixed contractual term.

**Musk agreed the nonprofit structure should evolve.** In July 2017, Musk forwarded an article and said "Maybe another reason to change course." Greg Brockman responded proposing "AI research + hardware for-profit (starting 2018)" and Musk responded "Let's talk Sat or Sun. I have a tentative game plan" (openai_blog_for_profit, July 21, 2017 emails). On August 11, 2017, after the Dota victory, Musk emailed: "Time to make the next step for OpenAI. This is the triggering event" (openai_blog_for_profit). He created the Delaware PBC for this purpose (openai_blog_for_profit, September 15, 2017 filing).

**The "benefit humanity" term is aspirational, not operational.** The Charter commits to using "any influence we obtain over AGI's deployment to ensure it is used for the benefit of all" (openai_charter), but this is a statement of values, not a concrete contractual obligation with measurable deliverables. Similarly, the Certificate's statement about open-sourcing "when applicable" contains a significant qualifier.

**Musk's own proposed terms would have given him absolute control, contradicting any claim of altruistic purpose.** He demanded majority equity (50-60%), initial board control, and the CEO position (openai_blog_for_profit: September 4, 2017 iMessage; September 12-13, 2017 email). Ilya and Greg noted: "The current structure provides you with a path where you end up with unilateral absolute control over the AGI" and "The goal of OpenAI is to make the future good and to avoid an AGI dictatorship" (openai_blog_for_profit, September 20, 2017 "Honest Thoughts" email). This suggests even Musk understood the terms were negotiable.

---

## QUESTION 3: Breach of Contract

### Strongest Case for Musk

**GPT-4 was kept entirely closed-source for commercial reasons.** Unlike GPT, GPT-2, and GPT-3 — all of which were accompanied by detailed research papers and had their architectures made public — GPT-4 was released with no publication describing its internal design (complaint_initial ¶ 90). This is a stark departure from established practice and, Musk argues, from the contractual commitment to openness. "Instead, there are just press releases bragging about performance" (complaint_initial ¶ 90).

**GPT-4 was exclusively licensed to Microsoft.** OpenAI entered a license giving Microsoft exclusive commercial rights to GPT-4, which is now integrated into Microsoft's Office suite (complaint_initial ¶ 30). This transforms what was supposed to be technology developed "for the benefit of humanity" into proprietary technology for "literally the largest company in the world" (complaint_initial ¶ 90).

**Microsoft's CEO confirmed the commercial capture.** Satya Nadella stated publicly: "We have all the IP rights and all the capability. If OpenAI disappeared tomorrow... We have the people, we have the compute, we have the data, we have everything. We are below them, above them, around them" (complaint_initial ¶¶ 102, 107). This confirms that OpenAI has become, functionally, a Microsoft subsidiary.

**The board was reconstituted to serve commercial interests.** After the November 2023 board crisis, the independent board members with AI governance expertise (Toner, McCauley, Sutskever) were replaced with individuals more amenable to Altman and Microsoft — including Larry Summers and Bret Taylor, with Microsoft getting an observer seat (complaint_initial ¶¶ 109-110). This gutted the governance structure designed to protect the nonprofit mission.

**GPT-4 may constitute AGI, which is explicitly excluded from the Microsoft license.** Microsoft's own researchers wrote that GPT-4 "could reasonably be viewed as an early (yet still incomplete) version of an artificial general intelligence (AGI) system" (complaint_initial ¶ 89). Under the Microsoft agreement, AGI is excluded from commercial licensing, but the reconstituted board has every incentive never to declare AGI achieved (complaint_initial ¶ 112).

### Strongest Case for Defendants

**The capped-profit structure was disclosed to and accepted by Musk.** When Musk was sent an advance copy of the OpenAI LP announcement in March 2019, "Elon's only feedback was: 'Please be explicit that I have no financial interest in the for-profit arm of OpenAI'" (openai_blog_for_profit). He did not object to the structure itself. He was also offered equity multiple times and declined (openai_blog_for_profit, October 2022 iMessages).

**Musk himself recognized the necessity of massive capital.** In December 2018, Musk emailed: "Even raising several hundred million won't be enough. This needs billions per year immediately or forget it" (openai_blog_for_profit, December 26, 2018 email). The for-profit structure and the Microsoft partnership were the means by which OpenAI obtained the resources Musk himself said were essential.

**Safety concerns, not commercial motives, drove reduced openness.** The Charter itself states: "we expect that safety and security concerns will reduce our traditional publishing in the future" (openai_charter). Musk agreed with this principle when he responded "Yup" to Sutskever's email about being "less open" as they approached AGI (openai_blog_response). The decision not to publish GPT-4's architecture is consistent with this agreed-upon approach.

**The nonprofit still retains ultimate control.** OpenAI GP, L.L.C., which controls the for-profit entities, is wholly owned by OpenAI, Inc. (the nonprofit) and controlled by the nonprofit's board (complaint_initial ¶ 75). The board's fiduciary duty is to humanity, not shareholders (complaint_initial ¶ 75). The corporate structure preserves nonprofit governance even with for-profit activities.

**OpenAI is fulfilling its mission of broad benefit.** Hundreds of millions of people use ChatGPT, including a free version. Albania uses it for EU accession; farmers in Kenya and India benefit from reduced costs; healthcare providers simplify documents with it (openai_blog_response). This is "broadly distributed benefits" as contemplated by the Charter.

---

## QUESTION 4: Damages for Breach of Contract

### Strongest Case for Musk

**Musk contributed $44+ million in reliance on the agreement.** These contributions were specifically made "in exchange and as consideration for the Founding Agreement" (complaint_initial ¶ 124). They were not unconditional gifts but were induced by specific promises about how the technology would be used and governed.

**The value of the misappropriated technology is enormous.** OpenAI's for-profit arm was valued at nearly $80 billion (complaint_initial ¶ 114). The technology developed with Musk's seed funding — which enabled the hiring of key researchers and early compute — laid the foundation for this value. The damages are the difference between what Musk was promised (publicly available AGI technology) and what he got (proprietary technology enriching Microsoft and individual defendants).

**Musk has offered to direct any damages to OpenAI's charity.** Per the Wikipedia summary (wikipedia_case_summary), in April 2026 Musk amended the lawsuit asking that monetary damages go to OpenAI's charity, which undercuts any suggestion that his motives are financial.

### Strongest Case for Defendants

**Musk received the tax benefits of charitable giving.** Musk's donations were tax-deductible (complaint_initial ¶ 114 discusses this dynamic). He received approximately 50 cents in tax relief per dollar donated. He cannot now claim those same donations as contractual damages.

**Musk was offered equity and declined.** "Multiple times over the years, we've offered Elon equity in OpenAI LP which he declined" (openai_blog_for_profit). If Musk felt entitled to a financial stake in OpenAI's commercial success, he had the opportunity and refused it.

**Musk suffered no cognizable personal injury.** His donations were used for AI research — exactly what he donated for. The technology was developed; the nonprofit still exists; the mission continues. That the organizational structure evolved does not mean his money was wasted or misused.

**Any "harm" is speculative and generalized.** Musk's complaint about harm to "humanity" is not a personal injury that can be quantified. His standing to claim damages on behalf of the public is questionable.

---

## QUESTION 5: Promissory Estoppel

### Strongest Case for Musk

**Clear promises were made.** The December 2015 announcement stated: "OpenAI is a non-profit artificial intelligence research company. Our goal is to advance digital intelligence in the way that is most likely to benefit humanity as a whole, unconstrained by a need to generate financial return. Since our research is free from financial obligations, we can better focus on a positive human impact" (Exhibit 3). Altman's June 2015 email made specific promises about the technology being "owned by the foundation and used 'for the good of the world'" (Exhibit 2).

**Defendants expected and induced reliance.** Musk was solicited for funding based on these promises. Musk's $1 billion pledge was specifically requested by the organization — indeed, Musk suggested the large number "to avoid sounding hopeless" (openai_blog_response). The promises were designed to induce exactly the kind of support Musk provided.

**Musk actually relied to his detriment.** He contributed $44+ million, devoted years of personal effort to recruiting, leased office space, and lent his reputation to the enterprise. All of this was done in reliance on the promises made to him.

**Injustice requires enforcement.** If the promises are not enforced, then defendants will have successfully used a nonprofit façade to develop technology worth tens of billions of dollars, funded in part by tax-deductible donations, only to convert it into private commercial gain. This would create a perverse incentive structure (complaint_initial ¶ 113).

### Strongest Case for Defendants

**Musk's reliance was not reasonable given his own conduct.** Musk actively pushed for a for-profit conversion in 2017, demanded majority equity and the CEO title (openai_blog_for_profit, Fall 2017 emails), and created the Delaware PBC himself (September 15, 2017 filing). He cannot claim he relied on a promise of permanent nonprofit status when he himself tried to convert the entity.

**The promises were qualified and evolving.** Musk agreed that openness would decrease over time ("Yup" — openai_blog_response). He recognized the need for billions in funding that a nonprofit couldn't raise (December 2018 email). He acknowledged the for-profit subsidiary without objection (March 2019). His "reliance" was on an understanding that the organization would evolve.

**Musk voluntarily left and started a competitor.** He departed OpenAI in February 2018, and in March 2023 founded xAI, a direct competitor (openai_blog_for_profit timeline). His claim of ongoing detrimental reliance is undermined by the fact that he moved on and is now pursuing the same goal through his own for-profit entity. His own departure was voluntary — he "told our team he was supportive of us finding our own path to raising billions of dollars" (openai_blog_response).

---

## QUESTION 6: Fraud

### Strongest Case for Musk

**Altman and Brockman repeatedly made representations about the nonprofit mission while taking actions inconsistent with it.** Altman told Musk "I remain enthusiastic about the non-profit structure!" in September 2017 (openai_blog_for_profit), yet OpenAI was simultaneously exploring for-profit structures. The OpenAI Charter continued to state "Our primary fiduciary duty is to humanity" (openai_charter) even as OpenAI became increasingly commercial.

**The November 2023 board crisis reveals the deception.** The board fired Altman because "he was not consistently candid in his communications with the board" (complaint_initial ¶ 94). This is OpenAI's own board finding that Altman lacked candor. Former employees in the amicus brief described Altman as "a person of low integrity who had directly lied to employees" (wikipedia_case_summary, April 2025 amicus brief reference).

**The secrecy around GPT-4 was commercially motivated, not safety-motivated.** Musk alleges that "this secrecy is primarily driven by commercial considerations, not safety" (complaint_initial ¶ 30). If OpenAI represented to donors that its technology would be open-source while knowing it would keep key developments proprietary for commercial advantage, that is a material misrepresentation.

**Altman had personal financial conflicts.** In 2019, OpenAI signed a letter of intent to buy $51 million in chips from a startup in which Altman was heavily invested (complaint_initial ¶ 120). This suggests Altman was using OpenAI to advance personal financial interests while representing that the organization existed for public benefit.

### Strongest Case for Defendants

**All material decisions were disclosed.** The for-profit subsidiary was publicly announced in March 2019. The Microsoft partnership was publicly announced. The capped-profit structure was explained publicly. Musk was personally informed of these developments and given advance copies of announcements (openai_blog_for_profit). There is no hidden fraud — the organizational evolution happened in public.

**Altman's statements were sincerely held at the time.** When Altman said he was "enthusiastic about the non-profit structure" in September 2017, that was in the context of rejecting Musk's demand for absolute control — it was a genuine statement about preferring nonprofit governance to Musk's proposed autocracy. The organization's views evolved as circumstances changed, which is not fraud.

**Musk cannot show justifiable reliance.** Given his deep involvement in the organization, his awareness of the financial challenges, his own participation in for-profit discussions, and his departure in 2018, any claim that he was deceived by subsequent organizational changes rings hollow. He was a sophisticated actor with full access to information.

**The fraud claim requires clear and convincing evidence.** This is a higher standard than preponderance of the evidence. The emails in the record show open, candid discussions between the parties — not concealment or deception.

---

## QUESTION 7: Unjust Enrichment

### Strongest Case for Musk

**Defendants personally benefited from Musk's contributions.** Musk's $44 million helped build the technology foundation that became a for-profit enterprise valued at ~$80 billion. Altman and Brockman hold positions of power and, through the for-profit structure, potentially stand to benefit financially. Microsoft invested billions and received commercial licensing rights to technology that Musk's donations helped seed.

**The contributions were made for a specific purpose that was violated.** Musk donated to a nonprofit with an "irrevocable" dedication to charitable purposes (Certificate of Incorporation, Exhibit 1). Using those contributions — or the derivative technology they funded — for private commercial benefit is a classic unjust enrichment scenario.

**The nonprofit playbook concern.** As Musk's complaint argues (complaint_initial ¶ 113), if this conduct stands, any startup could collect tax-deductible nonprofit donations to fund R&D, then slide the resulting IP into a for-profit venture — giving them a massive competitive advantage over honest competitors.

### Strongest Case for Defendants

**The funds were used for their stated purpose: AI research.** Every dollar Musk donated was spent on researchers, compute, and operations to advance AI research — exactly what the nonprofit promised to do. The fact that this research later became commercially valuable does not mean the original expenditure was improper.

**The nonprofit retains structural control.** The for-profit entities are ultimately governed by the nonprofit through OpenAI GP, L.L.C. (complaint_initial ¶ 75). The nonprofit has not been dissolved or captured; it retains oversight.

**Musk was offered a share and refused.** He was offered equity in OpenAI LP "multiple times" and declined (openai_blog_for_profit). He cannot now claim unjust enrichment when he voluntarily refused the very financial interest he now says was owed to him or to the public.

**No individual defendant was personally enriched by Musk's donations.** Sam Altman has stated: "I personally have no equity and never have" (openai_blog_for_profit, October 2022 iMessages). The materials do not contain evidence that Altman or Brockman personally profited from Musk's specific donations.

---

## QUESTION 8: Section 8 of the Clayton Act (Interlocking Directorates)

### Strongest Case for Musk

**Reid Hoffman sat on both boards simultaneously for six years.** The First Amended Complaint alleges Hoffman served on both OpenAI's and Microsoft's boards from March 2017 to March 2023 (ftc_statement_of_interest, p. 2 referencing Am. Compl. ¶¶ 163, 371). Microsoft is a major investor in and commercial partner of OpenAI, and they compete in generative AI (Microsoft with Copilot/Azure AI vs. OpenAI with ChatGPT).

**Deannah Templeton served as a de facto Microsoft representative on OpenAI's board.** She was simultaneously VP of Partnerships and Operations at Microsoft's Technology & Research division and a non-voting member of OpenAI's board from November 2023 to July 2024 (ftc_statement_of_interest, p. 2). The FTC/DOJ Statement of Interest specifically addresses this scenario, arguing that a board "observer" can constitute a "director" under Section 8 and that companies "cannot evade liability by relying on an agent to serve on a competitor's board" (ftc_statement_of_interest, pp. 6-7).

**The claims are not moot despite resignations.** The FTC/DOJ Statement explicitly argues that "ending an interlocking directorate... is not sufficient, on its own, to moot a claim under Section 8" (ftc_statement_of_interest, p. 4), citing TRW v. FTC, 647 F.2d 942 (9th Cir. 1981). Competitively sensitive information exchanged during the interlock period remains in the companies' possession and continues to distort competition.

**The government supports this theory.** The unusual step of the DOJ and FTC jointly filing a Statement of Interest underscores the seriousness of the interlock allegations and the government's view that Section 8 applies to these facts.

### Strongest Case for Defendants

**The interlocks have been unwound.** Neither Hoffman nor Templeton currently serves on OpenAI's board in any capacity (ftc_statement_of_interest, p. 4, noting Defendants' argument). Hoffman left in March 2023; Templeton left in July 2024. There is no ongoing violation.

**Templeton was a non-voting observer, not a director.** Section 8 applies to persons who "serve as a director or officer." A non-voting observer does not have the authority or duties of a director. The FTC's argument that observers can be directors is a legal theory, not established law.

**Microsoft and OpenAI may not be "competitors" for Section 8 purposes.** Section 8 requires that the corporations be "competitors such that the elimination of competition by agreement between them would constitute a violation of any of the antitrust laws." The relationship between Microsoft and OpenAI is primarily that of investor/partner, not competitor. Whether they compete sufficiently in "generative AI models and platforms" to trigger Section 8 is a factual question the materials do not resolve.

**Section 8 has de minimis exceptions.** The statute does not apply if competitive sales fall below certain thresholds (ftc_statement_of_interest, p. 3). The materials do not establish that these thresholds are met.

---

## QUESTION 9: Section 1 Sherman Act (Group Boycott)

### Strongest Case for Musk

**The "fund-no-competitors" allegation.** Plaintiffs allege that Microsoft and OpenAI coordinated to prevent investors from funding OpenAI's competitors (ftc_statement_of_interest, p. 10). The FTC/DOJ Statement confirms that "a group boycott can have a vertical aspect; and per se liability under Section 1 can attach so long as there is also a horizontal element to the alleged conspiracy" (ftc_statement_of_interest, pp. 10-11).

**The FTC/DOJ supports the legal viability of this theory.** The government's Statement of Interest discusses at length how group boycotts can be per se unlawful even when organized by a firm that does not directly compete with the boycotted parties (ftc_statement_of_interest, pp. 10-12, citing Apple, Klor's, and Toys "R" Us).

**Microsoft's dominant position gives the boycott teeth.** Microsoft's massive investment in OpenAI, combined with its cloud computing dominance, means that a coordinated effort to discourage investment in competitors could effectively starve them of resources.

### Strongest Case for Defendants

**The materials contain no specific evidence of a fund-no-competitors arrangement.** The primary source materials (emails, blog posts, filings) do not contain any specific communications evidencing an agreement between Microsoft and OpenAI to prevent investors from funding competitors. The FTC/DOJ Statement explicitly "take[s] no position on the alleged facts" (ftc_statement_of_interest, p. 10).

**Musk's own company, xAI, has raised substantial funding.** If there were an effective group boycott, xAI would have been unable to secure investment. The fact that Musk's own AI competitor exists and apparently operates contradicts the claim that a boycott has been effective. The materials note xAI was formed as a public benefit corporation in March 2023 (openai_blog_for_profit).

**This is a novel and untested theory.** Applying group boycott doctrine to venture capital investment decisions in an emerging technology market would be a dramatic extension of antitrust law.

---

## QUESTION 10: Unfair Competition (UCL § 17200)

### Strongest Case for Musk

**Soliciting donations under false pretenses is inherently unfair.** Musk alleges that defendants solicited tens of millions in charitable donations by representing that the funds would be used for nonprofit development of open-source AGI for humanity's benefit, when in fact the technology was being developed for Microsoft's commercial profit (complaint_initial ¶¶ 139-140).

**The interlocking directorate conduct independently supports a UCL claim.** The FTC/DOJ Statement argues that interlocking directorates are "an unfair method of competition under federal antitrust law, including the FTC Act" and that California courts look to Section 5 of the FTC Act to interpret the UCL (ftc_statement_of_interest, pp. 7-9, citing Cel-Tech Communications and Epic Games v. Apple).

**The conduct violates Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 17510.8.** This statute specifically addresses fiduciary duties owed to charitable donors, and the complaint alleges violation (complaint_initial ¶ 134).

### Strongest Case for Defendants

**The conduct was lawful and transparent.** The for-profit structure was publicly announced, the Microsoft partnership was disclosed, and Musk himself was personally informed. There is no "unfair" practice when all material information was available.

**The UCL claim is derivative of the other claims.** If the contract, fraud, and antitrust claims fail, the UCL claim has no independent basis. California courts require that UCL claims based on the "unfair" prong be tethered to a violation of or threat to antitrust policy (per Cel-Tech). If there is no antitrust violation, there is no UCL violation on the "unfair" prong.

**Musk lacks standing for injunctive relief under the UCL.** The UCL provides only equitable remedies (restitution, injunction). Musk must show he personally "lost money or property" as a result of the unfair practice. His tax-deductible donations may not qualify.

---

## QUESTION 11: Specific Performance / Injunctive Relief

### Strongest Case for Musk

**The stakes justify extraordinary relief.** If OpenAI has attained or is approaching AGI, the consequences of allowing it to remain in the hands of a profit-maximizing corporation are potentially catastrophic for humanity. The Founding Agreement and Charter were designed to prevent exactly this outcome.

**Monetary damages are inadequate.** The harm Musk alleges — the concentration of AGI technology in a for-profit entity rather than making it available for public benefit — cannot be remedied by money. Only specific performance (forcing OpenAI to open-source its technology and return to its nonprofit mission) addresses the actual harm.

**Musk has offered to direct damages to charity and seeks Altman's removal.** Per the Wikipedia summary, Musk has asked that any damages go to OpenAI's charity and that Altman be removed from the board (wikipedia_case_summary, April 2026 amendment). This demonstrates his interest is in the mission, not personal enrichment.

### Strongest Case for Defendants

**Specific performance is inappropriate for ongoing organizational governance.** Courts are reluctant to supervise the internal governance of complex organizations. Ordering OpenAI to open-source all its technology, restructure its board, or remove its CEO would require ongoing judicial supervision that courts generally avoid.

**Forcing open-sourcing could create safety risks.** The Charter and Musk's own agreed-upon statements recognize that openness should decrease as AGI approaches. A court order forcing publication of advanced AI architectures could create genuine safety and security risks.

**The organizational evolution reflects legitimate business judgment.** The for-profit structure was adopted to raise the capital necessary to achieve the mission — capital that Musk himself recognized was essential ("billions per year immediately or forget it"). Unwinding this structure would harm OpenAI's ability to compete with Google/DeepMind, which was the original reason for OpenAI's creation.

**Musk's competitive interest undermines his request for injunctive relief.** Musk operates xAI, a direct competitor. An order forcing OpenAI to open-source its technology would directly benefit xAI. The court should be skeptical of equitable relief that primarily benefits the plaintiff's own competing business.

---

## QUESTION 12: AGI Determination

### Strongest Case for Musk

**Microsoft's own researchers have described GPT-4 as early AGI.** A detailed Microsoft Research paper stated that GPT-4 "could reasonably be viewed as an early (yet still incomplete) version of an artificial general intelligence (AGI) system" (complaint_initial ¶ 89). GPT-4 scores in the 90th percentile on the Bar Exam, 99th percentile on the GRE Verbal, and 77% on the Advanced Sommelier exam (complaint_initial ¶ 86).

**The contractual definition of AGI supports this conclusion.** OpenAI defines AGI as "a highly autonomous system that outperforms humans at most economically valuable work" (openai_charter). GPT-4's performance across a wide range of professional tasks approaches or exceeds human performance.

**The board that determines AGI has been captured.** The determination of whether AGI has been achieved is contractually reserved to OpenAI's board — but that board was reconstituted to be friendly to Altman and Microsoft (
