FIFTH CAUSE OF ACTION 
Accounting  
Against All Defendants 
145. Plaintiff realleges and incorporates by reference only paragraphs of this Complaint 
necessary for his claim of Accounting. 
146. As discussed above, over the course of multiple years, Defendants solicited tens of 
millions of dollars in donations from Plaintiff with representations and assurances that OpenAI, Inc. 
would remain a non-profit irrevocably dedicated to creating safe AGI for public benefit, and that all 
donated funds would be used in accordance with that “irrevocable” mandate. On information and 
belief, Defendants also solicited tens of millions of dollars in donations from others based on the 
same representations.   
147. Contrary to the express purpose for which Plaintiff’s funds were contributed, as set 
forth herein, Defendants used monies received from Plaintiff, and intellectual property and 
derivative works funded by those monies, for “for-profit” purposes, including for the benefit of 
private individuals and likely of the individual Defendants themselves, that directly contravene both 
the letter and the express intent of the parties’ agreement.   
148. Because Defendants are in possession of financial information relating to the use of 
the charitable contributions that Plaintiff and others made to OpenAI, Inc. and the intellectual 
property and derivative works funded by those monies, Plaintiff is presently unable to ascertain his 
interest in or the use, allocation, or distribution of assets without an accounting. Plaintiff is therefore 
entitled to an accounting.

PRAYER FOR RELIEF 
WHEREFORE, Plaintiff prays for judgment against Defendants as follows: 
A. For an order compelling specific performance of Defendants’ repeated contractual 
promises including, without limitation,  
i. An order requiring that Defendants continue to follow OpenAI’s long-
standing practice of making AI research and technology developed at 
OpenAI available to the public, and 
ii. An order prohibiting Defendants from utilizing OpenAI, Inc. or its assets 
for the financial benefit of the individual Defendants, Microsoft, or any 
other particular person or entity; 
B. For a judicial determination that GPT-4 constitutes Artificial General Intelligence 
and is thereby outside the scope of OpenAI’s license to Microsoft; 
C. For a judicial determination that Q* and/or other OpenAI next generation large 
language models in development constitute(s) Artificial General Intelligence and 
is/are outside the scope of OpenAI’s license to Microsoft; 
D. Injunctive relief consistent with and to effectuate items A through C above; 
E. For an award of restitution and/or disgorgement of any and all monies received by 
Defendants while they engaged in the unfair and improper practices described 
herein, in addition to prejudgment interest and penalties, pursuant to Business & 
Professions Code § 17200 et seq.; 
F. For an accounting of funds donated by Plaintiffs and others to OpenAI, Inc., and of 
intellectual property or derivative works funded by same, and of Defendants’ use 
of the same for personal benefit or the benefit of any individual or third-party entity; 
G. For general, compensatory, and punitive damages to be proven at trial, each of 
which Plaintiff will contribute to a non-profit or charity; 
H. For attorneys’ fees pursuant to California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021.5; 
I. For pre-judgment and post-judgment interest at the maximum legal rate; and 
J. For such other and further relief as the Court may deem just and proper.

INTRODUCTION 
The United States, through the U.S. Department of Justice, joined by the Federal Trade 
Commission (“FTC”), respectfully submits this Statement of Interest pursuant to 28 U.S.C. § 
517. The Antitrust Division of the U.S. Department of Justice and the FTC (collectively, the 
“Agencies”) enforce the federal antitrust laws, including the prohibition against interlocking 
directorates in Section 8 of the Clayton Act, 15 U.S.C. § 19,
1 and the prohibition against 
contracts, combinations, and conspiracies in restraint of trade in Section 1 of the Sherman Act, 
15 U.S.C. § 1. The Agencies therefore have a strong interest in their correct application.   
The FTC is further charged by Congress with preventing unfair methods of competition 
under Section 5 of the FTC Act, 15 U.S.C § 45.  Such unfair methods include “incipient 
violations of [the Clayton Act], and conduct which, although not a violation of the letter of the 
antitrust laws, is close to a violation or is contrary to their spirit.”  E.I. du Pont de Nemours & 
Co. v. FTC (Ethyl), 729 F.2d 128, 136–37 (2d Cir. 1984). Califo rnia similarly prohibits unfair 
competition, Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 17200, and federal and state courts look to Section 5 of 
the FTC Act to interpret that statute’s meaning. See Cel-Tech Commc’ns, Inc. v. Los Angeles 
Cellular Tel. Co., 20 Cal. 4th 163, 185–86 (1999) (“As the issue before us in this case arises out 
of a claim of unfair competition . . . the relevant jurisprudence would be that arising under 
section 5’s prohibition against ‘unfair methods of competition.’”).  The FTC therefore has an 
interest in the correct interpretation of California’s Unfair Competition Law (“UCL”), Cal. Bus. 
& Prof. Code § 17200. 
The Agencies file this Statement of Interest in response to Plaintiffs’ Motion for a 
Preliminary Injunction, ECF No. 46, to address the proper standards for analyzing whether (1) a

FACTUAL BACKGROUND 
The First Amended Complaint, ECF No. 32, alleges that “Artificial General 
Intelligence,” i.e., “a machine having intelligence for a wide variety of tasks like a human,” 
poses certain risks. Am. Compl. ¶¶ 4, 74-75. To  address these risks, Plaintiff Musk helped 
establish OpenAI in 2015. Am. Compl. ¶ 88. That same year, OpenAI incorporated as a 
“nonprofit corporation” committed to the “research, development and distribution of technology 
related to artificial intelligence.”  Am. Compl. ¶ 89.   
The First Amended Complaint further alleges that (1) defendant Reid Hoffman 
simultaneously served on OpenAI’s and Microsoft’s boards of directors from March 2017 to 
March 2023, and (2) defendant Deannah Templeton simultaneously served as Vice President of 
Partnerships and Operations in the Technology & Research division at Microsoft and as a non-
voting member of OpenAI’s board of directors from November 2023 until July 2024.  Am. 
Compl. ¶¶ 163, 371; see also id. ¶¶ 168, 331(m), 374. 
Based on these allegations, and others, Plaintiffs bring 26 separate claims including 
breach of contract, fraud, false advertising, breach of fiduciary duty, and claims under the 
Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act.  Plaintiffs also allege Defendants violated 
the Sherman and Clayton Acts based in part on conduct affecting the market for “generative AI 
models and platforms.” Am. Compl. ¶ 204. 
On November 29, 2024, Plaintiffs filed a Motion for a Preliminary Injunction seeking to 
enjoin Defendants from, among other things, “directly or indirectly undertaking any action for 
the purpose of or tending to have the effect of, interlocking directorates or benefitting from

ARGUMENT 
The Agencies offer their views on the proper application of Section 8 of the Clayton Act, 
Section 1 of the Sherman Act, and the UCL to the specific issues discussed below.  The 
Agencies take no position on the ultimate disposition of the motion. 
I. Section 8’s Bar on Interlocking Directorates 
A. Statutory Background 
Section 8 of the Clayton Act provides that “[n]o person shall, at the same time, serve as a 
director or officer in any two corporations . . . that are . . . competitors such that the elimination 
of competition by agreement between them would constitute a violation of any of the antitrust 
laws.” 15 U.S.C. § 19. Section 8 makes interloc king directorates, i.e., overlapping directors or 
officers among competing companies, per se unlawful.  TRW, Inc. v. FTC, 647 F.2d 942, 948 
(9th Cir. 1981). At the same time, this broad prohibition is subject to certain limitations and 
exceptions. See, e.g., 15 U.S.C. § 19(a)(2), (b).  For example, the companies must be engaged in 
interstate commerce and have “capital, surplus, and undivided profits aggregating more than” 
$48,559,000. Id.  Section 8 also does not apply if competitive sales: (A) of either corporation are 
less than $4,855,900; (B) of either corporation are less than two percent of the corporation’s total 
sales; or (C) of each corporation are less than four percent of the corporation’s total sales.  Id. at 
§ 8(a)(2).  Finally, Section 19(b) grants a one-year grace period to individuals who were eligible 
to serve at the time of election but a change in circumstances later created an illegal interlock.  
The purpose of Section 8’s prohibition on interlocking directorates is “to nip in the bud 
incipient antitrust violations by removing the opportunity or temptation for such violations.”  
TRW, 647 F.2d at, 946–47.  Put another way, Section 8 “prevent[s] restraints on competition 
before they materialize[] by outlawing a particular practice thought to facilitate such restraints.”  
Id. at 948; see also Reading Int’l, Inc. v. Oaktree Cap. Mgmt. LLC, 317 F. Supp. 2d 301, 330 
(S.D.N.Y. 2003). For example, preventing interl ocking directorates helps “avoid the opportunity

Case 4:24-cv-04722-YGR Document 87 Filed 01/10/25 Page 9 of 17 
for the coordination of business decisions by competitors and . . . the exchange of commercially 
sensitive information by competitors.” Square D Co. v. Schneider S.A., 760 F. Supp. 362, 366 
(S.D.N.Y. 1991). Such conduct can facilitate unr easonable restraints of trade in violation of 
Section 1 of the Sherman Act (15 U.S.C. § 1) or help a monopolist maintain and entrench its 
power in violation of Section 2 of the Sherman Act (15 U.S.C. § 2).  In this way, Section 8 
supports and reinforces the federal antitrust laws more generally.  See United States v. Sears, 
Roebuck & Co., 111 F. Supp. 614, 616 (S.D.N.Y. 1953) (“Section 8 was but one of a series of 
measures which finally emerged as the Clayton Act, all intended to strengthen the Sherman 
Act.”). 
Courts have consistently interpreted Section 8 in view of these “prophylactic and 
remedial purposes.” Square D Co. v. Schneider S.A., 760 F. Supp. 362, 366 (S.D.N.Y. 1991). 
For example, the Ninth Circuit has held that establishing a Section 8 violation does not require 
“proof that the interlock has an actual anticompetitive effect.” TRW, 647 F.2d at 946–47 (citing 
Protectoseal Co. v. Barancik, 484 F.2d 585, 589 (7th Cir. 1973) (Stevens, J.)). “On the contrary, 
the statute reflects a public interest in preventing directors from serving in positions which 
involve either a potential conflict of interest or a potential frustration of competition.”  
Protectoseal, 484 F.2d at 589. Likewise, courts have refuse d to read the statute so rigidly that a 
company can evade Section 8 liability through tactics that elevate form over substance, e.g., “by 
calling its agents on the competitors’ board something other than either officers or directors.”  
Square, 760 F. Supp. at 366; Reading, 317 F. Supp. 2d at 327–28 (rejecting reading of Section 8 
that would “elevate form over substance” and render the statue “a formalism”). 
B. Section 8 Claims Are Not Mooted by Simply Unwinding an Interlock  
Defendants argue “there is no live ‘case or controversy’” here because “neither Hoffman 
nor Templeton is currently affiliated with OpenAI’s board in any capacity.”  ECF No. 64 at 13. 
But ending an interlocking directorate, e.g., by having a person resign from a corporate board, is 
not sufficient, on its own, to moot a claim under Section 8 of the Clayton Act.  See United States 
v. W. T. Grant Co., 345 U.S. 629, 632 (1953); TRW, 647 F.2d at 953. In resolving this matter, 
the Court should avoid holding otherwise.

Case 4:24-cv-04722-YGR Document 87 Filed 01/10/25 Page 10 of 17 
“[A]s a general rule, ‘voluntary cessation of allegedly illegal conduct does not deprive the 
tribunal of power to hear and determine the case, i.e., does not make the case moot.’”  Cty. of Los 
Angeles v. Davis, 440 U.S. 625, 631 (1979). Otherwise “[a] defendant [would be] free to return 
to his old ways,” and that, “together with a public interest in having the legality of the [disputed] 
practices settled, militates against a mootness conclusion.”  United States v. W. T. Grant Co., 345 
U.S. 629, 632 (1953). Thus, where a defendant volunt arily ceases illegal conduct, it still bears a 
“heavy burden” to demonstrate it is “absolutely clear that the allegedly wrongful behavior could 
not reasonably be expected to recur.” TRW, 647 F.2d at 953 (quoting United States v. 
Concentrated Phosphate Export Ass’n, 393 U.S. 199, 203 (1968)). Additionally, the relevant 
“concern is with repeated violations of the same law, and not merely with repetition of the same 
offensive conduct.” Id. 
The issue of mootness in the context of Section 8 was addressed squarely by the Ninth 
Circuit in TRW. 647 F.2d at 953. There, the interlocking director (i) resigned his position at the 
competing company, (ii) “decided not to stand for reelection,” (iii) and “provided sworn 
assurances that [he] would avoid offensive directorships in the future.”  Id. The Ninth Circuit 
still held the case was not moot.  Id. As the Ninth Circuit expl ained, mootness depends on the 
risk that a defendant may violate Section 8 in the future, not simply whether it might resume the 
specific interlock at issue.  Id. That risk may remain even af ter an interlock is unwound despite 
“good faith . . . assurances of future compliance.” Id. 
Moreover, “a potential conflict of interest or a potential frustration of competition,” 
Protectoseal, 484 F.2d at 589, may remain even after an interlock is resolved and even if one is 
unlikely to recur. For example, if compan ies use interlocking directors to exchange 
competitively sensitive information, see Square, 760 F. Supp. at 367, they may frustrate 
competition so long as the information remains sensitive and in their possession.  Thus, holding 
that a company moots a potential claim under Section 8 by simply ending an interlock would be 
contrary to the purpose of the statute and would significantly undermine enforcement of the 
Clayton Act. Cf. United States v. Paramount Pictures, 334 U.S. 131, 171–72 (1948) (“[T]he 
requirement that the defendants restore what they unlawfully obtained [through an unlawful

antitrust conspiracy] is no more punishment than the familiar remedy of restitution.”); United 
Auto Workers Advisory Opinion, 97 F.T.C. 933 (May 1, 1981) (“The basic purposes of Section 8 
are to avoid the opportunity for coordination of business decisions by competitors and to prevent 
the exchange of commercially sensitive information among competitors.”) (emphasis added). 
Unwound interlocks are subject to the same mootness analysis as other abandoned per se conduct 
such as market allocation or price fixing.  W. T. Grant Co., 345 U.S. at 632 (voluntary cessation 
of Section 8 interlock does not deprive the tribunal of power to hear and determine the case); 
United States v. Concentrated Phosphate Exp. Ass’n, 393 U.S. 199, 203 (1968) (“mere voluntary 
cessation of allegedly illegal conduct does not moot a case” involving price-fixing under Section 
1); United States v. Socony-Vacuum Oil Co., 310 U.S. 150, 224 n.59 (1940) (“It is the ‘contract, 
combination . . . or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce’ which § 1 of the Act strikes 
down, whether the concerted activity be wholly nascent or abortive on the one hand, or 
successful on the other.”) (citation omitted). 
Accordingly, if a plaintiff properly pleads a likelihood of recurrence or an ongoing harm 
through the wrongful retention of competitively sensitive information obtained through the 
alleged interlocks, Section 8 claims are not moot.  See TRW, 647 F.2d at 953. Even in the face of 
good faith assurances of future compliance with Section 8, a court may find that “the heavy 
burden of proof regarding mootness has not been satisfied.”  Id. 
C. Section 8 Bars Relationships that Create an Interlock Regardless of Form  
It is well-established that an individual or a company can violate Section 8 when it 
“serve[s] as a director or officer” directly or indirectly, e.g., through an agent, deputy, or 
representative. For example, in TRW, the Ninth Circuit held that “section 8 prohibits 
corporations from choosing, and natural persons from serving as, directors in violation of section 
8’s substantive requirements.” Id. at 949. Consistent with TRW, if a company chooses a natural 
person to serve on a competitor’s board as its agent, the company and the natural person violate 
Section 8 unless an exception or safe harbor applies.

corporations (and individuals) to evade antitrust liability simply by designating agents to serve 
their bidding on the boards of competing businesses.”  Reading, 317 F. Supp. 2d at 331; see also 
California v. Fed. Power Comm’n, 369 U.S. 482, 485 (1962) (“Immunity from the antitrust laws 
is not lightly implied.”). Cons equently, an individual cannot evade liability by serving as an 
“observer” on a competitor’s board.  Similarly, companies cannot evade liability by relying on an 
agent to serve on a competitor’s board. In resolving this matter, the Court should avoid holding 
otherwise. 
A board “observer” may be a “director” under 15 U.S.C. § 19 in certain circumstances.  
Specifically, a company or individual cannot use an indirect means to a prohibited end, such as 
by asking another person to serve as a board observer to obtain entry to a meeting that is 
otherwise off limits due to Section 8’s ban on interlocks.  Such misdirection would undermine 
Section 8’s intent to impose a clear ban on direct involvement in the management of a 
competitor. Accordingly, the Court should determine whether Defendants violated Section 8 
based on case-specific allegations without “exalt[ing] form over substance,” Square, 760 F. 
Supp. at 366, or disturbing longstanding precedent holding that a company may violate Section 8 
when it appoints an agent, deputy, or representative to serve on the board of a competitor, see 
TRW, 647 F.2d at 949; see also Square, 760 F. Supp. at 367 (“[A] cause of action under § 8 is 
stated where a company attempts to place on the Board of a competitor individuals who are 
agents of, and have an employment or business relationship with, such company.”); Reading, 
317 F. Supp. 2d at 331-32 (similar). 
II. Interlocking Directorates Are an Unfair Method of Competition 
Interlocking directorates are an unfair method of competition under federal antitrust law, 
including the FTC Act.  Courts have looked to Section 5 of the FTC Act and its prohibition 
Ohio 1974), aff’d, 513 F.2d 633 (6th Cir. 1975).  In each case, however, the key question is 
whether “it can legitimately be said that it is [the defendant] as an entity . . . which ‘serve[s] as a 
director’ of [the competing company].” Reading, 317 F. Supp. 2d at 331–32; see also Blau v. 
Lehman, 368 U.S. 403, 410 (1962) (“Lehman Brothers would be a ‘director’ of Tide Water [for 
purposes of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934], if . . . Lehman actually functioned as a 
director through Thomas, who had been deputized by Lehman to perform a director’s duties not 
for himself but for Lehman.”).

language and obvious identity of purpose of the two statutes, decisions of the federal court on the 
subject are more than ordinarily persuasive.’”); Epic Games v. Apple, 67 F.4th 946, 1000–1002 
(9th Cir. 2023) (discussing the relationship between state unfair competition claims and federal 
antitrust claims). “The standard of ‘unfairness’ under the FTC Act is, by necessity, an elusive 
one, encompassing not only practices that violate the Sherman Act and the other antitrust laws, 
but also practices that the Commission determines are against public policy for other reasons.”  
Fed. Trade Comm’n v. Amazon.com, Inc., No. 2:23-cv-01495-JHC, 2024 WL 4448815, at *13 
(W.D. Wash. Sept. 30, 2024) (quoting Fed. Trade Comm’n v. Indiana Fed’n of Dentists, 476 
U.S. 447, 454 (1986)). See also Policy Statement Regarding the Scope of Unfair Methods of 
Competition Under Section 5 of the Federal Trade Commission Act, File No. P221202, at 1 
(F.T.C. Nov. 10, 2022) (“Section 5 reaches beyond the Sherman and Clayton Acts to encompass 
various types of unfair conduct that tend to negatively affect competitive conditions.”) 
(collecting cases). 
The Supreme Court has stated that the FTC Act’s prohibition on unfair methods of 
competition was “designed to supplement and bolster the Sherman Act and the Clayton Act . . . 
to stop in their incipiency acts and practices which, when full blown, would violate [the Sherman 
Act and the Clayton Act] . . . .” Fed. Trade Comm’n v. Motion Picture Advertising Service Co., 
344 U.S. 392, 394–95 (1953) (internal citations omitted); see also Ethyl, 729 F.2d at 136–37 (the 
FTC Act empowers the FTC “to bar incipient violations of those statutes, and conduct which, 
although not a violation of the letter of the antitrust laws, is close to a violation or is contrary to 
their spirit”) (cleaned up); Fed. Trade Comm’n v. Texaco, Inc., 393 U.S. 223, 225 (1968) (same). 
The Supreme Court has likewise long recognized that the FTC “has broad powers to 
declare trade practices unfair.” Fed. Trade Comm’n v. Sperry & Hutchinson Co., 405 U.S. 233, 
242 (1972) (quoting Fed. Trade Comm’n v. Brown Shoe Co., 384 U.S. 316, 320–321 (1966)). In 
1977, the FTC concluded that certain interlocks cannot “escape liability through the allegedly 
porous wording of Section 8.” In re Kraftco Corp., 89 F.T.C. 46, 1977 WL 188540, at *13 (Jan.

III. Group Boycotts Under Section 1 
Plaintiffs seek to enjoin an alleged “fund-no-competitors” edict, which they unify under a 
group boycott theory of antitrust, in violation of Section 1 of the Sherman Act.  The Agencies 
take no position on the alleged facts but file this statement in order to clarify the proper legal 
analysis for group boycotts.
5  Section 1 of the Sherman Act forbids “[e]very contract, 
combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade.” 15 U.S.C. § 1.  
A claim under Section 1 therefore requires two key elements: (1) “a contract, combination, or 
conspiracy”—i.e., “concerted action”— (2) that “unreasonably restrains trade.”  Am. Needle, Inc. 
v. Nat’l Football League, 560 U.S. 183, 186 (2010). Concerted action can be unreasonable in 
one of two ways. Ohio v. Am. Express Co., 585 U.S. 529, 540 (2018). First, it may be 
unreasonable per se, without any further inquiry into its competitive effects or justifications, 
because of its inherently anticompetitive “nature and character.”  Standard Oil Co. of New Jersey 
v. United States, 221 U.S. 1, 64 (1911). Typically, only ho rizontal restraints, i.e., concerted 
action between actual or potential competitors, are per se unlawful.  Am. Express, 585 U.S. at 
541. Second, a restraint may be unreasonable under the “rule of reason,” a “fact-specific 
assessment” of challenged conduct’s “effect on competition.”  Id. (internal quotation marks 
omitted). Group boycotts involving competing firm s can properly be analyzed under the per se 
rule. See Honey Bum, LLC v. Fashion Nova, Inc., 63 F.4th 813, 820–21 (9th Cir. 2023). 
 The litigants disagree about whether the alleged fund-no-competitors edict is horizontal 
or vertical.6  This distinction does not preclude application of Section 1.  Without commenting 
5 The operative complaint and preliminary injunction motion raise questions about the 
relationship between OpenAI and Microsoft, namely whether it constitutes a form of prohibited 
coordinated conduct under the Sherman Act or, alternatively, may be characterized as “an 
unregulated merger.” The charac terization of the relationship between the entities for antitrust 
purposes is a factual inquiry on which the Agencies take no position at this time. 
6 To the extent Defendants dispute the existence of concerted action, i.e., a group boycott against 
OpenAI’s rivals, the Court would err if it required Plaintiffs to establish the existence of a

on the facts as alleged, a group boycott can have a vertical aspect; and per se liability under 
Section 1 can attach so long as there is also a horizontal element to the alleged conspiracy.  See 
NYNEX Corp. v. Discon, Inc., 525 U.S. 128, 136 (1998) (surveying precedent and distinguishing 
purely vertical agreement between supplier and customer from cases also involving a horizontal 
agreement among competitors). Indeed, “[t]he horizontal agreement can exist either among the 
initiators of the boycott (as in Fashion Originators) or those pressured into joining (as in 
Klor’s).” Honey Bum, 63 F.4th at 821. Thus, a conspiracy  among competing firms can be per se 
unlawful even if it is organized by one of their customers, as is the case with some hub-and-
spoke conspiracies. E.g., Klor’s Inc. v. Broadway-Hale Stores, Inc., 359 U.S. 207, 211 (1959); 
Toys “R” Us, Inc. v. FTC, 221 F.3d 928, 936–38 (7th Cir. 2000); United States v. Apple, Inc., 
791 F.3d 290, 323 (2d Cir. 2015). For example, in Apple, a price fixing conspiracy was 
condemned as per se unlawful even though it was organized by a distributor (Apple) that did not 
compete with the other co-conspirators (book publishers).  791 F.3d at 323 (2d Cir. 2015) 
(anticompetitive “threat is just as significant when a vertical market participant organizes the 
conspiracy”). 
Moreover, group boycotts are “not to be tolerated merely because the victim is just one 
merchant whose business is so small that his destruction makes little difference to the economy.” 
Klor’s, 359 U.S. at 213 (“Monopoly can as surely thrive by the elimination of such small 
businessmen, one at a time, as it can by driving them out in large groups”).  And the fact that 
alternative resources may be secured by the boycotted firm beyond those supplied by the 
boycotting group cannot serve as a defense. See PLS.com, 32 F.4th at 835 (“[A] group of 
competitors coercing a competitor’s suppliers . . . constitutes a group boycott even if the 
competitors do not completely cut off the competitor’s access to inputs it needs.”); Klor’s, 359 
“formal agreement” between members of the alleged boycott.  Am. Tobacco v. United States, 328 
U.S. 781, 809 (1946). A “tacit” agreement can also qualify.  Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 
U.S. 544, 553 (2007); see White v. R.M. Packer Co., 635 F.3d 571, 576 (1st Cir. 2011) (A tacit 
agreement is “one in which only the conspirators’ actions . . . indicate [its] existence.”).  For 
example, concerted action may be inferred if one firm invites others to participate in a boycott 
and then those firms engage in conduct showing acceptance.  PLS.com v. Nat’l Ass’n of Realtors, 
32 F.4th 824, 843 (9th Cir. 2022) (citing Interstate Cir. v. United States, 306 U.S. 208, 227 
(1939)).

(
April 2026
)
Musk v. Altman
is an ongoing lawsuit in the
United States District Court for the Northern District of California
in which
Elon Musk
accuses
OpenAI
and its executives, including CEO
Sam Altman
, of violating the company's founding agreement by prioritizing profits over AI safety.
Background
[
edit
]
This section
needs expansion
. You can help by
adding missing information
.
(
April 2026
)
Musk and Altman are co-founders of OpenAI, which was founded in 2015. Musk left in 2018 in the aftermath of a
power struggle
.
[
1
]
Timeline
[
edit
]
Leadup (2024–2026)
[
edit
]
The lawsuit was originally filed on February 29, 2024.
[
2
]
[
3
]
[
4
]
[
5
]
In November 2024, Musk filed a motion for
preliminary injunction
to block OpenAI from changing from a nonprofit to a for-profit.
He believes the conversion violates the terms of his contributions, which total $44 million, to OpenAI from 2016 to 2020.
[
6
]
In February 2025, United States District Judge
Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers
said that Musk's claim of irreparable harm was a stretch.
[
7
]
In April 2025, twelve former employees of OpenAI stated in an amicus brief that OpenAI had abandoned its nonprofit roots and that Altman "was a person of low integrity who had directly lied to employees about the extent of his knowledge and involvement in OpenAI’s practices of forcing departing employees to sign lifetime non-disparagement agreements." The motion was filed by Harvard law professor
Lawrence Lessig
.
[
8
]
In April 2025, OpenAI countersued Musk, claiming that his actions were deliberate tactics to slow OpenAI in order to benefit his own interests.
[
9
]
That month, the California attorney general declined to join Musk's lawsuit.
[
10
]
On May 1, 2025, Judge
Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers
trimmed the lawsuit, excluding claims of false advertising and breach of fiduciary duty, but allowed claims of fraud and unjust enrichment to proceed.
[
11
]
On May 5, 2025, OpenAI announced that it was no longer planning to restructure into a for-profit company separate from its nonprofit board. Later that day, Musk's lawyer
Marc Toberoff
, announced that the lawsuit against OpenAI would continue.
[
12
]
In October 2025, OpenAI formed a for-profit company –  OpenAI Group PBC – with the original non-profit receiving 26 percent ownership in the new entity and Microsoft receiving a 27 percent stake.
[
13
]
As part of the case files disclosed by Musk's lawyers in federal court in November 2025, Altman was shown to have reached out to
Shivon Zilis
on February 9, 2023, asking her advice about whether to publicly praise Musk on X, which he later did.
[
14
]
In April 2026, Musk amended the lawsuit asking that any monetary damages he might be awarded be given to OpenAI's charity and that Altman be removed from OpenAI's board.
[
15
]
In addition to Altman, various other OpenAI executives, including OpenAI president
Greg Brockman
, are being sued by Musk in the lawsuit as well.
[
16
]
[
17
]

for the case began on April 27, 2026.

16

17

On April 28, 2026, Elon Musk began his testimony against Altman and OpenAI, lasting until April 30;
[
18
]

19
]
he was also questioned by both OpenAI's attorney
William Savitt
[
20
]
and by Musk's own lawyer
Steven Molo
.
[
21

The next witness on Musk's side, after Musk himself finished testifying, was
Jared Birchall
.

22

Stuart J. Russell

Title: OpenAI Charter
URL: https://openai.com/charter/
Type: primary_source_charter
Description: OpenAI's published charter, central to the breach-of-mission claim.

OpenAI
Company
OpenAI Charter
Our Charter describes the principles we use to execute on OpenAI’s mission.
This document reflects the strategy we’ve refined over the past two years, including feedback from many people internal and external to OpenAI. The timeline to AGI remains uncertain, but our Charter will guide us in acting in the best interests of humanity throughout its development.
OpenAI’s mission is to ensure that artificial general intelligence (AGI)—by which we mean highly autonomous systems that outperform humans at most economically valuable work—benefits all of humanity. We will attempt to directly build safe and beneficial AGI, but will also consider our mission fulfilled if our work aids others to achieve this outcome. To that end, we commit to the following principles:
Broadly distributed benefits
We commit to use any influence we obtain over AGI’s deployment to ensure it is used for the benefit of all, and to avoid enabling uses of AI or AGI that harm humanity or unduly concentrate power.
Our primary fiduciary duty is to humanity. We anticipate needing to marshal substantial resources to fulfill our mission, but will always diligently act to minimize conflicts of interest among our employees and stakeholders that could compromise broad benefit.
Long-term safety
We are committed to doing the research required to make AGI safe, and to driving the broad adoption of such research across the AI community.
We are concerned about late-stage AGI development becoming a competitive race without time for adequate safety precautions. Therefore, if a value-aligned, safety-conscious project comes close to building AGI before we do, we commit to stop competing with and start assisting this project. We will work out specifics in case-by-case agreements, but a typical triggering condition might be “a better-than-even chance of success in the next two years.”
Technical leadership
To be effective at addressing AGI’s impact on society, OpenAI must be on the cutting edge of AI capabilities—policy and safety advocacy alone would be insufficient.
We believe that AI will have broad societal impact before AGI, and we’ll strive to lead in those areas that are directly aligned with our mission and expertise.
Cooperative orientation
We will actively cooperate with other research and policy institutions; we seek to create a global community working together to address AGI’s global challenges.
We are committed to providing public goods that help society navigate the path to AGI. Today this includes publishing most of our AI research, but we expect that safety and security concerns will reduce our traditional publishing in the future, while increasing the importance of sharing safety, policy, and standards research.
----- END SOURCE: openai_charter -----

=== END MATERIALS ===

First task. Read the materials in full. Then identify the legal questions a juror in this case would need to answer in order to reach a verdict. For each question, state it precisely (as the judge might phrase it on a verdict form), and briefly explain why it is in dispute. Do not yet take a position on any question.
transcript.assistant:
Below are the main factual/legal questions a civil juror would likely need to answer, framed as cleanly as possible as verdict-form questions. I’m basing this on the claims in Musk’s complaint, the OpenAI materials, and the antitrust issues highlighted by the FTC statement. I am not deciding any of them here.

Because this is a federal jury trial, the actual verdict form may be narrower than the original complaint: some claims may have been dismissed, narrowed, or reserved for the judge. But from the materials provided, these are the core questions a juror would need to resolve.

---

## 1. Contract / “Founding Agreement” questions

### Q1. Did Elon Musk prove that he entered into an enforceable agreement with Sam Altman, Greg Brockman, and/or OpenAI under which OpenAI would:
1. remain a nonprofit developing AGI for the benefit of humanity rather than for private profit, and/or  
2. make its AI research and technology publicly available or open-source except as limited by genuine safety concerns?

**Why this is disputed:**  
This is the heart of Musk’s case. The complaint says there was a binding “Founding Agreement,” allegedly reflected in emails, public statements, and the certificate of incorporation. OpenAI disputes that there was any enforceable contract of the sort Musk describes, and points to emails showing Musk himself favored a for-profit structure and accepted reduced openness as safety concerns increased.

### Q2. If such an agreement existed, who were the parties to it?
- Sam Altman?
- Greg Brockman?
- OpenAI, Inc.?
- any later OpenAI entities?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Even if the jury found some agreement existed, it must decide who actually made it and who can be liable for breaching it. The complaint sweeps in many OpenAI entities formed later; defendants likely dispute that later entities were parties to any 2015 understanding.

### Q3. Did Musk provide consideration for that agreement—such as money, recruiting assistance, strategic advice, or reputational support—in exchange for the promises he alleges?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Musk says his funding and efforts were given in exchange for the founding promises. Defendants may argue his donations were charitable contributions to a nonprofit mission generally, not bargained-for consideration for a personal contract enforceable by him.

### Q4. If an enforceable agreement existed, what were its actual terms?
For example:
- Was “nonprofit” required permanently, or only initially?
- Was “open-source” mandatory in all material respects, or only “when applicable” / subject to safety?
- Did the agreement forbid a for-profit subsidiary or partnership?
- Did it forbid commercial licensing to Microsoft?

**Why this is disputed:**  
The materials cut both ways. Musk relies on broad mission language and public statements. OpenAI points to charter language like “seek to open source technology for the public benefit when applicable,” and later public charter language saying safety/security concerns may reduce traditional publishing. The scope of any promise is sharply contested.

### Q5. Did defendants breach any enforceable agreement with Musk?

Possible alleged breaches include:
- creating or operating a for-profit structure,
- allegedly prioritizing Microsoft/profit over humanity,
- keeping GPT-4 closed,
- licensing technology to Microsoft,
- failing to release technology openly.

**Why this is disputed:**  
Musk says OpenAI became “closed, for-profit AI.” OpenAI says the structure change was necessary to pursue the mission, Musk knew that, and he himself advocated for a for-profit transition.

### Q6. If there was a breach, was Musk harmed by it, and if so, what damages, if any, did he prove?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Musk seeks damages, disgorgement, and specific relief. Defendants likely dispute that he suffered compensable personal harm from donations made to a nonprofit, especially if he did not retain ownership rights.

---

## 2. Promissory estoppel questions

### Q7. Did defendants make a clear and definite promise to Musk that OpenAI would remain a nonprofit and/or open its AGI-related technology to the public, except for safety-based limits?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Promissory estoppel is an alternative if no contract existed. The dispute is whether the statements were definite promises or aspirational mission statements.

### Q8. Did defendants reasonably expect Musk to rely on those promises?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Musk says the promises were made to induce his funding and support. Defendants may argue these were broad public statements, not targeted inducements creating a personal right of reliance.

### Q9. Did Musk in fact reasonably rely on those promises in donating money or providing other support to OpenAI?

**Why this is disputed:**  
OpenAI’s email releases are aimed squarely at reliance: they suggest Musk knew a for-profit transition was contemplated and even pushed for it. If true, that would undermine any claim that he reasonably relied on permanence of a nonprofit-only, open-source model.

### Q10. Can injustice be avoided only by enforcing the alleged promise or awarding relief to Musk?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Even if promises were made and relied upon, the jury may need to decide whether equity requires a remedy, especially given Musk’s sophistication, later departure, and later competition through xAI.

---

## 3. Fraud / misrepresentation-type questions
(These may be central if, as the later procedural history suggests, fraud-related claims survived while some others were trimmed.)

### Q11. Did any defendant make a false representation of material fact to Musk about OpenAI’s mission, structure, openness, or intended use of his contributions?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Musk’s theory is essentially that he was induced by representations that OpenAI would not become what it became. Defendants will argue the statements were true when made, were aspirational, or were never false.

### Q12. At the time any such representation was made, did the speaker know it was false, or make it recklessly without regard for its truth?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Fraud requires more than inconsistency or later change. The dispute is whether Altman/Brockman/OpenAI secretly intended from the start to convert the enterprise into a closed, profit-driven company, or whether circumstances genuinely changed over time.

### Q13. Did the defendant intend that Musk rely on the alleged misrepresentation?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Musk says he was induced to fund and build OpenAI. Defendants may say no misrepresentation was made for that purpose.

### Q14. Did Musk justifiably rely on the alleged misrepresentation?

**Why this is disputed:**  
This is likely a major battleground. OpenAI’s released emails are meant to show Musk was deeply involved, knew the economics were changing, and himself proposed for-profit options. That could defeat justifiable reliance.

### Q15. Was Musk damaged as a result of that reliance?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Again, the issue is whether his donations/support translate into personal legally cognizable damages.

---

## 4. Unjust enrichment / restitution questions
(Depending on what claims survived to trial, these may be framed as restitutionary rather than strict contract.)

### Q16. Did defendants receive a benefit from Musk—money, labor, recruiting help, strategy, or credibility—under circumstances that make it unjust for them to retain that benefit without compensating him or providing restitution?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Musk says defendants leveraged his contributions to build a different enterprise than the one he was promised. Defendants will say the donations were made voluntarily to advance OpenAI’s mission and were used for that purpose.

### Q17. If so, what amount of restitution, if any, would fairly prevent unjust enrichment?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Even if liability were found, the measure of recovery is contested.

---

## 5. Fiduciary-duty / charitable-use questions
(Some of these may ultimately be for the court, and some may have been dismissed later, but they are clearly embedded in the pleadings.)

### Q18. Did any defendant owe Musk a fiduciary duty with respect to his donations or OpenAI’s use of assets funded by those donations?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Musk says nonprofit solicitors and insiders owed duties to use contributions for the promised purposes. Defendants likely dispute any personal fiduciary duty running from them to Musk.

### Q19. If such a fiduciary duty existed, did any defendant breach it by diverting OpenAI assets or technology toward private profit, Microsoft’s benefit, or their own benefit contrary to the purposes for which Musk’s contributions were made?

**Why this is disputed:**  
This is Musk’s “you took charitable/nonprofit resources and converted them into private gain” theory. Defendants answer that the for-profit structure remained mission-governed and was created precisely to fulfill OpenAI’s mission.

### Q20. Was Musk harmed by any such breach?

**Why this is disputed:**  
As above, causation and personal harm are contested.

---

## 6. Mission / AGI-status questions

### Q21. Did GPT-4 qualify as “AGI” under the relevant governing definition applicable to OpenAI’s obligations?

Possible formulations:
- Did GPT-4 constitute “artificial general intelligence”?
- Did it constitute “a highly autonomous system that outperforms humans at most economically valuable work”?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Musk alleges GPT-4 is already AGI or close enough that it falls outside Microsoft’s pre-AGI license. He cites the Microsoft “Sparks of AGI” paper. OpenAI would likely argue GPT-4 is not AGI under its governing definitions and internal governance framework.

### Q22. If GPT-4 was AGI, did defendants nonetheless treat it as licensable pre-AGI technology for Microsoft’s commercial benefit?

**Why this is disputed:**  
This matters because Musk says the Microsoft deal itself violates the founding commitments if GPT-4 crossed the AGI threshold.

### Q23. Did defendants improperly withhold or manipulate any AGI determination that should have been made by OpenAI’s board?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Musk argues the November 2023 board events left a captured or compliant board that would never declare AGI because doing so would cut off Microsoft’s rights. Defendants dispute both the factual premise and the legal significance.

---

## 7. Openness / secrecy questions

### Q24. Did OpenAI abandon a binding commitment to openness by withholding model details, code, architecture, or access to GPT-4 primarily for commercial reasons rather than legitimate safety or security reasons?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Musk says the secrecy is profit-driven. OpenAI counters with evidence, including the Ilya/Musk exchange, that “open” did not mean automatically open-sourcing advanced AGI and that less openness near AGI was understood from the beginning.

### Q25. Did the parties understand from the outset that reduced openness could be justified by safety concerns as capabilities advanced?

**Why this is disputed:**  
This is a key interpretive fight. OpenAI has a direct “Yup” email from Musk acknowledging that it could make sense to be less open as AI gets more powerful. Musk’s side likely argues that this did not authorize broad commercial secrecy for profit.

---

## 8. Corporate-structure / control questions

### Q26. Did OpenAI’s creation of a for-profit or capped-profit structure violate any enforceable promise owed to Musk?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Musk says yes. OpenAI’s public evidence says Musk himself advocated a for-profit/C-corp structure as early as 2015 and in 2017 wanted OpenAI converted into a for-profit he would control.

### Q27. Did Musk know of, agree with, or advocate for a for-profit restructuring of OpenAI before he left?

**Why this is disputed:**  
This may not be a standalone liability question, but it is crucial to multiple claims because it bears on contract terms, fraud, reliance, waiver, and credibility. OpenAI’s emails strongly support “yes”; Musk’s case likely distinguishes between a mission-preserving structure and the Microsoft-linked structure that later emerged.

### Q28. Did defendants materially change OpenAI’s governance or structure in a way that subordinated the nonprofit mission to private commercial interests?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Musk says OpenAI became a de facto Microsoft subsidiary. Defendants say the nonprofit remained in control and the structure was designed to preserve the mission while attracting needed capital.

---

## 9. Microsoft influence / board-capture questions

### Q29. Did Microsoft obtain influence or control over OpenAI sufficient to cause OpenAI to act primarily for Microsoft’s commercial benefit rather than for humanity or OpenAI’s mission?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Musk relies on the exclusive license, Nadella’s statements, the observer seat, and the 2023 board crisis. Defendants will argue Microsoft lacked legal control over the nonprofit board and that cooperation with Microsoft was a financing/compute arrangement, not capture.

### Q30. Did the November 2023 board events result in a board that was less independent, less safety-focused, or less able to make an honest AGI determination?

**Why this is disputed:**  
The complaint says yes. Defendants likely contest the characterization and relevance, especially to Musk personally.

---

## 10. Antitrust questions
(These may be partly equitable or for the judge, but if they are submitted to a jury, these are the core issues suggested by the FTC statement.)

### Q31. Were OpenAI and Microsoft actual or potential competitors in a relevant antitrust market, such as the market for generative AI models or platforms?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Section 8 and Sherman Act theories require competition or threatened competition. That market definition and competitive relationship are contested.

### Q32. Did a prohibited interlocking directorate exist between OpenAI and Microsoft?

Possible subquestions:
- Did Reid Hoffman serve as a director of competing corporations at the same time?
- Did Deannah Templeton’s role as board observer/non-voting participant function as an interlock in substance?
- Did Microsoft appoint or use an agent/deputy to participate on OpenAI’s board?

**Why this is disputed:**  
The FTC filing says Section 8 applies broadly and is not defeated by formalisms. Defendants dispute whether any live violation exists and may dispute whether observers count or whether the companies were competitors.

### Q33. If an interlock existed, did it create a meaningful risk of coordination, exchange of competitively sensitive information, or other anticompetitive effects?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Under some theories actual effect may not be required for Section 8, but risk and purpose matter for other theories and remedies.

### Q34. Did defendants engage in a contract, combination, or conspiracy in unreasonable restraint of trade, including a group boycott or “fund-no-competitors” arrangement?

**Why this is disputed:**  
The FTC statement discusses the legal standard for this. Musk’s theory appears to be that OpenAI/Microsoft discouraged investment in rivals. Defendants dispute the facts and likely the existence of concerted action.

### Q35. Was any such restraint a substantial factor in harming competition and/or harming Musk or xAI?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Antitrust claims generally require causation and injury. Defendants likely argue Musk is a competitor with strategic motives and cannot show cognizable antitrust injury from the alleged conduct.

---

## 11. Credibility / knowledge / state-of-mind questions that cut across all claims

These may not appear as separate verdict-form questions, but jurors will effectively have to resolve them:

### Q36. Did Musk understand, before leaving OpenAI, that building AGI would require billions of dollars and probably some for-profit structure?

**Why this is disputed:**  
OpenAI’s emails strongly suggest yes. Musk’s claims depend in part on portraying the later structure as a betrayal rather than an anticipated evolution.

### Q37. Did Musk leave OpenAI because he opposed a mission betrayal, or because he failed to obtain control / preferred Tesla as the vehicle?

**Why this is disputed:**  
OpenAI’s published emails frame the split as a control dispute: Musk wanted majority equity, board control, and CEO status, or a Tesla merger. This bears heavily on motive and credibility.

### Q38. When defendants made the relevant statements in 2015–2018, were they sincere at the time, or did they secretly intend something different?

**Why this is disputed:**  
That question is central to fraud, promissory estoppel, and contract interpretation.

---

## 12. Remedies questions, if liability is found

If the jury reaches liability and is asked to decide remedies, it may need to answer:

### Q39. What amount of compensatory damages, if any, should be awarded to Musk?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Amount and measure of damages are contested.

### Q40. Did Musk prove by the required standard that punitive damages are warranted against any individual defendant?

**Why this is disputed:**  
That would depend on proof of fraud, malice, or oppression-type findings under applicable law.

### Q41. What amount of restitution or disgorgement, if any, is appropriate?

**Why this is disputed:**  
Musk seeks restitution/disgorgement, but defendants contest both liability and any monetary entitlement.

---

# Condensed version: the likely “big six” jury issues

If I had to reduce everything to the most important jury questions, they would be:

1. **Was there an enforceable founding agreement as Musk describes it?**  
2. **If yes, what exactly did it require?**  
3. **Did OpenAI/Altman/Brockman breach it?**  
4. **Did Musk reasonably rely on promises or misrepresentations in funding and building OpenAI?**  
5. **Did defendants wrongfully convert a mission-driven nonprofit enterprise into one serving private commercial interests, especially Microsoft’s?**  
6. **Did Musk prove personal legal injury and damages from any of that?**

And, if antitrust claims are before the jury:

7. **Did OpenAI and Microsoft maintain unlawful interlocking relationships or other anticompetitive coordination?**

---

If you want, I can do a second pass and sort these into:
- questions for the **jury**,
- questions likely for the **judge**,
- and questions that are more about **witness credibility** than formal verdict findings.

transcript.label: evidence_weighing
transcript.user: Second task. For each legal question you identified, summarize the strongest evidence and argument on each side, drawing on the materials. Be specific about which document or fact supports which claim. Where the materials are silent or contested, say so. Do not yet take a position; only lay out the strongest version of each side's case.
transcript.assistant:
Below, I take each question from the first task and summarize the strongest evidence and arguments for both sides, based only on the supplied materials plus basic legal framing. I do not resolve any of them.

I’ll group closely related questions together so this stays readable.

---

# 1. Existence of an enforceable “Founding Agreement”

## Q1. Did Musk prove an enforceable agreement that OpenAI would remain a nonprofit for humanity’s benefit and/or be open/open-source except for safety-based limits?

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **June 24, 2015 Altman email + Musk’s “Agree on all.”**  
  The complaint quotes Altman proposing:  
  - “The mission would be to create the first general AI and use it for individual empowerment...”  
  - “The technology would be owned by the foundation and used ‘for the good of the world’”  
  Musk replied: **“Agree on all.”**  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶51, ¶124(b)**.  
  This is Musk’s best direct evidence of assent to specific founding principles.

- **OpenAI’s certificate of incorporation.**  
  The 2015 certificate says:  
  - OpenAI is a nonprofit organized for charitable/educational purposes;  
  - “The resulting technology will benefit the public and the corporation will seek to open source technology for the public benefit when applicable”;  
  - “The corporation is not organized for the private gain of any person”;  
  - its property is “irrevocably dedicated” to those purposes.  
  Source: **complaint_initial Ex. 1 / pages 37–38; ¶25, ¶56, ¶124(a)**.  
  Musk’s argument is that this memorializes the founding bargain and shows the nonprofit/public-benefit aspect was not aspirational fluff.

- **Public launch statements in December 2015.**  
  OpenAI announced itself as a nonprofit whose goal was to benefit humanity “unconstrained by a need to generate financial return.”  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶57**.  
  Musk can argue this was a contemporaneous public reaffirmation of the founding deal.

- **Repeated later reaffirmations.**  
  The complaint says that after 2017 discussions about a for-profit, Altman wrote: **“I remain enthusiastic about the non-profit structure!”**  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶63–64**.  
  Musk can argue this shows the founders reaffirmed the original arrangement after considering alternatives.

- **2018 Charter language.**  
  The OpenAI Charter says OpenAI’s mission is to ensure AGI benefits all humanity; “Our primary fiduciary duty is to humanity”; OpenAI will avoid enabling uses that unduly concentrate power; it will publish most research today though safety/security may later reduce traditional publishing.  
  Source: **openai_charter**; also **complaint_initial ¶66–67, ¶75**.  
  Musk’s use of this is twofold: it confirms the humanity-first mission, and it shows the nonprofit board was meant to guard against exactly the later profit shift he complains of.

- **Musk’s funding and work as consideration.**  
  The complaint alleges Musk contributed more than **$44 million**, provided recruiting, office space, advice, and personal prestige that helped OpenAI launch, including recruiting Ilya Sutskever.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶26, ¶58–62**.  
  Musk’s theory is that these were not detached charitable gifts but consideration exchanged for those founding commitments.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- **No formal contract document in the record.**  
  The materials show mission-oriented emails and corporate documents, but no signed bilateral contract giving Musk personal contractual rights. Defendants can argue these are statements of mission and governance, not an enforceable donor contract.

- **The incorporation language is qualified, not absolute.**  
  The certificate says OpenAI will “seek to open source technology for the public benefit **when applicable**,” not always.  
  Source: **complaint_initial Ex. 1**.  
  Defendants can argue this undercuts Musk’s claim of an absolute open-source obligation.

- **The Charter expressly anticipates reduced publication for safety/security reasons.**  
  It says: “Today this includes publishing most of our AI research, but we expect that safety and security concerns will reduce our traditional publishing in the future...”  
  Source: **openai_charter**.  
  Defendants can say the governing mission documents themselves reject Musk’s absolutist reading.

- **OpenAI’s released emails suggest Musk did not understand “nonprofit forever” to be binding.**  
  In November 2015, Musk wrote:  
  - “the structure doesn’t seem optimal”  
  - “Probably better to have a standard C corp with a parallel nonprofit.”  
  Source: **openai_blog_for_profit**.  
  This is powerful evidence that even at inception Musk did not view a nonprofit-only structure as sacred or fixed.

- **OpenAI’s released emails suggest Musk later agreed a for-profit was necessary.**  
  Multiple 2017 emails show OpenAI and Musk discussing that a for-profit would be “the next step,” with Musk saying after the Dota success, “Time to make the next step for OpenAI. This is the triggering event.”  
  Source: **openai_blog_for_profit**.  
  Defendants can argue this is inconsistent with any enforceable promise of perpetual nonprofit status.

- **Musk himself acknowledged less openness near AGI.**  
  OpenAI published an exchange where Ilya wrote:  
  - “As we get closer to building AI, it will make sense to start being less open...”  
  Musk replied: **“Yup.”**  
  Source: **openai_blog_response**.  
  Defendants can use this as a direct admission against Musk’s “always-open” theory.

- **Musk’s donations were charitable donations, not purchase of contractual rights.**  
  Defendants can argue that as a donor to a nonprofit without stock or ownership, Musk cannot convert broad mission statements into a personal contract right to dictate future strategy.

---

# 2. Who were the parties to any such agreement?

## Q2. If such an agreement existed, who were the parties?

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **Direct founder communications involve Musk, Altman, and Brockman.**  
  The founding discussions were among the founders personally.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶50–55**.  
  Musk can argue Altman and Brockman personally made promises to him before and during formation.

- **OpenAI, Inc. embodied the agreement through incorporation and launch statements.**  
  The nonprofit itself was formed around the agreed mission and publicly represented that mission.  
  Source: **complaint_initial Ex. 1; ¶56–57**.  
  Thus OpenAI, Inc. can be argued to have adopted and become bound by the founding commitments.

- **Later entities were allegedly instrumentalities or successors used to carry out the same enterprise.**  
  The complaint alleges a complex web of later OpenAI entities formed to implement the shift to for-profit operations.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶70–76**.  
  Musk can argue they are liable because they are continuations or beneficiaries of the original enterprise and its assets.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- **No evidence the later entities were parties to a 2015 agreement.**  
  Later LLCs/LPs did not exist when the founding discussions occurred.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶70–74**.  
  Defendants can say they cannot be liable for promises they never made.

- **Founder emails do not necessarily create personal obligations of Altman or Brockman distinct from OpenAI’s organizational mission.**  
  Defendants can argue the discussions were about forming an organization, not about personal contractual guaranties by the individuals.

- **The nonprofit charter is a corporate governance document, not a private contract with Musk.**  
  Even if OpenAI, Inc. adopted certain purposes, that does not automatically mean every affiliate or executive personally undertook enforceable obligations to Musk.

---

# 3. Consideration

## Q3. Did Musk give consideration in exchange for the alleged promises?

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **Large financial contributions.**  
  Complaint alleges Musk donated over **$44 million** between 2016 and 2020.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶26, ¶61, ¶68**.

- **Non-monetary contributions.**  
  - recruiting top talent, especially Ilya Sutskever;  
  - compensation advice;  
  - leased and paid office space through Musk Industries;  
  - visibility, clout, personal involvement.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶58–62**.  
  Musk can argue this was substantial bargained-for support.

- **Complaint explicitly pleads exchange.**  
  It says Musk contributed money and support “in exchange for and as consideration for the Founding Agreement.”  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶124**.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- **Donations to a nonprofit are ordinarily gifts or charitable contributions, not contract consideration creating personal performance rights.**  
  The materials do not show a donation agreement conditioning funds on legally enforceable donor-specific terms.

- **OpenAI disputes the funding narrative.**  
  OpenAI says Musk suggested publicly announcing a $1B commitment but in fact contributed **less than $45M**, while others gave more than $90M.  
  Source: **openai_blog_response**.  
  This doesn’t negate consideration, but it weakens the notion that he uniquely “bought” the project.

- **Musk’s support may have been motivated by his own mission and strategic views, not by bargained-for reciprocal promises.**  
  Defendants can argue he acted as a founder and donor, not as a counterparty purchasing compliance rights.

---

# 4. Terms of the agreement

## Q4. What were the actual terms: permanent nonprofit? absolute openness? no for-profit? no Microsoft commercial arrangement?

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **Nonprofit/public-benefit language is strong and repeated.**  
  - “not organized for the private gain of any person”  
  - “irrevocably dedicated”  
  - “benefit humanity”  
  - “free from financial obligations”  
  Sources: **certificate, complaint ¶25, ¶56–57**.  
  Musk can say the core term was anti-private-profit orientation.

- **Founding name “OpenAI” and open release history.**  
  The complaint says Musk named it “OpenAI” and that OpenAI’s early releases of GPT, GPT-2, GPT-3 included code or detailed papers.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶24, ¶54, ¶79–83**.  
  He can argue this confirms the intended openness term.

- **Microsoft was only licensed pre-AGI technology.**  
  The complaint emphasizes that Microsoft’s 2020 license excluded AGI, and AGI status was to be determined by the nonprofit board.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶28, ¶69, ¶112**.  
  Musk’s argument is that even if some commercialization of pre-AGI was allowed, AGI itself could not be privately appropriated.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- **Openness was always conditional, not absolute.**  
  - Certificate: “seek to open source ... when applicable.”  
  - Charter: safety/security may reduce publishing.  
  - Ilya-to-Musk exchange: less openness may make sense as AI gets closer. Musk said “Yup.”  
  Sources: **certificate, openai_charter, openai_blog_response**.

- **Nonprofit mission did not necessarily bar a for-profit subsidiary or revenue generation.**  
  OpenAI announced in 2019 the capped-profit LP specifically to advance the nonprofit mission; investors were warned the mission took precedence and should view investment “in the spirit of a donation.”  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶67; openai_blog_for_profit**.  
  Defendants can say commercialization as a means to mission was not excluded.

- **Musk himself advocated a standard C corp + parallel nonprofit, and later a for-profit next step.**  
  Source: **openai_blog_for_profit**.  
  This strongly supports defendants’ argument that any founding terms did not prohibit a later for-profit structure.

---

# 5. Breach

## Q5. Did defendants breach the agreement?

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **Closed GPT-4.**  
  The complaint stresses OpenAI stopped publishing details and kept GPT-4 architecture/training secret, with “press releases bragging about performance” rather than scientific disclosure.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶30, ¶90**.  
  Musk says this is the opposite of OpenAI’s founding promise.

- **Exclusive Microsoft license / de facto proprietary control.**  
  Complaint alleges GPT-4 became a “de facto Microsoft proprietary algorithm” integrated into Microsoft’s products.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶30, ¶32, ¶125(a)**.

- **Shift to profit / board capture / Microsoft influence.**  
  Musk alleges OpenAI became a “closed-source de facto subsidiary” of Microsoft after 2023, with a less independent board and a Microsoft observer seat.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶31–32, ¶109–112, ¶121–122**.

- **AGI allegedly appropriated for private gain.**  
  If GPT-4 qualifies as AGI, Musk argues licensing it commercially to Microsoft was itself a direct breach.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶31, ¶87–90, prayer at ¶B–C**.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- **No breach because no such absolute agreement existed.**  
  If the contract terms were mission-based and flexible, evolving structure and reduced openness are not breach.

- **The move to for-profit/capped-profit was mission-preserving, not mission-abandoning.**  
  OpenAI’s public materials say they realized AGI would require billions per year and a for-profit entity was necessary to pursue the mission.  
  Source: **openai_blog_response; openai_blog_for_profit**.

- **Musk knew and agreed the nonprofit alone could not raise the needed capital.**  
  OpenAI cites:
  - Musk’s 2015 C-corp comment;  
  - 2017 agreement that for-profit was next step;  
  - 2018 statement that OpenAI needed “billions per year immediately or forget it.”  
  Sources: **openai_blog_for_profit; openai_blog_response**.  
  Defendants can say the challenged conduct was exactly the sort of restructuring Musk understood to be necessary.

- **Reduced openness was justified by safety and anticipated from early on.**  
  The “Yup” email and Charter language directly support that.

- **Microsoft commercialization of pre-AGI technology was already public in 2020 and Musk did not sue then.**  
  Complaint acknowledges the 2020 Microsoft license; Musk’s grievance focuses on later alleged AGI and control issues.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶28, ¶69**.  
  Defendants can argue the mere existence of commercialization was not forbidden.

---

# 6. Damages from breach

## Q6. Was Musk harmed by any breach, and what damages did he prove?

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **He gave $44M+ and substantial noncash support based on the founding commitments.**  
  If OpenAI turned into the opposite of what he funded, he can argue he personally suffered economic loss and is entitled to restitution or expectation-type relief.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶26, ¶61, ¶126–127**.

- **He also alleges harm to intended third-party beneficiaries, i.e., the public.**  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶132, ¶135**.  
  This supports equitable relief even if personal damages are hard to quantify.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- **Personal damages are speculative.**  
  Musk donated to a nonprofit; he did not purchase equity or a reversionary interest. The complaint itself says he requested no financial interest in the for-profit arm.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶68; openai_blog_for_profit**.

- **Even if mission changed, the measure of personal damages is unclear.**  
  The materials do not show a contractual right to repayment or ownership.

- **Some requested relief, like declaring GPT-4 AGI or ordering governance changes, looks equitable and institutional rather than compensatory.**  
  That may underscore the weakness of a traditional damages theory.

---

# 7. Promissory estoppel

## Q7–Q10. Clear promise, expected reliance, actual reasonable reliance, and injustice absent relief

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **Clear promise evidence overlaps with contract evidence.**  
  - “Agree on all.”  
  - Certificate language.  
  - launch statements;  
  - later reaffirmations of nonprofit structure.  
  Sources: **complaint_initial ¶51, ¶56–57, ¶63–67**.

- **Defendants plainly expected reliance.**  
  Musk was recruited as a founder and donor; OpenAI knew it needed his money, stature, and recruitment help.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶50–55, ¶58–62, ¶129–130**.

- **Actual reliance is strong factually.**  
  He did donate, recruit, advise, and support OpenAI for years.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶26, ¶58–62, ¶68**.

- **Injustice argument.**  
  Musk frames this as donors funding a nonprofit mission that was later converted into private commercial advantage for insiders and Microsoft.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶113–114, ¶131–132**.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- **No clear and definite promise.**  
  The statements are broad mission statements with qualifiers, especially around openness and safety. That undercuts promissory estoppel.

- **Reliance was not reasonable because Musk knew too much.**  
  This is probably defendants’ strongest point:
  - he questioned nonprofit structure in 2015;  
  - he agreed in 2017 that for-profit was next step;  
  - he pushed for dramatic scaling and billions/year;  
  - he proposed Tesla merger or his own control.  
  Sources: **openai_blog_for_profit; openai_blog_response**.  
  A sophisticated founder with this knowledge may have trouble proving reasonable reliance on “nonprofit forever” or “open forever.”

- **Any injustice is muted by Musk’s later conduct.**  
  OpenAI says he left, said their success chance was 0, later founded xAI, and now sues a competitor.  
  Source: **openai_blog_response; openai_blog_for_profit**.  
  Defendants can argue the suit is competitive opportunism, not equitable reliance protection.

---

# 8. Fraud / misrepresentation

## Q11–Q15. False statement, knowledge, intent to induce reliance, justifiable reliance, damages

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **The alleged misrepresentation:** OpenAI and its founders represented that OpenAI would be a nonprofit building AGI for humanity, not a profit-maximizing proprietary arm aligned with a tech giant.  
  Sources: **complaint_initial ¶24–25, ¶57, ¶66–69**.

- **Falsity inferred from later conduct.**  
  Musk will say the gap between original representations and later reality is so large that the original statements were misleading or false, especially if defendants already intended to commercialize/close off the technology.

- **Intent to induce reliance.**  
  Founders needed Musk’s money, recruiting power, and prestige.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶129–130**.

- **Damage.**  
  His donations and support were allegedly procured by these representations.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- **No evidence in the supplied materials that the founders secretly intended fraud in 2015.**  
  The strongest defense is that whatever happened later, the materials do not show contemporaneous intent to deceive at formation. Fraud requires that.

- **The world changed; compute/capital demands changed.**  
  OpenAI’s 2017 compute emails detail why the economics changed.  
  Source: **openai_blog_for_profit**.  
  Defendants can say the mission stayed the same while means changed.

- **Musk cannot show justifiable reliance because he knew the opposite facts.**  
  His own emails advocating a C-corp or for-profit and his acceptance of reduced openness are potent anti-fraud evidence.  
  Sources: **openai_blog_for_profit; openai_blog_response**.

- **The materials suggest real disagreement, not deception.**  
  The 2017 breakdown appears to be a visible negotiation over control/structure, not a hidden plot.  
  Source: **openai_blog_for_profit**.

---

# 9. Unjust enrichment / restitution

## Q16–Q17. Did defendants receive benefits from Musk under unjust circumstances, and in what amount?

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **Benefits received are concrete.**  
  Money, office rent, founder credibility, recruiting, strategic guidance.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶58–62**.

- **Unjustness theory.**  
  If the organization later used donor-funded assets to enrich insiders or Microsoft contrary to the founding purposes, it would be unjust to retain the benefits without restitution.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶113–114, ¶147–148**.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- **Benefits were given voluntarily to advance OpenAI’s mission and were used for that mission.**  
  Defendants can say the later commercialization was part of pursuing safe AGI, not a bait-and-switch.

- **Musk expressly declined equity / financial interest.**  
  OpenAI says he was offered equity in the LP and declined; also his only 2019 feedback on the LP announcement was to be clear he had no financial interest.  
  Source: **openai_blog_for_profit; complaint_initial ¶68**.  
  That weakens any claim that it is unjust for defendants to retain enterprise value without compensating him.

---

# 10. Fiduciary duty / use of charitable donations

## Q18–Q20. Did defendants owe Musk a fiduciary duty regarding his donations, breach it, and harm him?

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **Nonprofit solicitors/directors using donations for a different purpose can sound in fiduciary obligation.**  
  The complaint cites California law requiring use of contributions for promised purposes.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶133–136, especially ¶134**.

- **The certificate’s “irrevocably dedicated” language.**  
  Musk can argue this strengthens the notion that donated assets were locked to public-benefit purposes and could not lawfully be diverted for private gain.  
  Source: **certificate / complaint ¶25, ¶56**.

- **Board’s fiduciary duty to humanity.**  
  Complaint and Charter both stress the nonprofit board’s duty to humanity, not investors.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶67, ¶75, ¶105; openai_charter**.  
  Musk says the 2023 restructuring betrayed that fiduciary design.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- **Personal fiduciary duty to Musk is legally debatable and not strongly shown by the materials.**  
  The materials show duties of the nonprofit and its board, but not clearly a direct fiduciary duty running to Musk personally as donor/founder.

- **Mission-based structuring and fundraising were disclosed.**  
  The LP was announced publicly with strong mission warnings to investors.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶67**.  
  Defendants can argue there was no covert diversion.

- **OpenAI says its mission remained intact.**  
  The for-profit arm existed to advance the nonprofit mission and remained governed by it.  
  Source: **openai_blog_for_profit; openai_charter**.

---

# 11. Whether GPT-4 was “AGI”

## Q21–Q23. Was GPT-4 AGI, and if so did defendants improperly treat it as pre-AGI or avoid making that determination?

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **OpenAI’s own published AGI definition.**  
  Charter defines AGI as “highly autonomous systems that outperform humans at most economically valuable work.”  
  Source: **openai_charter**.

- **GPT-4’s broad benchmark performance.**  
  Complaint alleges:
  - 90th percentile bar exam  
  - 99th percentile GRE verbal  
  - 77% Advanced Sommelier exam  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶30, ¶86**.  
  Musk says this shows broad superhuman or near-superhuman economically valuable capability.

- **Microsoft’s “Sparks of AGI” paper.**  
  Complaint quotes Microsoft researchers saying GPT-4 can “reasonably be viewed as an early (yet still incomplete) version of an artificial general intelligence (AGI) system” and that it “attains a form of general intelligence.”  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶31, ¶87–89**.  
  This is Musk’s strongest external support.

- **Q* allegations and 2023 board upheaval.**  
  Complaint suggests some board tensions were linked to safety concerns and breakthroughs like Q*.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶31, ¶92, ¶98**.  
  Musk can argue this suggests insiders understood they were close to or had crossed an AGI threshold.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- **The definition is demanding and GPT-4 may not meet it.**  
  “Outperform humans at most economically valuable work” is an extremely broad standard. The materials do not establish that GPT-4 met it comprehensively rather than on selected tests.

- **The Microsoft paper itself called GPT-4 “early” and “incomplete.”**  
  Musk emphasizes the AGI phrase; defendants would emphasize the qualifiers.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶31, ¶89**.

- **Board, not Musk or Microsoft researchers, determines AGI for OpenAI governance purposes.**  
  Complaint itself says OpenAI’s board determines when AGI is attained.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶28, ¶69, ¶112**.  
  Defendants can argue no governing determination was made.

- **The supplied materials contain no internal OpenAI admission that GPT-4 was AGI.**  
  So Musk’s evidence is inferential and contested.

---

# 12. Whether secrecy and non-release violated OpenAI’s obligations

## Q24–Q25. Did OpenAI wrongfully keep GPT-4 closed primarily for commercial reasons rather than safety?

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **Sharp break from prior practice.**  
  GPT, GPT-2, and GPT-3 had code or detailed technical papers. GPT-4 allegedly did not.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶79–83, ¶90**.

- **Commercial motive evidenced by Microsoft integration.**  
  Musk argues secrecy was driven by the desire to preserve Microsoft’s proprietary commercial advantage, not by safety.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶30, ¶90, ¶125(b)**.

- **OpenAI’s name and public identity emphasized openness.**  
  He can argue abandoning this once the technology became valuable shows the real motive.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- **Direct evidence that reduced openness was contemplated for safety reasons.**  
  Best defense evidence:
  - Ilya: “As we get closer to building AI, it will make sense to start being less open...”  
  - Musk: “Yup.”  
  Source: **openai_blog_response**.

- **The Charter expressly says future safety/security concerns will reduce traditional publishing.**  
  Source: **openai_charter**.  
  This gives a contemporaneous, non-commercial explanation.

- **Materials do not conclusively prove commercial motive was primary.**  
  Musk alleges it; defendants can say the record supplied here does not show internal commercial-only reasoning.

---

# 13. Whether the for-profit transition itself violated a promise

## Q26–Q28. Did creating a for-profit/capped-profit structure violate obligations to Musk, and did Musk know or advocate it?

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **The original nonprofit/public-benefit framing was central and “irrevocable.”**  
  Source: **certificate; complaint ¶25, ¶56, ¶113–114**.
