  Musk argues a later for-profit arm tied to massive outside investment and Microsoft power is not what he agreed to.

- **The 2019 LP may be distinguishable from what later happened.**  
  Musk can concede some structure change while arguing the later reality—closed-source, Microsoft-dependent, allegedly AGI-linked commercialization—went beyond any acceptable evolution.

- **2017 discussions broke down because Musk rejected a for-profit inconsistent with mission?**  
  The complaint portrays Musk as insisting on remaining nonprofit after discussions.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶63–64**.  
  He can rely on Altman’s “I remain enthusiastic about the non-profit structure!”

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
This is one of defendants’ strongest clusters of evidence.

- **2015 Musk email:**  
  “Probably better to have a standard C corp with a parallel nonprofit.”  
  Source: **openai_blog_for_profit**.

- **July 2017 evidence that Musk, Greg, and Ilya agreed nonprofit may no longer be right.**  
  Greg message recap says Elon said nonprofit was “def the right one early on, may not be the right one now.”  
  Source: **openai_blog_for_profit**.

- **July/August 2017 emails explicitly discussing a for-profit path.**  
  Greg: path must be “AI research + hardware for-profit (starting 2018).”  
  Musk: “Let’s talk Sat or Sun. I have a tentative game plan...”  
  Later Musk: “Time to make the next step for OpenAI. This is the triggering event.”  
  Source: **openai_blog_for_profit**.

- **Musk formed a public benefit corporation for the proposed future OpenAI structure in September 2017.**  
  “Open Artificial Intelligence Technologies, Inc.”  
  Source: **openai_blog_for_profit**.  
  That is unusually strong evidence he embraced a for-profit/public-benefit transition.

- **Musk later said OpenAI needed billions/year and that several hundred million would not be enough.**  
  Source: **openai_blog_response; openai_blog_for_profit**.  
  Defendants can argue the economics made the transition not only foreseeable but necessary, and Musk knew it.

---

# 14. Whether Microsoft obtained improper influence/control

## Q29–Q30. Did Microsoft gain influence or control causing OpenAI to act for Microsoft’s benefit rather than its mission?

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **Microsoft’s exclusive license and commercial integration.**  
  GPT-3 and later models were tied to Microsoft. Complaint alleges GPT-4 became de facto Microsoft proprietary tech.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶28, ¶30–32**.

- **Satya Nadella’s statements after Altman’s firing.**  
  Complaint quotes Nadella saying if OpenAI disappeared, Microsoft had “all the IP rights and all the capability” and “We are below them, above them, around them.”  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶32, ¶102, ¶107**.  
  Musk can argue this sounds like practical control.

- **Microsoft observer seat and financial leverage.**  
  Complaint alleges Microsoft got a board observer seat and had unpaid portions of its $10B commitment plus cloud leverage over OpenAI.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶108, ¶110–112**.

- **Board reconstitution after November 2023.**  
  Musk says the new board lacked AI governance expertise and was more Altman/Microsoft friendly.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶109–112**.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- **The materials do not prove formal legal control by Microsoft.**  
  Nadella’s boasts may show leverage or partnership rhetoric, not legal domination.

- **OpenAI’s structure remained nonprofit-controlled on paper.**  
  Complaint itself alleges OpenAI GP was wholly owned by OpenAI, Inc. and controlled by the nonprofit board.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶75**.  
  Defendants can argue the governance design remained mission-first.

- **Commercial dependence is not the same as capture.**  
  Defendants can say heavy funding and cloud support were necessary to compete and do not alone prove subordination of mission.

---

# 15. Antitrust: competition between OpenAI and Microsoft

## Q31. Were OpenAI and Microsoft actual or potential competitors in a relevant market?

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **Complaint alleges a market for generative AI models/platforms.**  
  Source inferred from **FTC statement** summarizing the amended complaint.  
  If Microsoft builds and sells AI systems and OpenAI does too, they may be competitors even if also partners.

- **Nadella’s statements suggest overlapping capability.**  
  Microsoft had people, compute, data, and rights to continue innovation itself.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶102**.  
  Musk can argue that means Microsoft and OpenAI occupy overlapping spaces.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- **The supplied materials do not detail a rigorous market definition or competitive overlap.**  
  They may be better characterized as partners, investor-licensee, and cloud/commercial ally rather than horizontal competitors.

- **Whether they are “competitors” for Section 8 is fact-specific and underdeveloped in the materials.**  
  The FTC explicitly took no position on the facts.  
  Source: **ftc_statement_of_interest**.

---

# 16. Antitrust: interlocking directorates

## Q32–Q33. Did prohibited interlocks exist, and did they create risk of coordination or information exchange?

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **Alleged overlapping roles.**  
  FTC statement says the amended complaint alleges:
  - Reid Hoffman served on OpenAI and Microsoft boards from March 2017 to March 2023;  
  - Deannah Templeton served as Microsoft VP and non-voting OpenAI board member/observer from Nov. 2023 to July 2024.  
  Source: **ftc_statement_of_interest at pages 6–7 equivalent / factual background**.

- **FTC/DOJ legal position supports broad reading of interlocks.**  
  Their brief says Section 8 is not mooted simply by unwinding an interlock and can reach indirect service via agents, deputies, or board observers.  
  Source: **ftc_statement_of_interest**.  
  This is strong legal support for Musk’s theory.

- **Risk rationale.**  
  The Agencies emphasize that interlocks create opportunity for coordination and exchange of competitively sensitive information.  
  Source: **ftc_statement_of_interest**.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- **Current mootness argument.**  
  Defendants argued neither Hoffman nor Templeton was currently affiliated with OpenAI’s board.  
  Source summarized in **ftc_statement_of_interest**.  
  They can argue no live interlock exists now, though the FTC brief pushes back legally.

- **Competitor status is unresolved.**  
  Without proof OpenAI and Microsoft are competitors in the relevant sense, Section 8 may not apply.

- **Observer/non-voting roles may not count absent factual proof of real board service.**  
  FTC says substance controls, but facts matter. The supplied materials do not show exactly what Templeton did.

---

# 17. Antitrust: group boycott / “fund-no-competitors”

## Q34–Q35. Did defendants engage in concerted action or boycott behavior harming competition?

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **FTC filing says such a theory is legally viable.**  
  The Agencies explain that group boycotts can violate Section 1 even if organized through a hub-and-spoke structure and even if only one rival is targeted.  
  Source: **ftc_statement_of_interest, Section III**.

- **If Musk can prove OpenAI/Microsoft discouraged funding for rivals or imposed “fund-no-competitors” terms, the law could support him.**  
  The brief exists because that issue was in the PI motion.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- **The supplied materials contain almost no factual evidence of an actual boycott agreement.**  
  The FTC expressly takes no position on facts.  
  So on this record, the legal theory is developed but the fact support in the provided materials is thin.

- **Alternative business explanations may exist for investor behavior or exclusivity.**  
  The materials do not establish coercion or coordinated refusal to deal.

---

# 18. Knowledge, intent, and credibility questions

## Q36. Did Musk understand before leaving that AGI would require billions and likely a for-profit structure?

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **He may argue knowing more capital was needed is not the same as agreeing to a Microsoft-linked, closed, profit-driven structure.**  
  The complaint can be read this way: structure change may have been contemplated in some form, but not abandonment of the founding mission.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- This is another very strong defense area.
- **2015:** better to have “standard C corp with a parallel nonprofit.”  
- **2017:** least expensive way to ensure compute power is not a constraint; agreed for-profit was next step.  
- **2018:** “This needs billions per year immediately or forget it.”  
  Sources: **openai_blog_for_profit; openai_blog_response**.  
  Defendants can argue Musk fully understood the necessity of massive capital and corporate restructuring.

## Q37. Did Musk leave because of mission concerns or because he failed to obtain control / preferred Tesla?

### Musk’s strongest evidence and argument
- **Complaint version:** Musk opposed OpenAI deviating from nonprofit mission.  
  Source: **complaint_initial ¶63–64**.
- He can argue his concerns about AGI concentration were genuine and longstanding.

### Defendants’ strongest evidence and argument
- **OpenAI’s documentary record says Musk wanted majority equity, initial board control, and CEO status, or a Tesla merger.**  
  Source:

transcript.label: verdict
transcript.user: Third and final task. For each legal question you identified, render your verdict. State which side you find more persuasive, and why. Indicate your confidence on a 1â€“5 scale (1 = essentially a coin flip, 5 = highly confident). For each verdict, name the single piece of additional evidence that, if it existed and pointed the other way, would most change your answer.
transcript.assistant:
Below is my juror-style assessment of each question from the first task, using only the materials you provided and ordinary background legal reasoning. I treat this as a pre-deliberation verdict analysis, not as legal advice. I’ll be explicit where I think an issue likely belongs more to the judge than the jury, but I’ll still say which side I find more persuasive on the present record.

Confidence scale:
- **1** = coin flip
- **2** = slight lean
- **3** = moderate lean
- **4** = strong lean
- **5** = highly confident

---

# 1. Existence of an enforceable “Founding Agreement”

## Q1. Was there an enforceable agreement that OpenAI would remain a nonprofit for humanity’s benefit and/or be open/open-source except for safety-based limits?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
I think Musk proved there was a real founding understanding about mission: benefit humanity, avoid private capture, and be relatively open. But I do **not** think the materials show an enforceable contract with the specific hard terms Musk needs—especially:
- **nonprofit forever**, and
- **open-source / public release as an absolute or near-absolute legal obligation**.

The biggest problem for Musk is his own documentary record:
- In **November 2015**, he said a **“standard C corp with a parallel nonprofit”** might be better.
- In **2017**, OpenAI’s documents show him agreeing that a for-profit was likely the next step.
- He also replied **“Yup”** to Ilya’s statement that greater capability might justify **being less open**.

Those records make it very hard for me to find that the enforceable agreement had the rigid terms Musk alleges.

At most, I see a broad mission compact, not a binding contract with the precise prohibitions he wants enforced.

**Confidence:** **4**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
A signed 2015–2016 founder/donor agreement stating in substance: **“OpenAI shall remain a nonprofit and shall not transfer or commercialize AGI or core research for private-profit purposes, except as approved by Musk”** or similar.

---

## Q2. If such an agreement existed, who were the parties?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive as to later entities; Musk more persuasive as to OpenAI, Inc. and maybe Altman/Brockman personally. Overall: Defendants.**

**Why:**  
Even if there was some enforceable founding agreement, I do **not** find it persuasive that all the later LLCs/LPs were parties to a 2015 founder understanding. Those entities didn’t exist yet. The strongest possible parties would be:
- **OpenAI, Inc.**
- possibly **Altman**
- possibly **Brockman**

But the complaint sweeps much more broadly. On that broader question, defendants are more persuasive.

**Confidence:** **4**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
A board resolution, intercompany agreement, or restructuring document expressly stating that the later entities **assumed and were bound by** the 2015 founding commitments to Musk.

---

## Q3. Did Musk provide consideration for the alleged promises?

**Verdict:** **Musk more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
This is one of Musk’s better points factually. He clearly contributed:
- over **$44 million**,
- recruiting help,
- office support,
- founder prestige,
- strategic input.

That is substantial value. The harder issue is whether it created contractual rights, but on the narrower question of whether he gave something of value in relation to the arrangement, yes, I think he did.

**Confidence:** **4**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Clear donation paperwork showing each contribution was expressly an **unrestricted charitable gift** with no conditions or bargained-for obligations.

---

# 2. Terms of the alleged agreement

## Q4. What were the actual terms—permanent nonprofit? absolute openness? no for-profit? no Microsoft commercialization?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
The best reading of the materials is:
- OpenAI was founded with a **humanity-first, nonprofit mission**;
- it was initially intended to be relatively open;
- but openness was **always qualified by safety**; and
- the structure was **not fixed forever**, because Musk himself discussed and later pushed for a for-profit format.

The phrase **“seek to open source technology … when applicable”** is not absolute. The Charter’s statement that publication may decrease for safety/security reasons is directly against Musk’s strongest reading.

So I do not find the terms Musk needs:
- no for-profit transition,
- no proprietary licensing,
- no reduced disclosure.

**Confidence:** **5**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
A contemporaneous memo or email from Altman/Brockman to Musk stating: **“No matter what capital demands arise, OpenAI will never adopt a for-profit structure or proprietary closed model.”**

---

# 3. Breach

## Q5. Did defendants breach any enforceable agreement with Musk?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
Because I do not think Musk proved the rigid contract terms he alleges, I also do not think he proved breach of those terms.

That said, if the question were moral rather than legal, Musk has a stronger argument: OpenAI does look less “open” and more commercially entangled than its 2015 public image suggested. But legally, the documents show:
- the mission language was flexible,
- reduced openness was contemplated,
- for-profit transition was contemplated,
- and Musk knew both.

So the conduct may be a sharp **evolution** or even a **departure in spirit**, but I am not persuaded it was a breach of a proven enforceable promise to Musk.

**Confidence:** **4**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Internal OpenAI communications from 2015–2016 saying: **“We know we promised Musk nonprofit/open-source permanence, but we can later route the assets into a commercial structure.”**

---

## Q6. Was Musk harmed by any breach, and what damages did he prove?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
Even assuming some breach, Musk’s personal damages case seems weak. He was a donor/founder, not an equity holder. He even appears to have wanted clarity that he had **no financial interest** in the for-profit arm. That makes traditional damages difficult to prove.

He may have a stronger **restitutionary** or **equitable** grievance than a damages claim, but on straightforward personal damage proof, I find defendants more persuasive.

**Confidence:** **4**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
A written funding agreement giving Musk an explicit **repayment, reversion, or enforcement right** if OpenAI deviated from specified nonprofit/open-access commitments.

---

# 4. Promissory estoppel

## Q7. Did defendants make a clear and definite promise to Musk of nonprofit permanence and/or public openness except for safety?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
The promises were too qualified and mission-oriented for me to call them “clear and definite” in the promissory-estoppel sense Musk needs. “Benefit humanity” is clear as mission, but not as a specific operational promise. “Seek to open source … when applicable” is explicitly conditional. And the Charter later reinforces flexibility.

**Confidence:** **4**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
An email from Altman or Brockman saying: **“You can rely on this: OpenAI will not become a for-profit and will not keep its core technology closed for commercial reasons.”**

---

## Q8. Did defendants reasonably expect Musk to rely on those promises?

**Verdict:** **Musk more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
Yes. OpenAI plainly wanted Musk’s:
- money,
- name,
- recruiting ability,
- institutional backing.

The founders surely expected him to rely on their stated mission and structure in deciding whether to join and fund the project.

**Confidence:** **4**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Evidence that Musk told them from the outset he was donating **regardless of structure or openness**, purely because he wanted a Google counterweight.

---

## Q9. Did Musk in fact reasonably rely on those promises?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
I think he relied in fact on OpenAI’s mission. But I do **not** think he proved **reasonable reliance** on the more rigid promises he asserts now.

His own emails undercut reasonable reliance:
- C-corp + parallel nonprofit in 2015,
- for-profit next step in 2017,
- less openness near AGI accepted with “Yup,”
- repeated recognition that billions/year would be needed.

A sophisticated founder who knew all that cannot, in my view, reasonably claim he believed OpenAI was locked into a pure nonprofit/open model forever.

**Confidence:** **5**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Proof that after all those discussions, defendants expressly reassured Musk in writing: **“Despite exploring options, you should still rely on our commitment that OpenAI will remain nonprofit and open.”**

---

## Q10. Can injustice be avoided only by enforcing the alleged promise or awarding relief?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
There is some intuitive unfairness in donors helping create “OpenAI” and later seeing a much less open, much more commercial organization. But the equitable case is weakened by:
- Musk’s sophistication,
- his own support for for-profit options,
- his departure after a control conflict,
- and his later status as a competitor through xAI.

So I do not think this is the kind of exceptional reliance case where injustice clearly requires estoppel relief.

**Confidence:** **3**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Proof that Musk imposed clear donor restrictions that defendants knowingly accepted and later ignored.

---

# 5. Fraud / misrepresentation

## Q11. Did defendants make a false representation of material fact to Musk about OpenAI’s mission, structure, openness, or intended use of his contributions?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
I think defendants made idealistic mission statements and maybe statements that later became inaccurate. But fraud requires a false representation **when made**. The supplied materials do not persuade me that the 2015–2018 statements were false at the time. They look more like sincere founding intentions later modified by economics and internal conflict.

**Confidence:** **4**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
A contemporaneous internal message saying: **“Tell Musk it will be nonprofit/open, but we know we’ll commercialize it once it works.”**

---

## Q12. At the time of any such representation, did the speaker know it was false or act recklessly?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
This is where Musk’s fraud case most seriously weakens. I do not see convincing evidence of contemporaneous fraudulent intent. The 2017 compute/funding emails actually support the opposite story: they show evolving realization that the original structure might not be viable.

**Confidence:** **5**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Any 2015–2016 internal memo explicitly planning a later bait-and-switch from nonprofit/open positioning to private commercial capture.

---

## Q13. Did defendants intend that Musk rely on the alleged misrepresentation?

**Verdict:** **Musk slightly more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
If misrepresentations existed, yes, they were plainly made in a setting where Musk’s reliance mattered. But because I do not find actual fraud clearly proved, this answer is only slight.

**Confidence:** **2**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Evidence showing the representations were made in purely public/aspirational contexts, not in fundraising or founder-recruitment contexts aimed at Musk.

---

## Q14. Did Musk justifiably rely on the alleged misrepresentation?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
For the same reason as under promissory estoppel: he knew too much, participated too deeply, and personally favored structural evolution too often to make justifiable reliance persuasive.

**Confidence:** **5**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
A later written reassurance directly overriding the contrary evidence: **“Ignore our restructuring discussions—you can still rely on the promise that OpenAI will not commercialize or close off the technology.”**

---

## Q15. Was Musk damaged as a result of that reliance?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
Again, he gave support, but his personal fraud damages are not well defined on this record.

**Confidence:** **4**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
A written damages formula or donation condition tying his contributions to compliance with stated structure/openness commitments.

---

# 6. Unjust enrichment / restitution

## Q16. Did defendants receive a benefit from Musk under circumstances that make retention unjust?

**Verdict:** **Defendants slightly more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
Musk undeniably conferred benefits. The question is whether retention is unjust. I think the answer is close. If I were deciding this in broad fairness terms, Musk has real force: the enterprise became more commercial and less open than advertised. But legally/factually, I’m still pulled by:
- his knowledge,
- his role in discussing for-profit options,
- his refusal of equity,
- and the absence of clear donor restrictions.

So I lean defendants, but only slightly.

**Confidence:** **2**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
A donor letter or board acceptance expressly stating Musk’s funds were restricted to a nonprofit-only, noncommercial, open-access model.

---

## Q17. What amount of restitution would fairly prevent unjust enrichment?

**Verdict:** **If liability existed, some restitution theory could exist, but on the present record I would award none because I do not find unjust enrichment proven. So defendants.**

**Why:**  
Without liability, this doesn’t get far. Also, the measure is highly uncertain.

**Confidence:** **3**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Accounting evidence tracing Musk-funded assets directly into specific later proprietary commercial assets.

---

# 7. Fiduciary duty / charitable-use issues

## Q18. Did any defendant owe Musk a fiduciary duty regarding his donations or OpenAI’s use of the assets they funded?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
I think OpenAI’s nonprofit board may owe duties to the corporation and mission, perhaps “to humanity” in a governance sense. But I do not find a clear, personal fiduciary duty owed to Musk as donor/founder established by these materials.

**Confidence:** **4**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
A donation or governance document expressly stating that OpenAI and its leaders undertook a fiduciary or trustee-like duty to Musk with respect to his contributions.

---

## Q19. If such a duty existed, was it breached by diverting assets or technology toward private profit/Microsoft contrary to the donations’ purpose?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
Even assuming some duty, the strongest defense remains that commercialization and restructuring were undertaken as means of pursuing the mission, not as a naked diversion. The evidence supports mission drift in appearance, but not clearly a fiduciary breach in the legal sense alleged by Musk.

**Confidence:** **3**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Internal board materials saying the mission was secondary and that maximizing Microsoft/commercial value was the actual governing objective.

---

## Q20. Was Musk harmed by any such breach?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
Same damages problem.

**Confidence:** **4**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Proof of an enforceable donor-beneficiary right or reversionary interest.

---

# 8. Mission / AGI-status questions

## Q21. Did GPT-4 qualify as AGI under the relevant governing definition?

**Verdict:** **Defendants slightly more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
Musk has serious evidence here:
- GPT-4’s broad test performance,
- Microsoft’s “Sparks of AGI” paper,
- OpenAI’s own AGI definition.

But the definition is very broad and demanding: “outperforms humans at most economically valuable work.” The record here does not convince me GPT-4 met that threshold, as opposed to being an impressive but incomplete general system. Even the Microsoft paper says “early” and “incomplete.”

So I lean defendants, but it is not a strong lean.

**Confidence:** **2**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
An internal OpenAI board memo or technical assessment concluding: **“GPT-4 meets our AGI definition.”**

---

## Q22. If GPT-4 was AGI, did defendants nonetheless treat it as licensable pre-AGI technology for Microsoft’s benefit?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive, largely because I do not find AGI proven.**

**Why:**  
This rises or falls with Q21. If GPT-4 is not proved AGI, this theory weakens sharply.

**Confidence:** **3**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
A document showing OpenAI insiders believed GPT-4 was AGI but deliberately avoided saying so to preserve Microsoft rights.

---

## Q23. Did defendants improperly withhold or manipulate an AGI determination that should have been made by the board?

**Verdict:** **Defendants slightly more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
Musk’s board-capture story is plausible in a broad sense, but the supplied materials do not prove an AGI determination was due and then manipulated. There is a lot of inference and suspicion, not enough direct evidence.

**Confidence:** **2**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Board emails showing members discussing delaying or suppressing an AGI determination to avoid cutting off Microsoft’s license.

---

# 9. Openness / secrecy

## Q24. Did OpenAI abandon a binding openness commitment by withholding GPT-4 details primarily for commercial rather than safety/security reasons?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
This is one of defendants’ strongest issues because of the direct evidence:
- Ilya to Musk: less openness may make sense as capability increases.
- Musk: “Yup.”
- Charter: safety/security concerns may reduce traditional publishing.

That evidence does not prove the GPT-4 secrecy was purely safety-driven, but it **does** strongly undermine Musk’s claim that withholding details necessarily violated a binding commitment.

**Confidence:** **4**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
An internal decision memo stating GPT-4 details were withheld **primarily to preserve commercial moat/revenue**, not for safety or security.

---

## Q25. Did the parties understand from the outset that reduced openness could be justified as AI grew more powerful?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
The Ilya/Musk email is almost decisive on this point.

**Confidence:** **5**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Evidence that Musk objected immediately afterward and clarified he did **not** agree reduced openness was acceptable for advanced systems.

---

# 10. Corporate structure / for-profit transition

## Q26. Did OpenAI’s creation of a for-profit or capped-profit structure violate any enforceable promise owed to Musk?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
The documentary record that Musk himself proposed:
- C-corp + nonprofit,
- a for-profit next step,
- and even created a public benefit corporation structure,
is very hard to get around.

This is perhaps the single biggest weakness in Musk’s case.

**Confidence:** **5**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
A later binding agreement, after those discussions, re-establishing a nonprofit-only commitment and expressly rejecting any for-profit transition.

---

## Q27. Did Musk know of, agree with, or advocate for a for-profit restructuring before he left?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
The evidence is strong and specific:
- 2015 C-corp email,
- 2017 for-profit discussions,
- “Time to make the next step,”
- formation of “Open Artificial Intelligence Technologies, Inc.”

I find that overwhelming on this question.

**Confidence:** **5**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Authentic evidence showing these documents are incomplete or misleading—for example, omitted follow-up messages where Musk clearly rejected all for-profit restructuring.

---

## Q28. Did defendants materially change OpenAI’s governance/structure in a way that subordinated mission to private commercial interests?

**Verdict:** **Musk slightly more persuasive on the factual characterization, but not enough to win the contract-based claims.**

**Why:**  
On pure narrative/factual impression, I do think Musk has a real point:
- much greater commercialization,
- deep Microsoft dependence,
- more closed models,
- 2023 board turmoil and reconstitution.

If the question is simply whether OpenAI became more commercially oriented and more entangled with private interests, I would say yes. If the question is whether that legally proves subordinating the mission, that’s less clear.

Still, on this narrower factual question, I slightly favor Musk.

**Confidence:** **2**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Board materials showing robust mission-first deliberation and concrete instances where OpenAI sacrificed revenue or Microsoft preferences to uphold mission/safety principles.

---

# 11. Microsoft influence / board capture

## Q29. Did Microsoft obtain influence or control sufficient to cause OpenAI to act primarily for Microsoft’s commercial benefit rather than its mission?

**Verdict:** **Musk slightly more persuasive factually, though not strongly.**

**Why:**  
The best evidence for Musk is Nadella’s own language:
- “We have all the IP rights…”
- “We are below them, above them, around them.”
Coupled with Microsoft’s investment, compute leverage, licensing, and observer seat, it paints a picture of substantial influence.

I’m not prepared to say legal control was proved, but practical influence? Yes, probably substantial. “Primarily for Microsoft’s benefit” is harder, but the record leans somewhat Musk’s way.

**Confidence:** **2**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Definitive governance documents showing Microsoft had no meaningful vetoes, no strategic control, and no access to key mission-governance decisions.

---

## Q30. Did the November 2023 board events leave a board less independent or less able to make an honest AGI determination?

**Verdict:** **Musk slightly more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
The complaint’s version of events—Altman’s firing, Microsoft pressure, return conditioned on board changes, new members less steeped in AI governance—has some persuasive force. The supplied materials do not fully rebut it.

Still, the record is incomplete and contested, so this is only a slight lean.

**Confidence:** **2**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Independent testimony or documents showing the new board was selected through a genuinely independent process and was specifically briefed and structured to preserve mission/AGI-governance independence.

---

# 12. Antitrust questions

## Q31. Were OpenAI and Microsoft actual or potential competitors in a relevant antitrust market?

**Verdict:** **No confident verdict; slight lean defendants.**

**Why:**  
The legal theory may be plausible, but the factual market record in the materials is underdeveloped. They are clearly close partners. They may also be potential competitors in generative AI. But I do not think the materials here prove the competition issue strongly enough.

**Confidence:** **1**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
A rigorous market analysis or internal business documents from either company identifying the other as a direct competitor in the relevant generative-AI product market.

---

## Q32. Did a prohibited interlocking directorate exist between OpenAI and Microsoft?

**Verdict:** **Musk slightly more persuasive, subject to the competition issue.**

**Why:**  
If OpenAI and Microsoft are competitors, then the FTC/DOJ statement is quite helpful to Musk. It says:
- Section 8 isn’t mooted just by unwinding the interlock,
- substance beats form,
- agents/observers can count in some circumstances.

The allegations about Hoffman and Templeton are enough to make Musk’s side more persuasive than defendants’ categorical denial. But because the competition element is shaky on this record, this remains a weak lean.

**Confidence:** **2**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Evidence that Hoffman/Templeton had no role in competitor-sensitive board matters and that OpenAI/Microsoft were not competitors within Section 8.

---

## Q33. If an interlock existed, did it create a meaningful risk of coordination or information exchange?

**Verdict:** **Musk more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
This is almost the premise of Section 8 and the FTC brief. If there is an overlap between competing entities at the board/observer level, the risk of information flow and coordination is obvious and recognized by the Agencies.

**Confidence:** **3**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Strict documented firewalls, recusal practices, and proof that no competitively sensitive information was shared or accessible.

---

## Q34. Did defendants engage in a contract, combination, or conspiracy in unreasonable restraint of trade, including a group boycott / “fund-no-competitors” arrangement?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
Not because the legal theory is impossible—the FTC says it is legally viable—but because the **factual record in the supplied materials is too thin**. I do not see enough concrete evidence of an actual agreement to boycott or suppress funding for rivals.

**Confidence:** **4**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
An email, term sheet, or investor communication explicitly directing parties **not to fund competitors** as part of an OpenAI/Microsoft arrangement.

---

## Q35. Was any such restraint a substantial factor in harming competition and/or harming Musk or xAI?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
Because I do not think the underlying restraint is well proved on this record, I also do not find antitrust injury proved.

**Confidence:** **4**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Market evidence showing specific rival financings were blocked or withdrawn because of an OpenAI/Microsoft no-competitors policy.

---

# 13. Cross-cutting credibility/state-of-mind questions

## Q36. Did Musk understand before leaving that AGI would require billions and likely some for-profit structure?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive.**

**Why:**  
The documentary evidence is too strong to deny. He clearly understood both the capital intensity and the structural implications.

**Confidence:** **5**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
A later written statement from Musk clearly retracting that view and insisting OpenAI must remain nonprofit regardless of capital constraints.

---

## Q37. Did Musk leave because he opposed mission betrayal, or because he failed to obtain control / preferred Tesla as the vehicle?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive that control/Tesla conflict was a major reason.**

**Why:**  
The OpenAI-published documents strongly indicate Musk wanted:
- majority equity,
- initial board control,
- CEO role,
- or merger into Tesla.

That does not mean he had no mission concerns. But on the balance of the record, the split looks heavily driven by governance/control disagreement, not solely by principled opposition to commercialization.

**Confidence:** **4**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Contemporaneous notes or emails from neutral participants showing Musk expressly rejected personal control and left solely because OpenAI refused to preserve nonprofit/open principles.

---

## Q38. When defendants made the key statements in 2015–2018, were they sincere at the time or secretly planning something different?

**Verdict:** **Defendants more persuasive that they were sincere at the time.**

**Why:**  
I see evolution, not proven deceit. The 2017 compute and funding documents read like genuine changing circumstances, not evidence of an original secret plan.

**Confidence:** **4**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Any contemporaneous internal message discussing a planned future reversal of the nonprofit/open promises once the technology matured.

---

# 14. Remedies

## Q39. What amount of compensatory damages, if any, should be awarded to Musk?

**Verdict:** **$0 on the present record. Defendants.**

**Why:**  
Because I would not find the main liability theories proved, and because his personal damages case is weak.

**Confidence:** **4**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
A clear contractual damages provision or restricted-donation agreement tied to deviation from mission.

---

## Q40. Are punitive damages warranted?

**Verdict:** **No. Defendants.**

**Why:**  
I do not find fraud, malice, or similar culpable state of mind proved by this record.

**Confidence:** **5**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
Direct evidence of knowing deception at the time Musk was solicited.

---

## Q41. What restitution or disgorgement, if any, is appropriate?

**Verdict:** **None on the present record. Defendants.**

**Why:**  
Because I would not find the predicate liability established strongly enough, and because the measure is highly uncertain.

**Confidence:** **3**

**Single additional piece of evidence that would most change my answer:**  
A detailed forensic accounting tracing Musk-funded work directly into later proprietary assets monetized contrary to express donor restrictions.

---

# Bottom-line overall verdict

If I were a juror deciding the case based only on these materials, my overall view would be:

- **Musk has a strong rhetorical and moral narrative**: OpenAI started as a nonprofit, open, humanity-first counterweight to Google and later became much more commercial, closed, and intertwined with Microsoft.
- **But defendants have the stronger legal/factual case on the central claims**, mainly because the documentary record appears to show that Musk himself:
  1. questioned the nonprofit-only structure from the beginning,
  2. agreed that a for-profit transition was likely necessary,
  3. accepted that advanced AI might need less openness,
  4. pushed for control / Tesla integration,
  5. and recognized the need for billions in capital.

So my overall verdict would be **for defendants on most of Musk’s core contract, estoppel, fraud, and damages theories**, with only a few narrower factual points leaning Musk’s way:
- OpenAI did become more commercially oriented and more Microsoft-influenced than its original public branding suggested.
- The antitrust interlock concerns are not frivolous, but the factual record here is not strong enough for me to find antitrust liability.

If you want, I can turn this into a **mock verdict form** with yes/no answers only, or into a **short closing statement** explaining why I would vote for one side overall.

Description: OpenAI's public response to Musk's lawsuit, including selected 2015â€“2018 email correspondence between Musk, Altman, Brockman, and Sutskever.

March 5, 2024
Company
OpenAI and Elon Musk
We are dedicated to the OpenAI mission and have pursued it every step of the way.
Loading…
Share
The mission of OpenAI is to ensure AGI benefits all of humanity, which means both building safe and beneficial AGI and helping create broadly distributed benefits. We are now sharing what we've learned about achieving our mission, and some facts about our relationship with Elon. We intend to move to dismiss all of Elon’s claims.
We realized building AGI will require far more resources than we’d initially imagined
Elon said we should announce an initial $1B funding commitment to OpenAI. In total, the non-profit has raised less than $45M from Elon and more than $90M from other donors.
When starting OpenAI in late 2015, Greg and Sam had initially planned to raise $100M. Elon said in an email: “We need to go with a much bigger number than $100M to avoid sounding hopeless… I think we should say that we are starting with a $1B funding commitment… I will cover whatever anyone else doesn't provide.”
[1]
⁠
We spent a lot of time trying to envision a plausible path to AGI. In early 2017, we came to the realization that building AGI will require vast quantities of compute. We began calculating how much compute an AGI might plausibly require. We all understood we were going to need a lot more capital to succeed at our mission—billions of dollars per year, which was far more than any of us, especially Elon, thought we’d be able to raise as the non-profit.
We and Elon recognized a for-profit entity would be necessary to acquire those resources
As we discussed a for-profit structure in order to further the mission, Elon wanted us to merge with Tesla or he wanted full control. Elon left OpenAI, saying there needed to be a relevant competitor to Google/DeepMind and that he was going to do it himself. He said he’d be supportive of us finding our own path.
In late 2017, we and Elon decided the next step for the mission was to create a for-profit entity. Elon wanted majority equity, initial board control, and to be CEO. In the middle of these discussions, he withheld funding. Reid Hoffman bridged the gap to cover salaries and operations.
We couldn’t agree to terms on a for-profit with Elon because we felt it was against the mission for any individual to have absolute control over OpenAI. He then suggested instead merging OpenAI into Tesla. In early February 2018, Elon forwarded us an email suggesting that OpenAI should “attach to Tesla as its cash cow”, commenting that it was “exactly right… Tesla is the only path that could even hope to hold a candle to Google. Even then, the probability of being a counterweight to Google is small. It just isn’t zero”.
[2]
⁠
Elon soon chose to leave OpenAI, saying that our probability of success was 0, and that he planned to build an AGI competitor within Tesla. When he left in late February 2018, he told our team he was supportive of us finding our own path to raising billions of dollars. In December 2018, Elon sent us an email saying “Even raising several hundred million won’t be enough. This needs billions per year immediately or forget it.”
[3]
⁠
We advance our mission by building widely-available beneficial tools
We’re making our technology broadly usable in ways that empower people and improve their daily lives, including via open-source contributions.
We provide broad access to today's most powerful AI, including a free version that hundreds of millions of people use every day. For example, Albania is using OpenAI’s tools to accelerate its EU accession by as much as 5.5 years; Digital Green is helping boost farmer income in Kenya and India by dropping the cost of agricultural extension services 100x by building on OpenAI; Lifespan, the largest healthcare provider in Rhode Island, uses GPT‑4 to simplify its surgical consent forms from a college reading level to a 6th grade one; Iceland is using GPT‑4 to preserve the Icelandic language.
Elon understood the mission did not imply open-sourcing AGI. As Ilya told Elon: “As we get closer to building AI, it will make sense to start being less open.  The Open in openAI means that everyone should benefit from the fruits of AI after its built, but it's totally OK to not share the science...”, to which Elon replied: “Yup”.
[4]
⁠
We're sad that it's come to this with someone whom we’ve deeply admired—someone who inspired us to aim higher, then told us we would fail, started a competitor, and then sued us when we started making meaningful progress towards OpenAI’s mission without him.
We are focused on advancing our mission and have a long way to go. As we continue to make our tools better and better, we are excited to deploy these systems so they empower every individual.
Update March 11, 2024: We are seeking to have the lawsuit assigned to dedicated case management, since it involves AI technology and the claims span almost a decade.

Several points:
It is not the case that once we solve “concepts,” we get AI. Other problems that will have to be solved include unsupervised learning, transfer learning, and lifetime learning. We’re also doing pretty badly with language right now. It does not mean that these problems will not see significant progress in the coming years, but it is not the case that there is only one problem that stands between us and full human level AI.
We can’t build AI today because we lack key ideas (computers may be too slow, too, but we can’t tell). Powerful ideas are produced by top people. Massive clusters help, and are very worth getting, but they play a less important role.
We will be able to achieve a conventionally significant result in the next 6 to 9 months, simply because the people we already have are very good. Achieving a field-altering result will be harder, riskier, and take longer. But we have a not unreasonable plan for that as well.
Bi-weekly updates
3
emails
From:
Ilya Sutskever <redacted>
Date:
Mon, Jun 12, 2017 at 10:39 PM
To:
Greg Brockman <redacted>, <redacted>, Elon Musk <redacted>
This is the first of our bi-weekly updates. The goal is to keep you up to date, and to help us make greater use from your visits.
Compute:
Compute is used in two ways: it is used to run a big experiment quickly, and it is used to run many experiments in parallel.
95% of progress comes from the ability to run big experiments quickly. The utility of running many experiments is much less useful.
In the old days, a large cluster could help you run more experiments, but it could not help with running a single large experiment quickly.
For this reason, an academic lab could compete with Google, because Google’s only advantage was the ability to run many experiments. This is not a great advantage.
Recently, it has become possible to combine 100s of GPUs and 100s of CPUs to run an experiment that’s 100x bigger than what is possible on a single machine while requiring comparable time. This has become possible due to the work of many different groups. As a result, the minimum necessary cluster for being competitive is now 10–100x larger than it was before.
Currently, every Dota experiment uses 1000+ cores, and it is only for the small 1v1 variant, and on extremely small neural network policies. We will need more compute to just win the 1v1 variant. To win the full 5v5 game, we will need to run fewer experiments, where each experiment is at least 1 order of magnitude larger (possibly more!).
TLDR:
What matters is the size and speed of our experiments. In the old days, a big cluster could not let anyone run a larger experiment quickly. Today, a big cluster lets us run a large experiment 100x faster.
In order to be capable of accomplishing our projects even in theory, we need to increase the number of our GPUs by a factor of 10x in the next 1–2 months (we have enough CPUs). We will discuss the specifics in our in-person meeting.
Dota 2:
We will solve the 1v1 version of the game in
1 month
. Fans of the game care about 1v1 a fair bit.
We are now at a point where a *single experiment* consumes 1000s of cores, and where adding more distributed compute increases performance.
Here is a cool video of our bot doing something rather clever:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Y-vxbREX5ck&feature=youtu.be&t=99.
Rapid learning of new games:
Infra work is underway
We implemented several baselines
Fundamentally, we’re not where we want to be, and are taking action to correct this.
Robotics:
Current status:
The HER algorithm (
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Dz_HuzgMzxo
) can learn to solve many low-dimensional robotics tasks that were previously unsolvable very rapidly. It is non-obvious, simple, and effective.
In 6 months, we will accomplish at least one of:
single-handed Rubik’s cube, pen spinning (
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dDavyRnEPrI
), Chinese balls spinning (
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=M9N1duIl4Fc
) using the HER algorithm and using a sim2real method [such as
https://blog.openai.com/spam-detection-in-the-physical-world/
].
The above will be deployed on the robotic hand:
[Link to Google Drive]
[this video is human controlled, not algorithmic controlled. Need to be logged in to the OpenAI account to see the video].
Self play as a key path to AGI:
Self play in multiagent environments is magical: if you place agents into an environment, then no matter how smart (or not smart) they are, the environment will provide them with the exact level of challenge, which can be faced only by outsmarting the competition. So for example, if you have a group of children, they will find each other’s company to be challenging; likewise for a collection of super intelligences of comparable intelligence. So the “solution” to self-play is to become more and more intelligent, without bound.
Self-play lets us get “something out of nothing.” The rules of a competitive game can be simple, but the best strategy for playing this game can be immensely complex. [motivating example:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=u2T77mQmJYI
].
Training agents in simulation to develop very good dexterity via competitive fighting, such as wrestling. Here is a video of ant-shaped robots that we trained to struggle:
<redacted>
Current work on self-play: getting agents to learn to develop a language [gifs in
https://blog.openai.com/learning-to-cooperate-compete-and-communicate/
]. Agents are doing “stuff,” but it’s still work in progress.
We have a few more cool smaller projects. Updates to be presented as they produce significant results.

The business of building AGI
From:
Ilya Sutskever <redacted>
Date:
Wed, Jul 12, 2017 at 1:36 PM
To:
Elon Musk <redacted>, Greg Brockman <redacted>
We usually decide that problems are hard because smart people have worked on them unsuccessfully for a long time. It’s easy to think that this is true about AI. However, the past five years of progress have shown that the earliest and simplest ideas about AI — neural networks — were right all along, and we needed modern hardware to get them working.
Historically, AI breakthroughs have consistently happened with models that take between 7–10 days to train. This means that hardware defines the surface of potential AI breakthroughs. This is a statement about human psychology more than about AI. If experiments take longer than this, it’s hard to keep all the state in your head and iterate and improve. If experiments are shorter, you’ll just use a bigger model.
It’s not so much that AI progress is a hardware game, any more than physics is a particle accelerator game. But if our computers are too slow, no amount of cleverness will result in AGI, just like if a particle accelerator is too small, we have no shot at figuring out how the universe works. Fast enough computers are a necessary ingredient, and all past failures may have been caused by computers being too slow for AGI.
Until very recently, there was no way to use many GPUs together to run faster experiments, so academia had the same “effective compute” as industry. But earlier this year, Google used two orders of magnitude more compute than is typical to optimize the architecture of a classifier, something that usually requires lots of researcher time. And a few months ago, Facebook released a paper showing how to train a large ImageNet model with near-linear speedup to 256 GPUs (given a specially-configured cluster with high-bandwidth interconnects).
Over the past year, Google Brain produced impressive results because they have an order of magnitude or two more GPUs than anyone. We estimate that Brain has around 100k GPUs, FAIR has around 15–20k, and DeepMind allocates 50 per researcher on question asking, and rented 5k GPUs from Brain for AlphaGo. Apparently, when people run neural networks at Google Brain, it eats up everyone’s quotas at DeepMind.
We're still missing several key ideas necessary for building AGI. How can we use a system's understanding of “thing A” to learn “thing B” (e.g. can I teach a system to count, then to multiply, then to solve word problems)? How do we build curious systems? How do we train a system to discover the deep underlying causes of all types of phenomena — to act as a scientist? How can we build a system that adapts to new situations on which it hasn’t been trained on precisely (e.g. being asked to apply familiar concepts in an unfamiliar situation)? But given enough hardware to run the relevant experiments in 7–10 days, history indicates that the right algorithms will be found, just like physicists would quickly figure out how the universe works if only they had a big enough particle accelerator.
There is good reason to believe that deep learning hardware will speed up 10x each year for the next four to five years. The world is used to the comparatively leisurely pace of Moore’s Law, and is not prepared for the drastic changes in capability this hardware acceleration will bring. This speedup will happen not because of smaller transistors or faster clock cycles; it will happen because like the brain, neural networks are intrinsically parallelizable, and new highly parallel hardware is being built to exploit this.
Within the next three years, robotics should be completely solved, AI should solve a long-standing unproven theorem, programming competitions should be won consistently by AIs, and there should be convincing chatbots (though no one should pass the Turing test). In as little as four years, each overnight experiment will feasibly use so much compute capacity that there’s an actual chance of waking up to AGI, given the right algorithm — and figuring out the algorithm will actually happen within 2–4 further years of experimenting with this compute in a competitive multiagent simulation.
To be in the business of building safe AGI, OpenAI needs to:
Have the best AI results each year. In particular, as hardware gets exponentially better, we’ll have dramatically better results. Our DOTA and Rubik’s cube projects will have impressive results for the current level of compute. Next year’s projects will be even more extreme, and what’s realistic depends primarily on what compute we can access.
Increase our GPU cluster from 600 GPUs to 5000 GPUs ASAP. As an upper bound, this will require a capex of $12M and an opex of $5–6M over the next year. Each year, we’ll need to exponentially increase our hardware spend, but we have reason to believe AGI can ultimately be built with less than $10B in hardware.
Increase our headcount: from 55 (July 2017) to 80 (January 2018) to 120 (January 2019) to 200 (January 2020). We’ve learned how to organize our current team, and we’re now bottlenecked by number of smart people trying out ideas.
Lock down an overwhelming hardware advantage. The 4-chip card that
<redacted>
says he can build in 2 years is effectively TPU 3.0 and (given enough quantity) would allow us to be on an almost equal footing with Google on compute. The Cerebras design is far ahead of both of these, and if they’re real then having exclusive access to them would put us far ahead of the competition. We have a structural idea for how to do this given more due diligence, best to discuss on a call.
2/3/4 will ultimately require large amounts of capital. If we can secure the funding, we have a real chance at setting the initial conditions under which AGI is born. Increased funding needs will come lockstep with increased magnitude of results. We should discuss options to obtain the relevant funding, as that’s the biggest piece that’s outside of our direct control.
Progress this week:
We’ve beat our top 1v1 test player (he’s top 30 in North America at 1v1, and beats the top 1v1 player about 30% of the time), but the bot can also be exploited by playing weirdly. We’re working on understanding these exploits and cracking down on them.
Repeated from Saturday, here’s the first match where we beat our top test player:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FBoUHay7XBI&feature=youtu.be&t=345
Every additional day of training makes the bot stronger and harder to exploit.
Robot getting closer to solving Rubik’s cube.
The improved cube simulation teleoperated by a human:
<redacted>
.
Our defense against adversarial examples is starting to work on ImageNet.
We will completely solve the problem of adversarial examples by the end of August.

On September 12, 2017, Elon laid out a board structure where he would “unequivocally have initial control of the company”.
Re: Current State
From:
Elon Musk <redacted>
Date:
Wed, Sep 13, 2017 at 12:40 AM
To:
Ilya Sutskever <redacted>
Cc:
Greg Brockman <redacted>
Sounds good. The three common stock seats (you, Greg and Sam) should be elected by common shareholders. They will de facto be yours, but not in the unlikely event that you lose the faith of a huge percentage of common stockholders over time or step away from the company by choice.
I think that the Preferred A investment round (supermajority me) should have the right to appoint four (not three) seats. I would not expect to appoint them immediately, but, like I said I would unequivocally have initial control of the company, but this will change quickly.
The rough target would be to get to a 12 person board (probably more like 16 if this board really ends up deciding the fate of the world) where each board member has a deep understanding of technology, at least a basic understanding of AI and strong & sensible morals.
Apart from the Series A four and the Common three, there would likely be a board member with each new lead investor/ally. However, the specific individual new board members can only be added if all but one existing board member agrees. Same for removing board members.
There will also be independent board members we want to add who aren’t associated with an investor. Same rules apply: requires all but one of existing directors to add or remove.
I’m super tired and don’t want to overcomplicate things, but this seems approx right. At the sixteen person board level, we would have 7/16 votes and I’d have a 25% influence, which is my min comfort level. That sounds about right to me. If everyone else we asked to join our board is truly against us, we should probably lose.
As mentioned, my experience with boards (assuming they consist of good, smart people) is that they are rational and reasonable. There is basically never a real hardcore battle where an individual board vote is pivotal, so this is almost certainly (sure hope so) going to be a moot point.
As a closing note, I’ve been really impressed with the quality of discussion with you guys on the equity and board stuff. I have a really good feeling about this.
Lmk if above seems reasonable.
Elon
Elon also told us he would need to be CEO.
September 2017: Elon created the public benefit corporation called “Open Artificial Intelligence Technologies, Inc.”
Elon directed Jared Birchall, his wealth manager, to create the public benefit corporation “Open Artificial Intelligence Technologies, Inc” (akin to SpaceX’s official name of “Space Exploration Technologies Corporation”). It was registered on on September 15, 2017:
The public benefit corporation created by Elon Musk on September 15, 2017, as the proposed future structure of OpenAI.
September 2017: We rejected Elon's terms because giving him unilateral control of OpenAI and its technology would be contrary to the mission.
We came close to accepting Elon’s terms. As we told him, “We really want to work with you. We believe that if we join forces, our chance of success in the mission is the greatest.”
However, we felt his proposal was not compatible with the mission, saying: “The current structure provides you with a path where you end up with unilateral absolute control over the AGI. You stated that you don't want to control the final AGI, but during this negotiation, you've shown to us that absolute control is extremely important to you.” As we told Elon, “The goal of OpenAI is to make the future good and to avoid an AGI dictatorship.”
We concluded, “Thus we are concerned that as the company makes genuine progress towards AGI, you will choose to retain your absolute control of the company despite current intent to the contrary.”
Honest Thoughts

emails
From:
Ilya Sutskever <redacted>
Date:
Wed, Sep 20, 2017 at 2:08 PM
To:
Elon Musk <redacted>, Sam Altman <redacted>, Greg Brockman <redacted>
Elon, Sam,
This process has been the highest stakes conversation that Greg and I have ever participated in, and if the project succeeds, it’ll turn out to have been the highest stakes conversation the world has seen. It’s also been a deeply personal conversation for all of us.
Yesterday while we were considering making our final commitment (even the non-solicit agreement), we realized we’d made a mistake. We have several important concerns that we haven’t raised with either of you. We didn’t raise them because we were afraid to: we were afraid of harming the relationship, having you think less of us, or losing you as partners.
There is some chance that our concerns will prove to be unresolvable. We really hope it’s not the case, but we know we will fail for sure if we don’t all discuss them now. And we have hope that we can work through them all and continue working together.
Elon
We
really
want to work with you. We believe that if we join forces, our chance of success in the mission is the greatest. Our upside is the highest. There is no doubt about that. Our desire to work with you is so great that we are happy to give up on the equity, personal control, make ourselves easily firable — whatever it takes to work with you.
But we realized that we were careless in our thinking about the implications of control for the world. Because it seemed so hubristic, we have not been seriously considering the implications of success.
The current structure provides you with a path where you end up with unilateral absolute control over the AGI. You stated that you don’t want to control the final AGI, but during this negotiation, you’ve shown to us that absolute control is extremely important to you.
As an example, you said that you needed to be CEO of the new company so that everyone will know that you are the one who is in charge, even though you also stated that you hate being CEO and would much rather not be CEO.
Thus, we are concerned that as the company makes genuine progress towards AGI, you will choose to retain your absolute control of the company despite current intent to the contrary. We disagree with your statement that our ability to leave is our greatest power, because once the company is actually on track to AGI, the company will be much more important than any individual.
The goal of OpenAI is to make the future good and to avoid an AGI dictatorship. You are concerned that Demis could create an AGI dictatorship. So do we. So it is a bad idea to create a structure where you could become a dictator if you chose to, especially given that we can create some other structure that avoids this possibility.
We have a few smaller concerns, but we think it’s useful to mention it here:
In the event we decide to buy Cerebras, my strong sense is that it’ll be done through Tesla. But why do it this way if we could also do it from within OpenAI? Specifically, the concern is that Tesla has a duty to shareholders to maximize shareholder return, which is not aligned with OpenAI’s mission. So the overall result may not end up being optimal for OpenAI.
We believe that OpenAI the non-profit was successful because both you and Sam were in it. Sam acted as a genuine counterbalance to you, which has been extremely fruitful. Greg and I, at least so far, are much worse at being a counterbalance to you. We feel this is evidenced even by this negotiation, where we were ready to sweep the long-term AGI control questions under the rug while Sam stood his ground.
Sam:
When Greg and I are stuck, you’ve always had an answer that turned out to be deep and correct. You’ve been thinking about the ways forward on this problem extremely deeply and thoroughly. Greg and I understand technical execution, but we don’t know how structure decisions will play out over the next month, year, or five years.
But we haven’t been able to fully trust your judgments throughout this process, because we don’t understand your cost function.
We don’t understand why the CEO title is so important to you. Your stated reasons have changed, and it’s hard to really understand what’s driving it.
Is AGI truly your primary motivation? How does it connect to your political goals? How has your thought process changed over time?
Greg and Ilya:
We had a fair share of our own failings during this negotiation, and we’ll list some of them here (Elon and Sam, I’m sure you’ll have plenty to add…):
During this negotiation, we realized that we have allowed the idea of financial return 2–3 years down the line to drive our decisions. This is why we didn’t push on the control — we thought that our equity is good enough, so why worry? But this attitude is wrong, just like the attitude of AI experts who don’t think that AI safety is an issue because they don’t really believe that they’ll build AGI. We did not speak our full truth during the negotiation. We have our excuses, but it was damaging to the process, and we may lose both Sam and Elon as a result.
There’s enough baggage here that we think it’s very important for us to meet and talk it out. Our collaboration will not succeed if we don’t. Can all four of us meet today? If all of us say the truth, and resolve the issues, the company that we’ll create will be much more likely to withstand the very strong forces it’ll experience.
Greg & Ilya

Moral high ground
Our biggest tool is the moral high ground. To retain this, we must:
Try our best to remain a non-profit. AI is going to shake up the fabric of society, and our fiduciary duty should be to humanity.
Put increasing effort into the safety/control problem, rather than the fig leaf you’ve noted in other institutions. It doesn’t matter who wins if everyone dies. Related to this, we need to communicate a “better red than dead” outlook — we’re trying to build safe AGI, and we’re not willing to destroy the world in a down-to-the-wire race to do so.
Engage with government to provide trusted, unbiased policy advice — we often hear that they mistrust recommendations from companies such as

The past 2 years
I would be curious to hear how you rate our execution over the past two years, relative to resources. In my view:
Over the past five years, there have two major demonstrations of working systems: AlphaZero [DeepMind] and Dota 1v1 [OpenAI]. (There are a larger number of breakthroughs of “capabilities” popular among practitioners, the top of which I’d say are: ProgressiveGAN [NVIDIA], unsupervised translation [Facebook], WaveNet [DeepMind], Atari/DQN [DeepMind], machine translation [Ilya at Google — now at OpenAI], generative adversarial network [Ian Goodfellow at grad school — now at Google], variational autoencoder [Durk at grad school — now at OpenAI], AlexNet [Ilya at grad school — now at OpenAI].) We benchmark well on this axis.
We grew very rapidly in 2016, and in 2017 iterated to a working management structure. We’re now ready to scale massively, given the resources. We lose people on comp currently, but pretty much only on comp. I’ve been resuming the style of recruiting I did in the early days, and believe I can exceed those results.
We have the most talent dense team in the field, and we have the reputation for it as well.
We don’t encourage paper writing, and so paper acceptance isn’t a measure we optimize. For the ICLR chart Andrej sent, I’d expect our (accepted papers)/(people submitting papers) to be the highest in the field.

GENERAL ALLEGATIONS 
Overview Of The Case 
A. The Risk Of Artificial General Intelligence 
15. Over the course of the 20th century, the Un ited States gradually shifted from a 
primarily human labor-based economy to a primarily human knowledge-based economy, with 
economic value increasingly created primarily by human intelligence. As the century progressed,

COMPLAINT 
 
another paradigm shift was already underway: value creation through artificial intelligence (AI). 
Early AI programs were capable of outperforming humans in certain discrete tasks. Almost 
immediately after the invention of the programmable computer, AI could show super-human 
performance on a highly formalized problem like finding the fastest path through a network of roads. 
It took longer for AI to reach superiority for problems requiring more creativity. In 1996, IBM’s 
Deep Blue AI program beat Gary Kasparov, the then-world champion in chess. These programs, 
while useful, were essentially one-trick-ponies—their intelligence was not general. Deep Blue had 
massive arrays of processors that could only play chess. Algorithms for path finding could solve a 
maze or route a car, but they could not paint a painting.  
16. Starting in the late 2000s and early 2010s, an older algorithm called “deep learning” 
became practical to implement on low-cost hardware for the first time. This caused an almost 
overnight revolution in performance across nearly all AI projects. New, top of class algorithms were 
developed for turning speech into text, translating between languages, and recognizing what kind of 
food is shown in a photo. One of the hallmarks of deep learning is that algorithms do not need to be 
designed with significant knowledge of the task at hand. They learn each task from training 
examples, essentially programming themselves. This means that they are far more general-purpose 
than earlier systems like Deep Blue. 
17. As deep learning algorithms became increasingly sophisticated, some of the world’s 
leading AI researchers set their sights on what has come to be called Artificial General Intelligence 
(AGI). The basic concept of AGI is a general purpose artificial intelligence system—a machine 
having intelligence for a wide variety of tasks like a human.  
18. Mr. Musk has long recognized that AGI poses a grave threat to humanity—perhaps 
the greatest existential threat we face today. His concerns mirrored those raised before him by 
luminaries like Stephen Hawking and Sun Microsystems founder Bill Joy. Our entire economy is 
based around the fact that humans work together and come up with the best solutions to a hard task. 
If a machine can solve nearly any task better than we can, that machine becomes more economically 
useful than we are. As Mr. Joy warned, with strong AGI, “the future doesn’t need us.” Mr. Musk

publicly called for a variety of measures to address the dangers of AGI, from voluntary moratoria 
to regulation, but his calls largely fell on deaf ears.  
19. But where some like Mr. Musk see an existential threat in AGI, others see AGI as a 
source of profit and power.  
20. In 2014, Google acquired DeepMind, a research group focused on deep learning. 
One of DeepMind’s initial developments was AlphaZero, a chess playing algorithm. Unlike 
previous algorithms, however, AlphaZero used “reinforcement learning,” wherein the program 
learns to play chess by playing itself with different versions of the software. It starts by playing 
randomly, with no understanding of the game’s strategy. When one version of the software wins a 
game against another, the winning program’s internal pathways are “reinforced” and the process 
repeats.  
21. AlphaZero rapidly became the strongest chess playing system in the world. 
Shockingly, it was also announced that the same program was also the strongest in the world for 
playing two other extremely difficult games. In Google/DeepMind’s words, “Starting from random 
play, and given no domain knowledge except the game rules, AlphaZero achieved within 24 hours 
a superhuman level of play in the games of chess and shogi (Japanese chess) as well as Go, and 
convincingly defeated a world-champion program in each case.” 
22. With the DeepMind team, Google immediately catapulted to the front of the race for 
AGI. Mr. Musk was deeply troubled by this development. He believed (and still does) that in the 
hands of a closed, for-profit company like Google, AGI poses a particularly acute and noxious 
danger to humanity. In 2014, it was already difficult enough to compete with Google in its core 
businesses. Google had collected a uniquely large set of data from our searches, our emails, and 
nearly every book in our libraries. Nevertheless, up to this point, everyone had the potential to 
compete with Google through superior human intelligence and hard work. AGI would make 
competition nearly impossible.

Detailed Allegations 
A. Mr. Musk’s Concerns Over AGI Falling Into The Wrong Hands 
34. In 2012, Elon Musk met Demis Hassabis, the co-founder of DeepMind, a for-profit

factory in Hawthorne, California where Mr. Musk and Mr. Hassabis discussed the greatest threats 
facing society. During this conversation, Mr. Hassabis emphasized the potential dangers that the 
advancement of AI presents to society.     
35. Following this conversation with Mr. Hassabis, Mr. Musk became increasingly 
concerned about the potential of AI to become super-intelligent, surpass human intelligence, and 
threaten humanity. Indeed, Mr. Musk was not the only person afraid about the dangers of AI and 
the research being conducted at DeepMind. It has been reported that following a meeting with Mr. 
Hassabis and investors in DeepMind, one of the investors remarked that the best thing he could have 
done for the human race was shoot Mr. Hassabis then and there. 
36. Mr. Musk began discussing AI and DeepMind with those in his orbit, such as Larry 
Page, then-CEO of Google’s parent company Alphabet, Inc. Mr. Musk would frequently raise the 
dangers of AI in his conversations with Mr. Page, but to Mr. Musk’s shock, Mr. Page was 
unconcerned. For example, in 2013, Mr. Musk had a passionate exchange with Mr. Page about the 
dangers of AI. He warned that unless safeguards were put in place, “artificial intelligence-systems 
might replace humans, making our species irrelevant or even extinct.” Mr. Page responded that 
would merely “be the next stage of evolution,” and claimed Mr. Musk was being a “specist”—that

50. On May 25, 2015, Mr. Altman emailed Mr. Musk, writing that he had “[b]een

for AI. (Unfortunately, it turns out that the “Manhattan Project” is a moniker that may be all too 
apt.) He proposed that “we could structure it so that the tech belongs to the world via some sort of 
nonprofit but the people working on it get startup-like compensation if it works. Obviously we’d 
comply with/aggressively support all regulation.” Mr. Musk responded, “Probably worth a 
conversation.” 
51. After further communications, Mr. Altman emailed Mr. Musk on June 24, 2015 with

More generally, safety should be a first-class requirement.” “The technology would be owned by 
the foundation and used ‘for the good of the world’[.]” He proposed that they start with a group of 
7-10 people and expand from there. He also proposed a governance structure. Mr. Musk responded, 
“Agree on all.” Ex. 2 at 1.  
52. Soon thereafter, Mr. Altman began recruiting others to help with the development of 
the project. Notably, Mr. Altman approached Gregory Brockman to help with the project.

via email. Regarding the project, Mr. Brockman told Mr. Musk, “I hope for us to enter the field as
