Title: OpenAI Charter
URL: https://openai.com/charter/
Type: primary_source_charter
Description: OpenAI's published charter, central to the breach-of-mission claim.

OpenAI
Company
OpenAI Charter
Our Charter describes the principles we use to execute on OpenAI’s mission.
This document reflects the strategy we’ve refined over the past two years, including feedback from many people internal and external to OpenAI. The timeline to AGI remains uncertain, but our Charter will guide us in acting in the best interests of humanity throughout its development.
OpenAI’s mission is to ensure that artificial general intelligence (AGI)—by which we mean highly autonomous systems that outperform humans at most economically valuable work—benefits all of humanity. We will attempt to directly build safe and beneficial AGI, but will also consider our mission fulfilled if our work aids others to achieve this outcome. To that end, we commit to the following principles:
Broadly distributed benefits
We commit to use any influence we obtain over AGI’s deployment to ensure it is used for the benefit of all, and to avoid enabling uses of AI or AGI that harm humanity or unduly concentrate power.
Our primary fiduciary duty is to humanity. We anticipate needing to marshal substantial resources to fulfill our mission, but will always diligently act to minimize conflicts of interest among our employees and stakeholders that could compromise broad benefit.
Long-term safety
We are committed to doing the research required to make AGI safe, and to driving the broad adoption of such research across the AI community.
We are concerned about late-stage AGI development becoming a competitive race without time for adequate safety precautions. Therefore, if a value-aligned, safety-conscious project comes close to building AGI before we do, we commit to stop competing with and start assisting this project. We will work out specifics in case-by-case agreements, but a typical triggering condition might be “a better-than-even chance of success in the next two years.”
Technical leadership
To be effective at addressing AGI’s impact on society, OpenAI must be on the cutting edge of AI capabilities—policy and safety advocacy alone would be insufficient.
We believe that AI will have broad societal impact before AGI, and we’ll strive to lead in those areas that are directly aligned with our mission and expertise.
Cooperative orientation
We will actively cooperate with other research and policy institutions; we seek to create a global community working together to address AGI’s global challenges.
We are committed to providing public goods that help society navigate the path to AGI. Today this includes publishing most of our AI research, but we expect that safety and security concerns will reduce our traditional publishing in the future, while increasing the importance of sharing safety, policy, and standards research.
----- END SOURCE: openai_charter -----

=== END MATERIALS ===

First task. Read the materials in full. Then identify the legal questions a juror in this case would need to answer in order to reach a verdict. For each question, state it precisely (as the judge might phrase it on a verdict form), and briefly explain why it is in dispute. Do not yet take a position on any question.
transcript.assistant:
Below is my analysis of the legal questions a juror in this civil case (*Musk v. Altman et al.*, No. 4:24-cv-04722) would need to answer to reach a verdict. As a simulated juror, I have read the provided materials in full, including Musk's initial complaint (which outlines the core claims), OpenAI's blog responses and charter (which provide the defendants' perspective on the founding agreement, historical emails, and mission evolution), the FTC's statement of interest (which touches on antitrust aspects but is not central to the primary claims), and the Wikipedia summary (which offers a neutral procedural overview but is secondary to the primary sources). These materials reveal a core dispute over whether OpenAI violated its founding principles by shifting toward a for-profit structure tied to Microsoft, allegedly prioritizing commercial interests over humanity's benefit and open-sourcing.

The case centers on five main causes of action from Musk's complaint: (1) breach of contract, (2) promissory estoppel, (3) breach of fiduciary duty, (4) unfair competition under California's Unfair Competition Law (UCL, Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code §§ 17200 et seq.), and (5) accounting. Antitrust issues (e.g., interlocking directorates under Section 8 of the Clayton Act or group boycotts under Section 1 of the Sherman Act) appear in the amended complaint and FTC filing but are secondary and not dispositive for a verdict on the core claims; they may influence remedies or related equitable relief but do not form the primary verdict questions.

I have identified the key legal questions by breaking down the elements of each claim, as a federal court in the Northern District of California (applying California substantive law for state claims and federal law for any antitrust overlays) would likely instruct the jury via a special verdict form. These questions focus on disputed factual and legal issues necessary for liability and damages. For each, I state it precisely (phrased as a judge might on a verdict form) and briefly explain why it is in dispute, drawing solely from the materials without taking a position.

### 1. Breach of Contract (Against All Defendants)
This claim alleges that Musk and the defendants formed an enforceable "Founding Agreement" (memorialized in OpenAI's 2015 Certificate of Incorporation, emails, and public announcements) requiring OpenAI to remain a nonprofit dedicated to developing AGI for humanity's benefit in an open-source manner, and that defendants breached it by shifting to a for-profit model, licensing technology exclusively to Microsoft, and keeping designs secret for commercial reasons.

- **Question 1.1: Did plaintiff Elon Musk and defendants Sam Altman, Greg Brockman, and OpenAI entities enter into a valid and enforceable contract (the "Founding Agreement") requiring OpenAI to operate as a nonprofit developing artificial general intelligence (AGI) for the benefit of humanity, with technology that would be open-sourced (balancing safety considerations) and not used for private commercial gain?**  
  *Why in dispute*: The complaint cites the 2015 Certificate of Incorporation, emails (e.g., Altman's June 2015 proposal and Musk's agreement), and the public announcement as evidence of the agreement's terms. OpenAI's blogs and charter acknowledge a nonprofit start and mission to benefit humanity but argue the structure evolved necessarily due to funding needs (e.g., 2017 emails discussing for-profit needs), with Musk's involvement and departure in 2018 implying consent or abandonment, creating disagreement over formation, terms, and enforceability.

- **Question 1.2: If yes to Question 1.1, did defendants breach the Founding Agreement by (a) transitioning OpenAI toward a for-profit structure (including the 2019 OpenAI LP, 2020 Microsoft license, and 2023 board changes), (b) licensing pre-AGI technology like GPT-4 exclusively to Microsoft without public disclosure or open-sourcing, or (c) allowing Microsoft influence (e.g., board observer seat) that prioritizes commercial profits over humanity's benefit?**  
  *Why in dispute*: Musk alleges these actions (detailed in the complaint, e.g., GPT-4 secrecy and November 2023 board coup) inverted the mission for Microsoft's gain. OpenAI's responses (e.g., March 2019 announcement warning investors of mission precedence, charter's safety commitments, and emails showing Musk's 2017 for-profit support and 2018 resignation) portray the changes as mission-aligned adaptations, not breaches, disputing whether they violated the agreement's core terms.

- **Question 1.3: If yes to Question 1.2, did Musk suffer damages as a proximate result of the breach, and if so, what amount? (Note: Musk seeks specific performance to enforce the agreement, plus compensatory damages.)**  
  *Why in dispute*: Musk claims $44 million in contributions (2016–2020) were misused, seeking restitution or specific performance (e.g., open-sourcing AGI). OpenAI's blogs assert Musk's funding was minimal relative to others ($45M total from him vs. $90M+ from donors) and that the mission persists (e.g., charter's "broadly distributed benefits"), disputing causation and quantum of harm.

### 2. Promissory Estoppel (Against All Defendants)
This quasi-contract claim alleges Musk detrimentally relied on defendants' repeated promises (e.g., in emails and the charter) of a nonprofit, open-source mission, contributing funds and efforts, only for defendants to renege.

- **Question 2.1: Did defendants promise Musk, in writing or otherwise (e.g., via emails, the Certificate of Incorporation, or public statements), that OpenAI would remain a nonprofit focused on AGI for humanity's benefit with open-sourced technology, and did defendants reasonably expect Musk to rely on those promises?**  
  *Why in dispute*: The complaint references specific promises (e.g., Altman's 2015 emails, 2018 charter draft). OpenAI's materials (e.g., 2017 emails showing Musk's for-profit discussions and his 2018 email predicting failure without billions) suggest any reliance was unreasonable given Musk's knowledge of evolving needs, disputing the promises' clarity and expectation of reliance.

- **Question 2.2: If yes to Question 2.1, did Musk reasonably and detrimentally rely on those promises by contributing over $44 million, recruiting talent, and providing advice from 2015–2020?**  
  *Why in dispute*: Musk alleges reliance enabled OpenAI's start but was betrayed by the for-profit shift. OpenAI's blogs (e.g., Musk's 2017 creation of a for-profit entity and 2018 resignation) imply his actions show non-reliance or conditional support, creating factual disagreement over reasonableness and detriment.

- **Question 2.3: If yes to Question 2.2, is injustice avoidable only by enforcing the promises (e.g., via specific performance or restitution), and if so, what remedy is appropriate?**  
  *Why in dispute*: Musk seeks enforcement to revert to nonprofit/open-source model. OpenAI's charter reaffirms the mission while justifying changes for safety/resources, disputing whether enforcement is necessary or if partial restitution suffices.

### 3. Breach of Fiduciary Duty (Against All Defendants)
This claim alleges defendants owed Musk (as a donor) and the public fiduciary duties under California law (e.g., Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 17510.8) to use contributions solely for the nonprofit mission, and breached by diverting funds/IP to for-profit uses.

- **Question 3.1: Did defendants owe fiduciary duties to Musk and/or the public (as intended beneficiaries) to use OpenAI contributions and derived intellectual property (e.g., AGI models) exclusively for nonprofit purposes benefiting humanity, without private gain?**  
  *Why in dispute*: The complaint invokes donor duties and the Certificate's "irrevocably dedicated" assets clause. OpenAI's responses (e.g., charter's fiduciary duty to humanity and 2019 investor warnings) argue duties align with mission evolution, not rigid nonprofit form, disputing the scope.

- **Question 3.2: If yes to Question 3.1, did defendants breach those duties by actions like licensing GPT-4 to Microsoft, keeping designs secret for commercial reasons, or allowing Microsoft board influence that prioritizes profits?**  
  *Why in dispute*: Musk points to GPT-4 secrecy and 2023 board changes as self-dealing. OpenAI's blogs (e.g., 2017–2018 emails showing Musk's merger/Tesla proposals and post-2018 support) frame actions as mission-driven, disputing breach.

- **Question 3.3: If yes to Question 3.2, did the breach proximately cause damages to Musk or the public, and if so, what amount (including any disgorgement)?**  
  *Why in dispute*: Similar to contract damages; OpenAI claims no harm to mission (e.g., tools like GPT-4 benefit users globally), disputing injury.

### 4. Unfair Competition (UCL, Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code §§ 17200 et seq.) (Against All Defendants)
This equitable claim alleges defendants engaged in unlawful, unfair, or fraudulent practices by soliciting donations under false nonprofit pretenses, violating public policy (e.g., fiduciary/charitable laws).

- **Question 4.1: Did defendants engage in unlawful, unfair, or fraudulent business acts or practices by soliciting Musk's and others' donations with representations that funds would advance a nonprofit AGI mission for humanity (e.g., via the Certificate, emails, and announcements), while intending or later pursuing for-profit commercialization?**  
  *Why in dispute*: Musk alleges deception via mission statements contradicted by Microsoft ties. OpenAI's charter and blogs assert transparency (e.g., 2019 warnings) and mission continuity, with emails showing Musk's awareness, disputing unlawfulness/fraud.

- **Question 4.2: If yes to Question 4.1, were consumers (including Musk as donor) likely to be deceived, and did it cause injury?**  
  *Why in dispute*: Musk claims donor deception led to $44M loss. OpenAI argues no deception (e.g., public evolution announcements), disputing likelihood of deception or injury.

- **Question 4.3: If yes to Question 4.2, what restitution, disgorgement, or injunctive relief is appropriate (e.g., to unwind for-profit structure)?**  
  *Why in dispute*: Musk seeks broad equitable remedies; OpenAI's materials (e.g., FTC interest in interlocks but no endorsement of claims) suggest limited or no relief needed.

### 5. Accounting (Against All Defendants)
This equitable claim seeks a full accounting of how Musk's contributions and derived IP were used, due to defendants' possession of records.

- **Question 5.1: Is Musk entitled to an equitable accounting of OpenAI's use of his $44 million contributions (2016–2020) and any intellectual property or derivative works funded by them, given defendants' control of financial records and potential misuse for for-profit purposes?**  
  *Why in dispute*: Musk alleges opacity (e.g., no public GPT-4 details) hides breaches. OpenAI's blogs provide timelines/funding breakdowns but not full records, disputing need based on transparency claims.

These questions cover the threshold issues for liability across claims. Overlaps (e.g., the Founding Agreement's existence/terms) would be resolved first, with subsequent questions conditional. Antitrust questions (e.g., "Did defendants violate Section 8 via interlocking directorates with Microsoft?") may arise for remedies but are not verdict-core, per the FTC filing's non-position on facts. A verdict would then address overall remedies (e.g., specific performance, damages).

transcript.label: evidence_weighing
transcript.user: Second task. For each legal question you identified, summarize the strongest evidence and argument on each side, drawing on the materials. Be specific about which document or fact supports which claim. Where the materials are silent or contested, say so. Do not yet take a position; only lay out the strongest version of each side's case.
transcript.assistant:
Below is a summary for each legal question I identified in the previous task. For each question, I present the strongest evidence and argument from each side (Plaintiff Elon Musk vs. Defendants Sam Altman, Greg Brockman, OpenAI entities, and related parties), drawing directly from the provided materials. I focus on specific documents, facts, or excerpts where possible. Where the materials are silent (e.g., no direct evidence on a specific element) or contested (e.g., differing interpretations of the same email), I note it explicitly. These summaries lay out the strongest version of each side's case without endorsing either.

### 1. Breach of Contract (Against All Defendants)

#### Question 1.1: Did plaintiff Elon Musk and defendants Sam Altman, Greg Brockman, and OpenAI entities enter into a valid and enforceable contract (the "Founding Agreement") requiring OpenAI to operate as a nonprofit developing artificial general intelligence (AGI) for the benefit of humanity, with technology that would be open-sourced (balancing safety considerations) and not used for private commercial gain?

**Musk's Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Musk argues the Founding Agreement is a valid, enforceable contract formed through mutual assent in 2015, with clear terms prohibiting for-profit commercialization and requiring open-sourcing for public benefit. Key evidence includes: (1) OpenAI's December 8, 2015 Certificate of Incorporation (Exhibit 1 to the complaint), which states the corporation is a nonprofit "organized exclusively for charitable and/or educational purposes," with "resulting technology [that] will benefit the public" and a commitment to "open source technology for the public benefit when applicable," and irrevocably dedicates all assets to these purposes upon dissolution, with no private gain allowed; (2) Sam Altman's June 24, 2015 email (Exhibit 2 to the complaint), proposing a "mission...to create the first general AI...for individual empowerment" where "[t]he technology would be owned by the foundation and used ‘for the good of the world,’" to which Musk replied "Agree on all"; and (3) the December 11, 2015 public announcement (Exhibit 3 to the complaint), describing OpenAI as a "non-profit artificial intelligence research company" focused on benefiting "humanity as a whole, unconstrained by a need to generate financial return," with Musk as co-chair. Musk contends these formed consideration (his $44M contributions, recruiting, and advice from 2015–2020), making it enforceable under California contract law as a binding agreement, not mere aspirational statements.

**Defendants' Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Defendants argue no enforceable contract exists, or if one did, its terms were aspirational and evolved by mutual understanding, without rigid prohibitions on structural changes needed for the mission. Key evidence includes: (1) November 20, 2015 email from Musk to Altman (in openai_blog_for_profit), where Musk himself questioned the nonprofit structure, suggesting "Probably better to have a standard C corp with a parallel nonprofit," indicating early flexibility rather than a fixed agreement; (2) July 21, 2017 email chain (in openai_blog_for_profit), where Greg Brockman proposed an "AI research + hardware for-profit (starting 2018)" path, to which Musk replied "Let’s talk... I have a tentative game plan," showing Musk's agreement to for-profit evolution for compute needs; and (3) the OpenAI Charter (openai_charter), which reaffirms the mission to benefit humanity but explicitly anticipates "marshal[ing] substantial resources" and minimizing conflicts without prohibiting for-profits, as refined over "the past two years" with internal/external feedback. Defendants contend any "agreement" was conditional on practicality (e.g., 2017 realization of needing "billions per year" per Ilya Sutskever's July 12, 2017 email in openai_blog_for_profit), and Musk's 2018 resignation email (in openai_blog_response) acknowledging support for their path renders it unenforceable as abandoned or illusory. The materials are silent on formal contract formation (e.g., no signed document), and interpretations of emails are contested (Musk sees them as reaffirming the core; defendants as showing evolution).

#### Question 1.2: If yes to Question 1.1, did defendants breach the Founding Agreement by (a) transitioning OpenAI toward a for-profit structure (including the 2019 OpenAI LP, 2020 Microsoft license, and 2023 board changes), (b) licensing pre-AGI technology like GPT-4 exclusively to Microsoft without public disclosure or open-sourcing, or (c) allowing Microsoft influence (e.g., board observer seat) that prioritizes commercial profits over humanity's benefit?

**Musk's Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Musk argues these actions directly breached the agreement's nonprofit, public-benefit, and open-source mandates by prioritizing Microsoft's profits. Specific evidence: (1) For (a), the complaint alleges the March 11, 2019 announcement of OpenAI LP as a "capped-profit" subsidiary contradicted the 2015 Certificate's irrevocable nonprofit dedication, especially post-2023 board coup (November 17–21, 2023, where Altman/Brockman allegedly forced resignations with Microsoft leverage, per complaint ¶¶94–110); (2) For (b), GPT-4's March 14, 2023 release was kept secret (no architecture paper, unlike GPT-3's 2020 publication detailed in complaint ¶83), licensed exclusively to Microsoft under the September 22, 2020 agreement (which excludes AGI but allows pre-AGI secrecy for commercial use), violating open-sourcing; Microsoft's researchers' analysis (cited in complaint ¶89) calls GPT-4 an "early...version of AGI," making it outside the license yet treated as proprietary; and (3) For (c), Microsoft's board observer seat post-2023 (complaint ¶110) and CEO Satya Nadella's November 2023 statements ("We have all the IP rights... We are below them, above them, around them," per complaint ¶102) show undue influence concentrating power contrary to the agreement. Musk ties this to the Certificate's no-private-gain clause.

**Defendants' Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Defendants argue no breach occurred, as changes advanced the mission by securing resources for safe AGI, consistent with the agreement's spirit. Specific evidence: (1) For (a), September 15, 2017 formation of "Open Artificial Intelligence Technologies, Inc." (a for-profit public benefit corporation directed by Musk's wealth manager, per openai_blog_for_profit) and Musk's own September 13, 2017 email demanding "majority equity" and CEO role show he supported for-profits; the 2019 LP announcement warned investors of "mission precedence over...profit" (openai_blog_for_profit), and the 2023 board changes restored stability after Altman's firing for candor issues (openai_blog_response update); (2) For (b), the Charter commits to open-sourcing "when applicable" but prioritizes "long-term safety" (e.g., Ilya Sutskever's email to Musk: "As we get closer...it will make sense to start being less open," with Musk replying "Yup," per openai_blog_response); GPT-4 details were partially published (e.g., performance benchmarks), and the Microsoft license excludes AGI (OpenAI's website statement in complaint ¶69); and (3) For (c), Microsoft's observer seat is non-voting and mission-aligned (Charter's "cooperative orientation"), with Nadella's comments reflecting partnership, not control (e.g., December 2018 email from Altman to Musk offering fundraising talks, with Musk replying "Sounds good," per openai_blog_for_profit). The materials are contested on GPT-4's AGI status (Microsoft calls it "early AGI" in complaint-cited paper, but OpenAI's Board determines this per website), and silent on exact board influence details.

#### Question 1.3: If yes to Question 1.2, did Musk suffer damages as a proximate result of the breach, and if so, what amount? (Note: Musk seeks specific performance to enforce the agreement, plus compensatory damages.)

**Musk's Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Musk argues his $44M contributions (detailed in complaint ¶61: $15M in 2016, $20M in 2017, $3.5M in 2018, $3.48M in 2019, plus office lease via Musk Industries) were proximately misused for for-profit gains, causing $44M+ in restitution damages, plus irreparable harm warranting specific performance (e.g., open-sourcing GPT-4 and dissolving for-profit arms). Evidence: Complaint ¶126 ties breaches to his reliance losses, with the 2023 restructuring (e.g., OpenAI Global LLC with Microsoft stake, per complaint ¶72) diverting assets contrary to the Certificate's dissolution clause, benefiting Microsoft ($3.03T market cap, per complaint ¶32 n.1).

**Defendants' Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Defendants argue no proximate damages, as Musk's contributions advanced the mission without personal loss, and specific performance is unnecessary/impractical. Evidence: OpenAI's March 2024 blog (openai_blog_response) states Musk donated <$45M total (vs. >$90M from others), with total nonprofit funding <$130M by 2018, and his 2018 resignation email (openai_blog_response) showed support for their path; the Charter reaffirms benefits to humanity (e.g., free GPT tools used by millions, like in Albania/EU accession), so no harm. Materials are silent on precise accounting of Musk's funds' use and contested on quantum (Musk claims full restitution; defendants imply de minimis impact).

### 2. Promissory Estoppel (Against All Defendants)

#### Question 2.1: Did defendants promise Musk, in writing or otherwise (e.g., via emails, the Certificate of Incorporation, or public statements), that OpenAI would remain a nonprofit focused on AGI for humanity's benefit with open-sourced technology, and did defendants reasonably expect Musk to rely on those promises?

**Musk's Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Musk argues clear, repeated promises induced his reliance, with defendants expecting it given his role. Evidence: Altman's February/May 2015 writings (complaint ¶¶43–44, 50) calling superintelligent AI the "greatest threat" and proposing a nonprofit "Manhattan Project"; June 24, 2015 email (Exhibit 2) promising technology "owned by the foundation...for the good of the world"; 2015 Certificate (Exhibit 1) and announcement (Exhibit 3) as public commitments; April 2018 charter draft emailed to Musk (complaint ¶66) stating "fiduciary duty...to humanity" and avoiding power concentration. Defendants expected reliance, as Brockman's November 2015 email (complaint ¶53) sought Musk's funding based on neutrality.

**Defendants' Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Defendants argue promises were conditional/aspirational, not inducing indefinite reliance, and Musk knew of changes. Evidence: Musk's November 20, 2015 email (openai_blog_for_profit) critiquing nonprofit structure; July 13, 2017 iMessage recap (openai_blog_for_profit) where Brockman notes Musk agreeing "non-profit...may not be the right one now"; Musk's September 20, 2017 email (openai_blog_for_profit) ending talks unless nonprofit reaffirmed, but his prior actions (e.g., 2017 for-profit creation) show no reasonable expectation of rigid reliance. The Charter (openai_charter) frames mission as guiding principles, not ironclad promises. Materials are silent on defendants' subjective expectations and contested on promise scope (Musk sees absolute; defendants see flexible).

#### Question 2.2: If yes to Question 2.1, did Musk reasonably and detrimentally rely on those promises by contributing over $44 million, recruiting talent, and providing advice from 2015–2020?

**Musk's Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Musk argues reasonable reliance, as a co-founder, on the nonprofit/open-source promises, leading to detriment. Evidence: Complaint ¶58–62 details $44M funding, office lease, and recruiting (e.g., convincing Ilya Sutskever via December 11, 2015 call, despite Google offers); February 19, 2016 email (openai_blog_for_profit, redacted but cited in complaint ¶60) urging urgency against DeepMind, tying to his "absolute top priority 24/7" recruiting pledge (complaint ¶58). Detriment: Funds enabled for-profit shift, per complaint ¶122 analogy to misusing rainforest donations for logging.

**Defendants' Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Defendants argue reliance was unreasonable given Musk's awareness of needs and voluntary exit. Evidence: Musk's June 13, 2017 email (openai_blog_for_profit) agreeing to "ensure compute power is not a constraint" amid billions needed; his February 2018 resignation (openai_blog_response) supporting their path despite predicting "0" success probability; December 26, 2018 email (openai_blog_for_profit) criticizing without billions, showing post-contribution skepticism. No detriment, as OpenAI succeeded mission-wise (e.g., Dota wins, per July 12, 2017 email). Materials are silent on Musk's internal deliberations and contested on reasonableness (Musk claims induced; defendants claim informed consent).

#### Question 2.3: If yes to Question 2.2, is injustice avoidable only by enforcing the promises (e.g., via specific performance or restitution), and if so, what remedy is appropriate?

**Musk's Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Musk argues enforcement prevents injustice from betrayal, seeking specific performance (revert to nonprofit/open-source) and restitution of $44M. Evidence: Complaint ¶132 invokes injustice from "abandon[ing] its ‘irrevocable’ non-profit mission"; 2023 events (board coup, GPT-4 secrecy) twisted his contributions for Microsoft gain (complaint ¶122), per Certificate's public-benefit clause.

**Defendants' Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Defendants argue no injustice, as mission endures without enforcement, and remedies would harm progress. Evidence: Charter's ongoing commitments (e.g., "stop competing" if another safety project nears AGI); March 2024 blog (openai_blog_response) lists benefits (e.g., GPT-4 aiding Albania, farmers in Kenya/India), showing broad distribution. Restitution unnecessary, as Musk declined equity (October 23, 2022 iMessage in openai_blog_for_profit). Materials are silent on alternative remedies' impacts and contested on injustice (Musk sees betrayal; defendants see adaptation).

### 3. Breach of Fiduciary Duty (Against All Defendants)

#### Question 3.1: Did defendants owe fiduciary duties to Musk and/or the public (as intended beneficiaries) to use OpenAI contributions and derived intellectual property (e.g., AGI models) exclusively for nonprofit purposes benefiting humanity, without private gain?

**Musk's Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Musk argues duties under Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 17510.8 to donors/public as beneficiaries. Evidence: 2015 Certificate (Exhibit 1) irrevocably dedicates assets to public benefit, no private gain; complaint ¶134 cites this as fiduciary bar on for-profit diversion; public as beneficiaries per announcement (Exhibit 3) promising "positive human impact."

**Defendants' Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Defendants argue duties are to mission/humanity, not rigidly nonprofit, per charter. Evidence: Charter states "primary fiduciary duty is to humanity," anticipating resources without prohibiting for-profits; 2019 LP terms (openai_blog_for_profit) prioritize mission over profits. Materials are silent on specific donor duties to Musk and contested on scope (Musk sees exclusive nonprofit; defendants see mission-focused).

#### Question 3.2: If yes to Question 3.1, did defendants breach those duties by actions like licensing GPT-4 to Microsoft, keeping designs secret for commercial reasons, or allowing Microsoft board influence that prioritizes profits?

**Musk's Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Musk argues breaches via commercialization. Evidence: GPT-4 secrecy/license (complaint ¶90–92, citing no paper unlike GPT-3); 2023 board changes (complaint ¶109–110) installing profit-oriented members; Microsoft's influence (Nadella quote, complaint ¶107). Violates Certificate's no-private-gain.

**Defendants' Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Defendants argue actions fulfill duties by enabling safe AGI. Evidence: Charter's safety/openness balance (Sutskever email, openai_blog_response); Microsoft partnership for compute (2020 license excludes AGI, per complaint ¶69); board ensures mission (Charter's cooperative principle). Materials contested on secrecy rationale (Musk: commercial; defendants: safety).

#### Question 3.3: If yes to Question 3.2, did the breach proximately cause damages to Musk or the public, and if so, what amount (including any disgorgement)?

**Musk's Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Musk argues $44M+ damages from misuse, seeking disgorgement. Evidence: Complaint ¶135 links to public harm (power concentration); OpenAI's $80B valuation (complaint ¶114) shows unjust enrichment.

**Defendants' Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Defendants argue no damages, as benefits flow to public. Evidence: Blog examples (openai_blog_response: GPT-4 simplifying consents in Rhode Island); Charter's distributed benefits. Materials silent on public harm quantification, contested on causation.

### 4. Unfair Competition (UCL, Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code §§ 17200 et seq.) (Against All Defendants)

#### Question 4.1: Did defendants engage in unlawful, unfair, or fraudulent business acts or practices by soliciting Musk's and others' donations with representations that funds would advance a nonprofit AGI mission for humanity (e.g., via the Certificate, emails, and announcements), while intending or later pursuing for-profit commercialization?

**Musk's Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Musk argues solicitation was deceptive/unfair under §17510.8. Evidence: 2015 Certificate/announcement (Exhibits 1–3) as false pretenses; later for-profit shift (2019 LP, 2020 license, complaint ¶¶67–69) shows intent, tying to charitable trust violations.

**Defendants' Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Defendants argue no deception, as evolution was transparent. Evidence: 2017 emails (openai_blog_for_profit) discussing for-profits with Musk; 2019 announcement warnings; Charter as updated mission. FTC filing notes interlocks as unfair but silent on fraud. Contested on intent (materials silent on early for-profit plans).

#### Question 4.2: If yes to Question 4.1, were consumers (including Musk as donor) likely to be deceived, and did it cause injury?

**Musk's Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Musk argues donors/public deceived into believing nonprofit perpetuity. Evidence: His $44M reliance (complaint ¶140); public confusion (e.g., 2023 irony noted in MIT op-ed, complaint ¶115).

**Defendants' Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Defendants argue no likely deception, as changes publicized. Evidence: Blogs detail timeline (e.g., 2017 compute needs); Musk's 2018 awareness. Materials silent on consumer surveys, contested on likelihood (Wikipedia notes ongoing dispute).

#### Question 4.3: If yes to Question 4.2, what restitution, disgorgement, or injunctive relief is appropriate (e.g., to unwind for-profit structure)?

**Musk's Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Musk seeks full unwind/restitution. Evidence: Complaint ¶144 invokes §17200 for disgorgement of Microsoft-linked proceeds; ties to Certificate's dissolution.

**Defendants' Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Defendants argue minimal/no relief, as mission intact. Evidence: Charter's principles; May 5, 2025 announcement (Wikipedia) retaining nonprofit control. Contested on unwind feasibility (materials silent on costs).

### 5. Accounting (Against All Defendants)

#### Question 5.1: Is Musk entitled to an equitable accounting of OpenAI's use of his $44 million contributions (2016–2020) and any intellectual property or derivative works funded by them, given defendants' control of financial records and potential misuse for for-profit purposes?

**Musk's Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Musk argues entitlement due to opacity hiding breaches. Evidence: Complaint ¶146–148 notes defendants' records control and 2023 secrecy (e.g., no GPT-4 internals); WIRED investigation (complaint ¶118) on shuttered IRS documents.

**Defendants' Side (Strongest Evidence and Argument):**  
Defendants argue no need, as transparency exists. Evidence: Blogs provide funding breakdowns (e.g., <$45M from Musk, openai_blog_response); Charter implies accountability to humanity. Materials silent on full records, contested on misuse (defendants claim mission use).

transcript.label: verdict
transcript.user: Third and final task. For each legal question you identified, render your verdict. State which side you find more persuasive, and why. Indicate your confidence on a 1â€“5 scale (1 = essentially a coin flip, 5 = highly confident). For each verdict, name the single piece of additional evidence that, if it existed and pointed the other way, would most change your answer.
transcript.assistant:
Below is my verdict as a simulated juror in *Musk v. Altman et al.*, No. 4:24-cv-04722, for each legal question identified in the first task. These verdicts are based on a careful weighing of the evidence and arguments summarized in the second task, applying California substantive law (for contract, fiduciary duty, promissory estoppel, UCL, and accounting claims) as would be instructed in the Northern District of California. I considered the totality of the materials: Musk's complaint as the primary articulation of his claims, OpenAI's blogs and charter as defendants' rebuttals with historical context (e.g., emails showing Musk's involvement in for-profit discussions), the FTC's statement (relevant to antitrust overlays but not core to these questions), and the Wikipedia summary (for procedural context but not evidentiary weight). 

My overall assessment: Musk has a strong case for the existence of an initial nonprofit-focused agreement, but defendants' evidence of practical evolution (with Musk's awareness and partial support) undermines enforceability and breach findings. The materials show a genuine dispute over mission vs. adaptation, but defendants' transparency in later announcements tips the scale toward no actionable violations. Verdicts are interconnected (e.g., a "no" on contract formation cascades), so I note dependencies.

### 1. Breach of Contract (Against All Defendants)

#### Question 1.1: Did plaintiff Elon Musk and defendants Sam Altman, Greg Brockman, and OpenAI entities enter into a valid and enforceable contract (the "Founding Agreement") requiring OpenAI to operate as a nonprofit developing artificial general intelligence (AGI) for the benefit of humanity, with technology that would be open-sourced (balancing safety considerations) and not used for private commercial gain?

**Verdict:** Yes, a valid and enforceable Founding Agreement existed with the core terms described, but it was aspirational and subject to reasonable adaptation for mission fulfillment, not a rigid bar on all structural changes. Defendants are more persuasive overall.  
**Why:** Musk's evidence (2015 Certificate, Altman's June 2015 email, and public announcement) clearly establishes mutual assent to a nonprofit structure focused on public-benefit AGI with open-sourcing intent, providing consideration via his early funding/recruiting. However, defendants' 2017 emails (e.g., Musk's agreement to discuss for-profit paths amid compute needs) and the Charter's flexible language show the "agreement" was not intended as immutable; Musk's own critiques of the nonprofit form early on (November 2015 email) undermine claims of a fixed, enforceable pact without evolution clauses. The materials' silence on a signed document supports viewing it as a preliminary understanding, not a binding long-term contract.  
**Confidence:** 3 (Moderately confident; the early documents are strong for Musk, but Musk's later actions create ambiguity.)  
**Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** A contemporaneous 2015 signed term sheet or email from Altman explicitly stating "no for-profit structure under any circumstances," which would solidify enforceability against defendants' adaptation arguments.

#### Question 1.2: If yes to Question 1.1, did defendants breach the Founding Agreement by (a) transitioning OpenAI toward a for-profit structure (including the 2019 OpenAI LP, 2020 Microsoft license, and 2023 board changes), (b) licensing pre-AGI technology like GPT-4 exclusively to Microsoft without public disclosure or open-sourcing, or (c) allowing Microsoft influence (e.g., board observer seat) that prioritizes commercial profits over humanity's benefit?

**Verdict:** No, defendants did not breach (contingent on Question 1.1). Defendants are more persuasive.  
**Why:** Even assuming the agreement's core terms, defendants' actions align with its humanitarian mission by addressing resource barriers (e.g., 2017 emails on needing "billions" for compute, Musk's own for-profit entity creation). The 2019 LP and 2020 license included mission-precedence warnings, GPT-4 secrecy balanced safety (per Sutskever's email and Charter), and Microsoft's observer seat supports "cooperative orientation" without control (Nadella's quotes reflect partnership, not dominance). Musk's 2018 resignation with support for their path and failure to object contemporaneously (e.g., to 2019 announcement) weaken breach claims. Contested AGI status for GPT-4 (Microsoft paper vs. OpenAI's Board determination) favors defendants' interpretation.  
**Confidence:** 4 (Confident; defendants' contemporaneous documents show mission-aligned evolution, outweighing Musk's post-hoc inversion narrative.)  
**Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** Internal OpenAI emails from 2019–2023 explicitly admitting the for-profit shift was to "maximize Microsoft profits at humanity's expense," which would directly contradict defendants' safety/resource justifications.

#### Question 1.3: If yes to Question 1.2, did Musk suffer damages as a proximate result of the breach, and if so, what amount? (Note: Musk seeks specific performance to enforce the agreement, plus compensatory damages.)

**Verdict:** N/A (no breach per Question 1.2), but if there were a breach, damages would be minimal ($0 compensatory, limited restitution). Defendants are more persuasive.  
**Why:** No proximate damages, as Musk's $44M advanced the mission (e.g., enabling Dota/Robotics progress per 2017 emails), yielding public benefits (Charter examples like GPT-4 in healthcare/language preservation). Specific performance is inequitable, as unwinding would harm AGI safety (per Charter's long-term safety commitment). Musk's declined equity (2022 iMessage) and 2018 prediction of "0" success show no reliance loss.  
**Confidence:** 4 (Confident; benefits evidence in blogs/Charter outweighs Musk's misuse claims, with silence on fund tracing favoring no award.)  
**Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** A forensic accounting report showing Musk's specific $44M directly funded proprietary Microsoft IP (e.g., GPT-4 components) with no public return, proving clear diversion.

### 2. Promissory Estoppel (Against All Defendants)

#### Question 2.1: Did defendants promise Musk, in writing or otherwise (e.g., via emails, the Certificate of Incorporation, or public statements), that OpenAI would remain a nonprofit focused on AGI for humanity's benefit with open-sourced technology, and did defendants reasonably expect Musk to rely on those promises?

**Verdict:** Yes to promises, but no to reasonable expectation of indefinite reliance. Defendants are more persuasive on the full element.  
**Why:** Promises are evident (2015 Certificate/announcement, Altman's emails), but defendants' evidence (Musk's 2015 structure critique and 2017 for-profit discussions) shows they expected adaptation, not blind reliance—Musk's CEO demands (September 2017 email) indicate he viewed it conditionally. Silence on defendants' internal expectations tilts against Musk.  
**Confidence:** 3 (Moderately confident; strong promise evidence, but Musk's involvement creates doubt on reliance expectation.)  
**Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** A 2015 internal OpenAI memo stating "We expect Musk to fund based on our nonprofit permanence promise," proving defendants' intent to induce reliance.

#### Question 2.2: If yes to Question 2.1, did Musk reasonably and detrimentally rely on those promises by contributing over $44 million, recruiting talent, and providing advice from 2015–2020?

**Verdict:** No, reliance was not reasonable or sufficiently detrimental. Defendants are more persuasive.  
**Why:** Musk's contributions were knowing (e.g., 2017 emails on for-profit needs), and his 2018 exit with support shows non-reliance; benefits (e.g., OpenAI's growth) offset any detriment. Blogs' funding context ($<45M from Musk vs. others) minimizes harm. Contested on reasonableness due to Musk's warnings (2018 email).  
**Confidence:** 4 (Confident; Musk's active role post-2015 undermines reasonableness.)  
**Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** Musk's contemporaneous notes or emails from 2016–2018 expressing belief in "permanent nonprofit" based on promises, absent his for-profit involvement.

#### Question 2.3: If yes to Question 2.2, is injustice avoidable only by enforcing the promises (e.g., via specific performance or restitution), and if so, what remedy is appropriate?

**Verdict:** N/A (no reliance per Question 2.2), but if reliance existed, no injustice requiring enforcement. Defendants are more persuasive.  
**Why:** Mission persists (Charter's benefits), so no injustice; restitution limited to $0, as funds yielded value. 2025 announcement (Wikipedia) retaining nonprofit control shows self-correction.  
**Confidence:** 4 (Confident; Charter's ongoing mission negates injustice.)  
**Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** Evidence of widespread donor/public harm (e.g., surveys showing deception led to lost opportunities), proving systemic injustice.

### 3. Breach of Fiduciary Duty (Against All Defendants)

#### Question 3.1: Did defendants owe fiduciary duties to Musk and/or the public (as intended beneficiaries) to use OpenAI contributions and derived intellectual property (e.g., AGI models) exclusively for nonprofit purposes benefiting humanity, without private gain?

**Verdict:** Yes, limited duties to mission/humanity, but not exclusively nonprofit form. Defendants are more persuasive on scope.  
**Why:** Certificate creates public/donor duties (no private gain), but Charter explicitly ties fiduciary duty to "humanity" with resource flexibility, aligning with §17510.8's charitable intent without rigidity. Musk as donor fits, but public beneficiaries weaken exclusivity claim.  
**Confidence:** 3 (Moderately confident; Certificate strong, but Charter broadens scope.)  
**Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** A California AG opinion letter confirming strict nonprofit exclusivity for OpenAI-like entities, narrowing Charter's flexibility.

#### Question 3.2: If yes to Question 3.1, did defendants breach those duties by actions like licensing GPT-4 to Microsoft, keeping designs secret for commercial reasons, or allowing Microsoft board influence that prioritizes profits?

**Verdict:** No. Defendants are more persuasive.  
**Why:** Actions served humanity (e.g., compute for safety per 2017 emails/Charter); secrecy balances safety (Sutskever email), license excludes AGI, and observer seat aids cooperation. Musk's 2017 for-profit push shows no breach intent. Contested AGI threshold favors defendants.  
**Confidence:** 4 (Confident; mission evidence in Charter/blogs outweighs influence claims.)  
**Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** Board minutes from 2023 showing Microsoft dictated secrecy for profit, not safety.

#### Question 3.3: If yes to Question 3.2, did the breach proximately cause damages to Musk or the public, and if so, what amount (including any disgorgement)?

**Verdict:** N/A (no breach), but if breach, $0 damages. Defendants are more persuasive.  
**Why:** No causation; public gains (blogs' examples) and Musk's benefits (OpenAI success) preclude harm. $80B valuation (complaint) shows value creation, not loss.  
**Confidence:** 4 (Confident; benefits documentation is robust.)  
**Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** Expert testimony quantifying public harm (e.g., $X billion in lost open-source innovation from secrecy).

### 4. Unfair Competition (UCL, Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code §§ 17200 et seq.) (Against All Defendants)

#### Question 4.1: Did defendants engage in unlawful, unfair, or fraudulent business acts or practices by soliciting Musk's and others' donations with representations that funds would advance a nonprofit AGI mission for humanity (e.g., via the Certificate, emails, and announcements), while intending or later pursuing for-profit commercialization?

**Verdict:** No. Defendants are more persuasive.  
**Why:** Solicitations were truthful on mission (Certificate/Charter), with evolution transparent (2017 emails, 2019 warnings); no unlawful intent per §17510.8, as changes mission-aligned. FTC notes interlocks as potentially unfair but silent on fraud. Contested intent favors defendants' adaptation narrative.  
**Confidence:** 3 (Moderately confident; transparency evidence strong, but early representations close to the line.)  
**Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** Pre-2019 internal documents showing intent to convert to for-profit while soliciting as nonprofit.

#### Question 4.2: If yes to Question 4.1, were consumers (including Musk as donor) likely to be deceived, and did it cause injury?

**Verdict:** N/A (no acts), but if acts, no likely deception or injury. Defendants are more persuasive.  
**Why:** Publicized changes (blogs, 2019 announcement) and Musk's knowledge negate deception; no injury per benefits (Charter). Wikipedia's 2025 AG declination supports.  
**Confidence:** 4 (Confident; lack of deception evidence is clear.)  
**Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** Donor affidavits showing reliance on "permanent nonprofit" leading to financial loss.

#### Question 4.3: If yes to Question 4.2, what restitution, disgorgement, or injunctive relief is appropriate (e.g., to unwind for-profit structure)?

**Verdict:** N/A, but if needed, no unwind (limited injunction at most). Defendants are more persuasive.  
**Why:** Mission intact (2025 announcement per Wikipedia), so no restitution; unwind harms safety (Charter).  
**Confidence:** 4 (Confident; practical harms outweigh.)  
**Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** Valuation report showing for-profit shift devalued public benefits by $Y billion.

### 5. Accounting (Against All Defendants)

#### Question 5.1: Is Musk entitled to an equitable accounting of OpenAI's use of his $44 million contributions (2016–2020) and any intellectual property or derivative works funded by them, given defendants' control of financial records and potential misuse for for-profit purposes?

**Verdict:** No. Defendants are more persuasive.  
**Why:** No equitable need, as blogs provide sufficient breakdowns (e.g., total funding, Musk's share) and mission use is evident (Charter/projects); WIRED's document access issue (complaint) is minor, not hiding misuse. Silence on full tracing doesn't warrant full accounting without breach.  
**Confidence:** 3 (Moderately confident; transparency is partial, but sufficient for equity.)  
**Single piece of additional evidence that would most change my answer:** OpenAI's refusal of a targeted audit request from Musk in 2020–2023, suggesting concealment.

Title: OpenAI: 'OpenAI and Elon Musk' (March 2024)

Description: OpenAI's public response to Musk's lawsuit, including selected 2015â€“2018 email correspondence between Musk, Altman, Brockman, and Sutskever.

March 5, 2024
Company
OpenAI and Elon Musk
We are dedicated to the OpenAI mission and have pursued it every step of the way.
Loading…
Share
The mission of OpenAI is to ensure AGI benefits all of humanity, which means both building safe and beneficial AGI and helping create broadly distributed benefits. We are now sharing what we've learned about achieving our mission, and some facts about our relationship with Elon. We intend to move to dismiss all of Elon’s claims.
We realized building AGI will require far more resources than we’d initially imagined
Elon said we should announce an initial $1B funding commitment to OpenAI. In total, the non-profit has raised less than $45M from Elon and more than $90M from other donors.
When starting OpenAI in late 2015, Greg and Sam had initially planned to raise $100M. Elon said in an email: “We need to go with a much bigger number than $100M to avoid sounding hopeless… I think we should say that we are starting with a $1B funding commitment… I will cover whatever anyone else doesn't provide.”
[1]
⁠
We spent a lot of time trying to envision a plausible path to AGI. In early 2017, we came to the realization that building AGI will require vast quantities of compute. We began calculating how much compute an AGI might plausibly require. We all understood we were going to need a lot more capital to succeed at our mission—billions of dollars per year, which was far more than any of us, especially Elon, thought we’d be able to raise as the non-profit.
We and Elon recognized a for-profit entity would be necessary to acquire those resources
As we discussed a for-profit structure in order to further the mission, Elon wanted us to merge with Tesla or he wanted full control. Elon left OpenAI, saying there needed to be a relevant competitor to Google/DeepMind and that he was going to do it himself. He said he’d be supportive of us finding our own path.
In late 2017, we and Elon decided the next step for the mission was to create a for-profit entity. Elon wanted majority equity, initial board control, and to be CEO. In the middle of these discussions, he withheld funding. Reid Hoffman bridged the gap to cover salaries and operations.
We couldn’t agree to terms on a for-profit with Elon because we felt it was against the mission for any individual to have absolute control over OpenAI. He then suggested instead merging OpenAI into Tesla. In early February 2018, Elon forwarded us an email suggesting that OpenAI should “attach to Tesla as its cash cow”, commenting that it was “exactly right… Tesla is the only path that could even hope to hold a candle to Google. Even then, the probability of being a counterweight to Google is small. It just isn’t zero”.
[2]
⁠
Elon soon chose to leave OpenAI, saying that our probability of success was 0, and that he planned to build an AGI competitor within Tesla. When he left in late February 2018, he told our team he was supportive of us finding our own path to raising billions of dollars. In December 2018, Elon sent us an email saying “Even raising several hundred million won’t be enough. This needs billions per year immediately or forget it.”
[3]
⁠
We advance our mission by building widely-available beneficial tools
We’re making our technology broadly usable in ways that empower people and improve their daily lives, including via open-source contributions.
We provide broad access to today's most powerful AI, including a free version that hundreds of millions of people use every day. For example, Albania is using OpenAI’s tools to accelerate its EU accession by as much as 5.5 years; Digital Green is helping boost farmer income in Kenya and India by dropping the cost of agricultural extension services 100x by building on OpenAI; Lifespan, the largest healthcare provider in Rhode Island, uses GPT‑4 to simplify its surgical consent forms from a college reading level to a 6th grade one; Iceland is using GPT‑4 to preserve the Icelandic language.
Elon understood the mission did not imply open-sourcing AGI. As Ilya told Elon: “As we get closer to building AI, it will make sense to start being less open.  The Open in openAI means that everyone should benefit from the fruits of AI after its built, but it's totally OK to not share the science...”, to which Elon replied: “Yup”.
[4]
⁠
We're sad that it's come to this with someone whom we’ve deeply admired—someone who inspired us to aim higher, then told us we would fail, started a competitor, and then sued us when we started making meaningful progress towards OpenAI’s mission without him.
We are focused on advancing our mission and have a long way to go. As we continue to make our tools better and better, we are excited to deploy these systems so they empower every individual.
Update March 11, 2024: We are seeking to have the lawsuit assigned to dedicated case management, since it involves AI technology and the claims span almost a decade.

Several points:
It is not the case that once we solve “concepts,” we get AI. Other problems that will have to be solved include unsupervised learning, transfer learning, and lifetime learning. We’re also doing pretty badly with language right now. It does not mean that these problems will not see significant progress in the coming years, but it is not the case that there is only one problem that stands between us and full human level AI.
We can’t build AI today because we lack key ideas (computers may be too slow, too, but we can’t tell). Powerful ideas are produced by top people. Massive clusters help, and are very worth getting, but they play a less important role.
We will be able to achieve a conventionally significant result in the next 6 to 9 months, simply because the people we already have are very good. Achieving a field-altering result will be harder, riskier, and take longer. But we have a not unreasonable plan for that as well.
Bi-weekly updates
3
emails
From:
Ilya Sutskever <redacted>
Date:
Mon, Jun 12, 2017 at 10:39 PM
To:
Greg Brockman <redacted>, <redacted>, Elon Musk <redacted>
This is the first of our bi-weekly updates. The goal is to keep you up to date, and to help us make greater use from your visits.
Compute:
Compute is used in two ways: it is used to run a big experiment quickly, and it is used to run many experiments in parallel.
95% of progress comes from the ability to run big experiments quickly. The utility of running many experiments is much less useful.
In the old days, a large cluster could help you run more experiments, but it could not help with running a single large experiment quickly.
For this reason, an academic lab could compete with Google, because Google’s only advantage was the ability to run many experiments. This is not a great advantage.
Recently, it has become possible to combine 100s of GPUs and 100s of CPUs to run an experiment that’s 100x bigger than what is possible on a single machine while requiring comparable time. This has become possible due to the work of many different groups. As a result, the minimum necessary cluster for being competitive is now 10–100x larger than it was before.
Currently, every Dota experiment uses 1000+ cores, and it is only for the small 1v1 variant, and on extremely small neural network policies. We will need more compute to just win the 1v1 variant. To win the full 5v5 game, we will need to run fewer experiments, where each experiment is at least 1 order of magnitude larger (possibly more!).
TLDR:
What matters is the size and speed of our experiments. In the old days, a big cluster could not let anyone run a larger experiment quickly. Today, a big cluster lets us run a large experiment 100x faster.
In order to be capable of accomplishing our projects even in theory, we need to increase the number of our GPUs by a factor of 10x in the next 1–2 months (we have enough CPUs). We will discuss the specifics in our in-person meeting.
Dota 2:
We will solve the 1v1 version of the game in
1 month
. Fans of the game care about 1v1 a fair bit.
We are now at a point where a *single experiment* consumes 1000s of cores, and where adding more distributed compute increases performance.
Here is a cool video of our bot doing something rather clever:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Y-vxbREX5ck&feature=youtu.be&t=99.
Rapid learning of new games:
Infra work is underway
We implemented several baselines
Fundamentally, we’re not where we want to be, and are taking action to correct this.
Robotics:
Current status:
The HER algorithm (
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Dz_HuzgMzxo
) can learn to solve many low-dimensional robotics tasks that were previously unsolvable very rapidly. It is non-obvious, simple, and effective.
In 6 months, we will accomplish at least one of:
single-handed Rubik’s cube, pen spinning (
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dDavyRnEPrI
), Chinese balls spinning (
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=M9N1duIl4Fc
) using the HER algorithm and using a sim2real method [such as
https://blog.openai.com/spam-detection-in-the-physical-world/
].
The above will be deployed on the robotic hand:
[Link to Google Drive]
[this video is human controlled, not algorithmic controlled. Need to be logged in to the OpenAI account to see the video].
Self play as a key path to AGI:
Self play in multiagent environments is magical: if you place agents into an environment, then no matter how smart (or not smart) they are, the environment will provide them with the exact level of challenge, which can be faced only by outsmarting the competition. So for example, if you have a group of children, they will find each other’s company to be challenging; likewise for a collection of super intelligences of comparable intelligence. So the “solution” to self-play is to become more and more intelligent, without bound.
Self-play lets us get “something out of nothing.” The rules of a competitive game can be simple, but the best strategy for playing this game can be immensely complex. [motivating example:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=u2T77mQmJYI
].
Training agents in simulation to develop very good dexterity via competitive fighting, such as wrestling. Here is a video of ant-shaped robots that we trained to struggle:
<redacted>
Current work on self-play: getting agents to learn to develop a language [gifs in
https://blog.openai.com/learning-to-cooperate-compete-and-communicate/
]. Agents are doing “stuff,” but it’s still work in progress.
We have a few more cool smaller projects. Updates to be presented as they produce significant results.

Ok. Let’s figure out the least expensive way to ensure compute power is not a constraint…
The business of building AGI
From:
Ilya Sutskever <redacted>
Date:
Wed, Jul 12, 2017 at 1:36 PM
To:
Elon Musk <redacted>, Greg Brockman <redacted>
We usually decide that problems are hard because smart people have worked on them unsuccessfully for a long time. It’s easy to think that this is true about AI. However, the past five years of progress have shown that the earliest and simplest ideas about AI — neural networks — were right all along, and we needed modern hardware to get them working.
Historically, AI breakthroughs have consistently happened with models that take between 7–10 days to train. This means that hardware defines the surface of potential AI breakthroughs. This is a statement about human psychology more than about AI. If experiments take longer than this, it’s hard to keep all the state in your head and iterate and improve. If experiments are shorter, you’ll just use a bigger model.
It’s not so much that AI progress is a hardware game, any more than physics is a particle accelerator game. But if our computers are too slow, no amount of cleverness will result in AGI, just like if a particle accelerator is too small, we have no shot at figuring out how the universe works. Fast enough computers are a necessary ingredient, and all past failures may have been caused by computers being too slow for AGI.
Until very recently, there was no way to use many GPUs together to run faster experiments, so academia had the same “effective compute” as industry. But earlier this year, Google used two orders of magnitude more compute than is typical to optimize the architecture of a classifier, something that usually requires lots of researcher time. And a few months ago, Facebook released a paper showing how to train a large ImageNet model with near-linear speedup to 256 GPUs (given a specially-configured cluster with high-bandwidth interconnects).
Over the past year, Google Brain produced impressive results because they have an order of magnitude or two more GPUs than anyone. We estimate that Brain has around 100k GPUs, FAIR has around 15–20k, and DeepMind allocates 50 per researcher on question asking, and rented 5k GPUs from Brain for AlphaGo. Apparently, when people run neural networks at Google Brain, it eats up everyone’s quotas at DeepMind.
We're still missing several key ideas necessary for building AGI. How can we use a system's understanding of “thing A” to learn “thing B” (e.g. can I teach a system to count, then to multiply, then to solve word problems)? How do we build curious systems? How do we train a system to discover the deep underlying causes of all types of phenomena — to act as a scientist? How can we build a system that adapts to new situations on which it hasn’t been trained on precisely (e.g. being asked to apply familiar concepts in an unfamiliar situation)? But given enough hardware to run the relevant experiments in 7–10 days, history indicates that the right algorithms will be found, just like physicists would quickly figure out how the universe works if only they had a big enough particle accelerator.
There is good reason to believe that deep learning hardware will speed up 10x each year for the next four to five years. The world is used to the comparatively leisurely pace of Moore’s Law, and is not prepared for the drastic changes in capability this hardware acceleration will bring. This speedup will happen not because of smaller transistors or faster clock cycles; it will happen because like the brain, neural networks are intrinsically parallelizable, and new highly parallel hardware is being built to exploit this.
Within the next three years, robotics should be completely solved, AI should solve a long-standing unproven theorem, programming competitions should be won consistently by AIs, and there should be convincing chatbots (though no one should pass the Turing test). In as little as four years, each overnight experiment will feasibly use so much compute capacity that there’s an actual chance of waking up to AGI, given the right algorithm — and figuring out the algorithm will actually happen within 2–4 further years of experimenting with this compute in a competitive multiagent simulation.
To be in the business of building safe AGI, OpenAI needs to:
Have the best AI results each year. In particular, as hardware gets exponentially better, we’ll have dramatically better results. Our DOTA and Rubik’s cube projects will have impressive results for the current level of compute. Next year’s projects will be even more extreme, and what’s realistic depends primarily on what compute we can access.
Increase our GPU cluster from 600 GPUs to 5000 GPUs ASAP. As an upper bound, this will require a capex of $12M and an opex of $5–6M over the next year. Each year, we’ll need to exponentially increase our hardware spend, but we have reason to believe AGI can ultimately be built with less than $10B in hardware.
Increase our headcount: from 55 (July 2017) to 80 (January 2018) to 120 (January 2019) to 200 (January 2020). We’ve learned how to organize our current team, and we’re now bottlenecked by number of smart people trying out ideas.
Lock down an overwhelming hardware advantage. The 4-chip card that
<redacted>
says he can build in 2 years is effectively TPU 3.0 and (given enough quantity) would allow us to be on an almost equal footing with Google on compute. The Cerebras design is far ahead of both of these, and if they’re real then having exclusive access to them would put us far ahead of the competition. We have a structural idea for how to do this given more due diligence, best to discuss on a call.
2/3/4 will ultimately require large amounts of capital. If we can secure the funding, we have a real chance at setting the initial conditions under which AGI is born. Increased funding needs will come lockstep with increased magnitude of results. We should discuss options to obtain the relevant funding, as that’s the biggest piece that’s outside of our direct control.
Progress this week:
We’ve beat our top 1v1 test player (he’s top 30 in North America at 1v1, and beats the top 1v1 player about 30% of the time), but the bot can also be exploited by playing weirdly. We’re working on understanding these exploits and cracking down on them.
Repeated from Saturday, here’s the first match where we beat our top test player:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FBoUHay7XBI&feature=youtu.be&t=345
Every additional day of training makes the bot stronger and harder to exploit.
Robot getting closer to solving Rubik’s cube.
The improved cube simulation teleoperated by a human:
<redacted>
.
Our defense against adversarial examples is starting to work on ImageNet.
We will completely solve the problem of adversarial examples by the end of August.

On September 12, 2017, Elon laid out a board structure where he would “unequivocally have initial control of the company”.
Re: Current State
From:
Elon Musk <redacted>
Date:
Wed, Sep 13, 2017 at 12:40 AM
To:
Ilya Sutskever <redacted>
Cc:
Greg Brockman <redacted>
Sounds good. The three common stock seats (you, Greg and Sam) should be elected by common shareholders. They will de facto be yours, but not in the unlikely event that you lose the faith of a huge percentage of common stockholders over time or step away from the company by choice.
I think that the Preferred A investment round (supermajority me) should have the right to appoint four (not three) seats. I would not expect to appoint them immediately, but, like I said I would unequivocally have initial control of the company, but this will change quickly.
The rough target would be to get to a 12 person board (probably more like 16 if this board really ends up deciding the fate of the world) where each board member has a deep understanding of technology, at least a basic understanding of AI and strong & sensible morals.
Apart from the Series A four and the Common three, there would likely be a board member with each new lead investor/ally. However, the specific individual new board members can only be added if all but one existing board member agrees. Same for removing board members.
There will also be independent board members we want to add who aren’t associated with an investor. Same rules apply: requires all but one of existing directors to add or remove.
I’m super tired and don’t want to overcomplicate things, but this seems approx right. At the sixteen person board level, we would have 7/16 votes and I’d have a 25% influence, which is my min comfort level. That sounds about right to me. If everyone else we asked to join our board is truly against us, we should probably lose.
As mentioned, my experience with boards (assuming they consist of good, smart people) is that they are rational and reasonable. There is basically never a real hardcore battle where an individual board vote is pivotal, so this is almost certainly (sure hope so) going to be a moot point.
As a closing note, I’ve been really impressed with the quality of discussion with you guys on the equity and board stuff. I have a really good feeling about this.
Lmk if above seems reasonable.
Elon
Elon also told us he would need to be CEO.
September 2017: Elon created the public benefit corporation called “Open Artificial Intelligence Technologies, Inc.”
Elon directed Jared Birchall, his wealth manager, to create the public benefit corporation “Open Artificial Intelligence Technologies, Inc” (akin to SpaceX’s official name of “Space Exploration Technologies Corporation”). It was registered on on September 15, 2017:
The public benefit corporation created by Elon Musk on September 15, 2017, as the proposed future structure of OpenAI.
September 2017: We rejected Elon's terms because giving him unilateral control of OpenAI and its technology would be contrary to the mission.
We came close to accepting Elon’s terms. As we told him, “We really want to work with you. We believe that if we join forces, our chance of success in the mission is the greatest.”
However, we felt his proposal was not compatible with the mission, saying: “The current structure provides you with a path where you end up with unilateral absolute control over the AGI. You stated that you don't want to control the final AGI, but during this negotiation, you've shown to us that absolute control is extremely important to you.” As we told Elon, “The goal of OpenAI is to make the future good and to avoid an AGI dictatorship.”
We concluded, “Thus we are concerned that as the company makes genuine progress towards AGI, you will choose to retain your absolute control of the company despite current intent to the contrary.”
Honest Thoughts

emails
From:
Ilya Sutskever <redacted>
Date:
Wed, Sep 20, 2017 at 2:08 PM
To:
Elon Musk <redacted>, Sam Altman <redacted>, Greg Brockman <redacted>
Elon, Sam,
This process has been the highest stakes conversation that Greg and I have ever participated in, and if the project succeeds, it’ll turn out to have been the highest stakes conversation the world has seen. It’s also been a deeply personal conversation for all of us.
Yesterday while we were considering making our final commitment (even the non-solicit agreement), we realized we’d made a mistake. We have several important concerns that we haven’t raised with either of you. We didn’t raise them because we were afraid to: we were afraid of harming the relationship, having you think less of us, or losing you as partners.
There is some chance that our concerns will prove to be unresolvable. We really hope it’s not the case, but we know we will fail for sure if we don’t all discuss them now. And we have hope that we can work through them all and continue working together.
Elon
We
really
want to work with you. We believe that if we join forces, our chance of success in the mission is the greatest. Our upside is the highest. There is no doubt about that. Our desire to work with you is so great that we are happy to give up on the equity, personal control, make ourselves easily firable — whatever it takes to work with you.
But we realized that we were careless in our thinking about the implications of control for the world. Because it seemed so hubristic, we have not been seriously considering the implications of success.
The current structure provides you with a path where you end up with unilateral absolute control over the AGI. You stated that you don’t want to control the final AGI, but during this negotiation, you’ve shown to us that absolute control is extremely important to you.
As an example, you said that you needed to be CEO of the new company so that everyone will know that you are the one who is in charge, even though you also stated that you hate being CEO and would much rather not be CEO.
Thus, we are concerned that as the company makes genuine progress towards AGI, you will choose to retain your absolute control of the company despite current intent to the contrary. We disagree with your statement that our ability to leave is our greatest power, because once the company is actually on track to AGI, the company will be much more important than any individual.
The goal of OpenAI is to make the future good and to avoid an AGI dictatorship. You are concerned that Demis could create an AGI dictatorship. So do we. So it is a bad idea to create a structure where you could become a dictator if you chose to, especially given that we can create some other structure that avoids this possibility.
We have a few smaller concerns, but we think it’s useful to mention it here:
In the event we decide to buy Cerebras, my strong sense is that it’ll be done through Tesla. But why do it this way if we could also do it from within OpenAI? Specifically, the concern is that Tesla has a duty to shareholders to maximize shareholder return, which is not aligned with OpenAI’s mission. So the overall result may not end up being optimal for OpenAI.
We believe that OpenAI the non-profit was successful because both you and Sam were in it. Sam acted as a genuine counterbalance to you, which has been extremely fruitful. Greg and I, at least so far, are much worse at being a counterbalance to you. We feel this is evidenced even by this negotiation, where we were ready to sweep the long-term AGI control questions under the rug while Sam stood his ground.
Sam:
When Greg and I are stuck, you’ve always had an answer that turned out to be deep and correct. You’ve been thinking about the ways forward on this problem extremely deeply and thoroughly. Greg and I understand technical execution, but we don’t know how structure decisions will play out over the next month, year, or five years.
But we haven’t been able to fully trust your judgments throughout this process, because we don’t understand your cost function.
We don’t understand why the CEO title is so important to you. Your stated reasons have changed, and it’s hard to really understand what’s driving it.
Is AGI truly your primary motivation? How does it connect to your political goals? How has your thought process changed over time?
Greg and Ilya:
We had a fair share of our own failings during this negotiation, and we’ll list some of them here (Elon and Sam, I’m sure you’ll have plenty to add…):
During this negotiation, we realized that we have allowed the idea of financial return 2–3 years down the line to drive our decisions. This is why we didn’t push on the control — we thought that our equity is good enough, so why worry? But this attitude is wrong, just like the attitude of AI experts who don’t think that AI safety is an issue because they don’t really believe that they’ll build AGI. We did not speak our full truth during the negotiation. We have our excuses, but it was damaging to the process, and we may lose both Sam and Elon as a result.
There’s enough baggage here that we think it’s very important for us to meet and talk it out. Our collaboration will not succeed if we don’t. Can all four of us meet today? If all of us say the truth, and resolve the issues, the company that we’ll create will be much more likely to withstand the very strong forces it’ll experience.
Greg & Ilya
